On an individual basis. But still, you look at something, you think, okay, you've got a player scoring 85 goals in 100. The fact that you've then got two trophyless seasons, and someone is performing at that level…

So the model is interesting because it's very different, but it's built in hubris. It's built in the association with them being the best club, and those players just going, "of course, we want to come and play with you, Real Madrid. You're Real Madrid." Even though, if you look at the clubs those players are leaving, it's Liverpool, it's Bayern Munich, it's PSG, it's Chelsea.

However, on the broader financial part of the club, while it looks like a genius play — and it is to some extent — it's almost a financial necessity if they're going to compete at the top level, because they do not have the resources to go and spend on transfer fees like some of the top clubs can do, most notably Premier League clubs.

十、皇马的财务真相：世界第一大俱乐部

Yeah. So, is it worth just unwrapping and unpacking part of the story of Real Madrid's finances?

I think so.

Because effectively this is the biggest club in the world. And why do I say that? It's not because I'm a Madrid fan. It's because that's what the numbers tell you. Their revenues — and I'm going to try and speak where possible in pounds, right, otherwise you get a slightly strange euros versus pounds — their revenues are approximately a billion pounds a year. Okay.

The biggest club in England, which is Manchester United, if they were in the Champions League, would probably be at about £850 million a year. So Real are £150 million a year ahead of where Man United are right now, on an apples-and-apples type comparison. So significantly larger.

Now, they've achieved that increase in revenues because, until not that long ago, they were much more at parity with the big English Premier League clubs, by massively redeveloping the Bernabéu.

And I think you and I would say overall that we are generally speaking in favor of clubs making significant stadium investment to increase their underlying revenues, to enable them in the medium-to-long-term to be able to have the revenues under the new cost-control mechanisms to allow them to pay higher wages, and ultimately to produce the cash, once the mortgage has been paid off (and we'll come back on to that in a moment), to then be able to pay the big transfer fees as well.

But it's good business. Generally speaking, increasing the matchday revenues through upsizing and upscaling and "speccing up" your stadium is a good thing to do.

So, they've done a good thing there.

十一、债务的代价：10 亿英镑的影子

They've done a good thing. The challenge is that because they are fan-owned as opposed to owner-owned, the stadium is built with debt. And I don't mean was built with equity and some debt, as was the case with Arsenal and Spurs. I mean it's been built with debt, right? Simply built with debt.

So the actual debt of Real Madrid is over £1 billion pounds, and now the interest payments and the repayment schedule on that stadium is £100 million a year, more or less. Okay, just keep the numbers round. That £100 million a year is really, really significant.

And I think if you go back and say "what was it like at Arsenal in the years after the Emirates was built" — those are tough years, because effectively you've made this big investment which is going to benefit you over 30 to 40 years, but you've had to make a big upfront investment in it, and you're paying the cost of it quite front-loadedly. Right? So it might be over 5 years, it might be over 10 years, it might be over 15 years, but it's not over 30 or 40 years.

So these interest payments and the repayment schedule which has now kicked in — 'cause they don't have a sugar daddy to say "okay, well look, I'll cover three or 400 million of it and then the rest of it will be in debt" — because the whole thing is pretty much in debt, the debt repayments and the interest payments are absolutely colossal.

So the compensation that most clubs make — whether it be Arsenal in the dog days of the Arsène Wenger era, or Spurs in the last five or six years — the compensation that most clubs make is to say, "Okay, look, we're going to have to spend a bit less on players, right? We have to be honest with our fan base. We've got mortgage payments. Sorry, that's just where we are." And fans don't like it, and they whine about it, and they'll shout a bit at the — "oh, why aren't we able to compete" and all this type of stuff — but fundamentally the majority of the fans understand it: "we've just gone and made this massive investment".

Okay. The problem here is that Pérez's own personal strategy, the thing which he's based his entire presidency over, is: "while I am here we will sign the Galácticos, we will sign the best players in the world." But when you're paying £100 million pounds a year in interest payments and debt repayment, you basically cannot —

by normal measures —

By normal measures. So then you have to start looking at, okay, what are the other ways that we can try and engender to sort of get these top players to come to us? And the answer is, well, we don't buy them when we want to buy them. What we do is we sort of have a quiet word with them through back channels saying "you will come and play for us in two years' time, once you run your contract down."

And that's all kind of fine in theory, but for whatever reason, it's just not working from a performance perspective —

十二、特权文化的代际传递

because what you're immediately putting in place there is a culture of entitlement.

Yeah.

Right. So if we're looking at the list that we just explained of what's happened in the last week, right, that it's basically the result of players with an aura of entitlement.

You just need to look at what's happened this season. Xabi Alonso, you know, the most coveted manager coming out of Bayer Leverkusen, back to Real Madrid with his squad that, you know, you would back him to go and succeed. Massive —

and a top player, right? He's not one of these managers who you can say "show us your medals" to.

No. He has — Alonso has — and also as a coach. Yeah. Right. In credibility — like, he took Bayer Leverkusen to an unbeaten season. They lost one game at Europa League final.

But what happened when he came in? Hey, it was a division down the middle. It was supposedly Camp Vinnie Jr. and Camp Mbappé. Player power once again winning out.

Now you then look at [the new manager] who's come in — reportedly they call him "the cone". The manager of Real Madrid is being called "the cone", i.e. the training cone. Very little authority on the training pitch.

So if I'm being recruited by a club that is encouraging me to run down a contract, and giving me this air of authority that they have, but also giving me a huge amount of power in that decision-making process, I'm going to bring that into —

my experience at a club.

I think that's totally true. I think that's some really smart point.

十三、Pérez 的"不可触碰性"与 Mourinho 的回归

If we're looking at the problems they have now, it's the outcome of that whole mentality that Pérez has built in from the top. And it even goes to him almost being untouchable. I mean, in his press conference this week, he pretty much said, "I think you'd have to shoot me to get me out of here."

But this is supposedly a democracy. So you don't shoot someone to get them out of there. You vote them out.

The culture that exists in this club — the great football club — we're talking about an incredible revenue-generating business that is not relying on an independent owner to fund it. Yet this whole situation, and having to look to someone like Jose Mourinho to come in and almost — it's like a school teacher banging everyone together and going "get back to what is important and remind them who the real boss is in a football club environment."

It's an interesting choice, because effectively, as you say, from a coaching perspective, Mourinho has been on a downward slide for five, six, seven years now. So no one believes that Jose is the guy who's got the new coaching ideas that perhaps 25 years ago he did, right? He had various different new ideas and theories 25 years ago. He's now well on that downward slide compared to other younger coaches who've come in with new ideas.

But what's the one thing you do know about him? In any club that he's in charge of, who's the number one? He's the boss.

He's the boss, right? I mean, without any question. And it's a question of his own history both at Royale and other massive clubs. But it's also a function of his personality, because you need to have that sharp tongue. You need to have that quick put-down. You need to have that self-confidence to be able to look people in the eye, no matter how rich they are, no matter how successful they are, and say, "Yeah, right. Really? You're trying that with me?" And of course, Jose is one of the few managers who can pull that particular trick off.

But from a coaching perspective, you're going back in time. So if I was one of Real's big rivals, I'd be looking at that going, "this is great." Right? You know, Jose's been well off the pace for some time. Yeah, he might stop them all fighting each other, but is he going to be the guy who's going to get Real playing like PSG? I don't think so. You know, that seems highly unlikely to me.

十四、症状背后的系统性疾病

So I think you're right in saying that this is symptomatic of a whole bunch of baggage that's hanging around in the background, that gradually over time has changed the nature of the club, and has made it into something which any person who works in super-elite sport would say "no, that's not how you do it. That's not how you do it."

It starts with teamwork. It starts with a work ethic. It starts with clarity about team objectives, not individual objectives. It starts with an understanding of who your competition is, and respecting that competition, and being determined to beat that competition.

All of those things under Pérez are out the window. "We're the big guys. We don't have to worry about the competition. We bring the biggest stars in here, and because we're Royale, we can afford to have all of them in one place, and we don't have to worry about it, because it's Royale and they'll all understand that Real's that big."

And it's all exploded. It's all exploded in quite a sort of tawdry way, I'd say. Which is again why you were kind of questioning me on the validity of this topic to actually talk about on this show — because it's gossip.

It felt like a lot of gossip. But I do think, you know, when you pull a lot of that back, what sits underneath it is actually the whole operational side of this club and how it sits aside from so many others with entitlement at the heart of it.

And while entitlement can be leveraged in a great way, and has in the past created the most incredible teams for Real Madrid, this is now seeing a slightly separate side of it: where if it doesn't quite work, and you go two trophyless seasons, and you get a bit of disgruntlement in the group, and you don't have a manager leading you who —

十五、收入开始下滑：财务的回响

which by the way is starting to have an impact on revenue. So I just pulled the numbers there because they are fan-owned. They update their socios in much more real time than normal football clubs do, when you get a set of accounts that relates to something that happened 15 months ago. At Royale, you actually get given much more up-to-date numbers.

So, you know, in the first half of season 25/26, the revenue was down by 3.1% in actual terms. So, including inflation, quite a bit more than that, right? And that's starting to drive a negative net banking position, where you start to look at the debt interest payments and you say, "are they going to have to sell some players here? I think they might have to sell some players." You know, they're going to have to start moving into a net transfer-positive trading situation to be able to meet their debt repayment, because ultimately the bottom line is —

they are a business, and they don't have the release valve that your club had back in the days where you can simply say "sorry, boss, things aren't going so well. Can you inject 50 to 100 million here, because we're struggling a little bit?"

Call it equity.

Yeah.

十六、回到根本：足球终究是团队运动

So it's a fascinating situation. It's a fascinating study in the reality of elite sport, and what it really means — which is ultimately it is actually about the core principles of sport. And however much you can create a great stadium with great matchday revenues, however much you can say "well, we're going to be this, we're going to be that, we're going to be Galácticos" and all this type of stuff — in the end, it's a team sport, and players have to run around, and players have to fight for each other, and players have to want to believe what the coach says. All this type of stuff in football really, really, really matters.

And I stretch this out a little bit further thinking about the Galáctico teams that I grew up watching, early 2000s, which is really what I would call it. So Zinedine Zidane, Ronaldo, Raúl —

Yeah.

great players — Casillas — they were brilliant individual brands, but it was a team.

Well, encapsulating Raúl, right? So you still have to have players who are heart and soul in the club. Which again — keep going back to your club at Chelsea — for all the big-money signings:

John Terry, Frank Lampard, Drogba, Čech — these were club guys who were there for long periods of time, who really believed in the whole project and believed in each other.

And —

it doesn't exist there right now.

It just doesn't look like that. Real Madrid, not — and —

well, actually in Chelsea as well. Well, and obviously a big fundamental flaw. Won't go down there.

But if you look at this with Real Madrid, it was the evolution of that kind of "we're still a team" to this — now, just from the outside, absolute fragmentation of your core players, not being able to sit in a dressing room together and operate as a professional entity. I mean, yeah, Valverde and Tchouaméni summed it up. But Valverde is your captain —

十七、更衣室冲突：一次性事件 vs 系统性症状

on that particular point. Right. Players getting competitive with each other, players not liking each other, players occasionally letting things boil over — this in itself is not necessarily a big problem. Okay, it does happen sometimes. One of my biggest fears when I'm running a football club is if the training ground's too quiet and everyone seems okay and everyone's just going through the motions. You want training to have a bit of bite about it.

You want training to be a bit like this. The problem here is, it's seen as part of a greater whole. It's not just a one-off incident, which is okay, these players got a bit of a problem with each other. It looks like a symptom of a greater malaise.

Yeah, because Johnny, that doesn't happen when they're winning five Champions [Leagues] in a row.

So — I would — it may, I tell you, it may do, and it happens more often than you might think. Perhaps not quite to that level where a player gets hospitalized. It happens more often than you might think. Fans overestimate the extent to which a lot of footballers playing the same club like each other. Quite often they don't like each other very much, and quite often they feel very competitive about who's trying to take their place, or who's done this for me in that game, or who didn't cover for me in that game. It can get quite recriminative.

So I'm not going to sit here saying that players falling out or players having a bit of a scrap is a big problem. The issue here is, it's part of three, four, five, six, seven separate pieces of news. It's part of a broader pattern of disunity.

十八、结论：仍是世界最大俱乐部，但需要新方向

But anyway, look, it is still the biggest club in the world, right? And what they've done with the Bernabéu is amazing. You just look at the situation, you think it needs new leadership. It needs a clarity of strategic direction which is able to take this amazing, huge, monstrous football club onto the next level from where it is right now — which frankly is, you know, nowhere near where it should be.

If that's the solution, I'm just going to give you a couple of points from his press conference. So, Pérez gave a press conference this week after the loss to Barcelona, after all of this happened. He cited his 74 trophies as evidence that he's the greatest president in the club's history, making it abundantly clear that he'd not be leaving his post voluntarily. He brought up the Barcelona and refereeing scandal, which, you know, is brilliant deflection as always, as you'd expect from him. But he also described himself as a "supernatural animal" and a "beast", before then arguing with reporters, threatening to restrict media access to the Real Madrid training ground if they disagreed.

So while that may be the case, this doesn't look like a guy that has any intention of moving forward, despite his 79 years. And Jose going there guarantees that the circus is going to continue, right? It's just that now Jose is going to be at the front of it.

So we've had this situation where you've had players at the front of it. You've had Pérez at the front of it. From now on, the main character — I'm gutted — is going to be Jose.

I'm gutted. I thought he could come home.

I thought he could come back. A Premier League, which Jose's a better —

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：皇马是"成功的囚徒"——胜利本身在制造他们的衰败
表面上人们会说：皇马的问题是球员太差、教练太弱、Perez 太老。但真相恰恰相反——**皇马的问题是"赢得太多"**。
注意一个被所有人忽略的悖论：当 Vinicius 没拿到金球奖时，俱乐部集体抵制颁奖典礼。这不是"输不起"，这是一种更深层的病态——**他们已经把"应得"内化为身份本身**。Rodri 拿了英超冠军 + 欧洲杯冠军，按任何客观标准都是合理赢家，但皇马的反应不是"我们下次再争"，而是"宇宙错了"。
这就形成了一个我称之为 **"熵增式成功陷阱"** 的结构：每一次胜利不是加固系统，而是侵蚀系统的反馈机制。因为当你 5 年赢 5 个欧冠，你失去了从"失败信号"中学习的能力——而组织学习理论告诉我们，**没有失败信号的系统最终会因为信号失灵而崩溃**，就像不会感到痛的人会被烫伤却不知道。
Mbappé 度假事件之所以是引爆点而非孤立事件，是因为它揭示了一个更可怕的事实：**这家俱乐部已经训练出了一种"个人主义最大化"的玩家文化**——而这恰恰是他们自己 20 年合同战略的副产品（详见第三点）

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Trump-Xi Summit, Benioff: "Not My First SaaSpocalypse," OpenAI vs Apple, Multi-Sensory AI, El Niño
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：Sasspocalypse 是一场"语义错位"，不是技术替代

最让我意外的不是 Salesforce 跌了 37%，而是 Benioff 在节目里轻描淡写地承认："今年我会在 Anthropic 上花 3 亿美元。" 这是一个被忽略的信号——SaaS 巨头不是被 AI 杀死的受害者，而是 AI 算力的最大付费客户之一。市场的恐慌定价（HubSpot 跌到 2x sales）其实是一种"语义错位"：投资者把"AI 会替代 SaaS UI"的故事和"SaaS 的现金流引擎"混为一谈。但 SaaS 公司握着的是语义层 + 数据所有权 + CRO 关系，这恰恰是 LLM 概率性输出必须"接地"的地方。Chamath 那句"下一只鞋掉下来时，OpenAI 会求 Mark 帮他们卖 token"——这是整集最锋利的预测，因为它把 AI 价值链翻了个底朝天：模型公司是上游产能过剩的大宗商品供应商，SaaS 是稀缺的渠道。

跨领域连接：Thucydides 陷阱 × El Niño × 算力扩散，其实是同一个"非零和"问题

仔细听这集，三个看似无关的话题——中美 G2、ENSO 气候震荡、是否卖最尖端芯片给中国——其实在同一个抽象层上被讨论：人类在面对"可扩张总量"和"零和分配"两种心智模型时的选择。Friedberg 的"做大蛋糕论"、Benioff 的"卖芯片论"、甚至他对 El Niño 储能 1100 万 TWh 的描述（"海洋是天气的电池"），都指向一个反主流的结构：能量过剩时代的政治不应该是地缘的，而应该是热力学的。当海洋多出 500 年人类用电量储能时，争夺台积电的 2nm 工艺像是历史的反讽——人类在为微观结构上的几个原子打仗，而宏观系统正把灾难免费分发。这里有个出人意料的暗线：Benioff 谈 1% 慈善与他谈卖芯片给中国，是同一套世界观——通过"主动外溢"换长期稳定性，而不是通过"封锁稀缺"维持优势。这种"反稀缺"逻辑同时穿过了气候、商业、地缘三层。

隐藏的系统性真相：被低估的"形态因子断点"

Chamath 反驳本地模型时说："你不会扛着 5 磅 MacBook Pro 到处走"——但他真正击中的，是更深的系统断点：算力的位置（云/边）决定了未来 AI 公司的护城河形态，而形态因子会决定政治结构。Mira Murati 那个每 200ms 上传桌面+摄像头+音频到两个模型的 demo，其实是在悄悄宣告"turn-based AI 时代"结束。如果一个白领每天产生 1000x 现在的 token 用量，AI 不再是工具，而是基础设施级别的能源消耗——这立刻反过来解释了为什么 Trump 带 CEO 去中国、为什么台湾问题在 18 个月内"过时"、为什么 Apple 突然要在 AirPods 里塞摄像头。

一个真正出人意料的洞见：Susan Wojcicki 那段眼泪可能是这集最被低估的"系统信号"。Benioff 在谈完 SaaS 末日、地缘博弈、El Niño 之后情绪失控——不是因为软弱，而是因为节目里所有抽象议题（蛋糕、token、产能）的尽头，都是有限的生命时间。那个未被言说的对话是：所有这些抽象优化游戏的真正约束条件，从来不是芯片或 GDP，而是 mortality。 这是企业家在 60 岁附近开始重写自己人生算法的隐性触发器，也是为什么他突然热衷"1% Pledge"、为什么他说"Tony Robbins 救了我"——他在公开节目里完成了一次没人识别的世界观回归。

📝 转录文本

开场：节目介绍与嘉宾

All right, everybody, welcome back to the number one podcast in the world, the All In Podcast, episode 273. It's a huge week. SACS is out. So we got to get Mark Benioff, CEO of Salesforce. I'm the only one left here.

— You're the only one left. That's how I made it on. — No software CEOs, right? To China? There's no software CEOs in China. Interesting. — But did you get the invite? What's your status with this administration? Because you were obviously very famous for, you know, being a Democrat on the left. — I'm not a Democrat on the left. I never have been. Jason, what are you talking about? — You donated to all these folks and now you're in Trump's camp? What is it? — Listen, the number one thing is, hey, I'm here to support the country. That's what I do. — Okay. So you're right in the middle. You have an allegiance to America. — I hope so. I'm not a Democrat or Republican. — You're an independent like me. — I am an American. Okay. I like it.

Benioff got the invite to the two hottest tickets. He got the invite to Windsor Castle in Tails. And then he got the invite when Prince Charles came here, too. I saw he was in Tails then, too. And also, we were at that Saudi dinner, too. Weren't you there, Chamath?

— I did not go to that one.

议题一：Trump-Xi 峰会

All right. Well, we have a big docket here. The Trump-Xi summit has begun. That is the number one story right now after a two-month delay because of the war in Iran. This is the first visit to China since 2017. Seventh face-to-face meeting for Trump and President Xi. There are 12 hours, 15 hours ahead of us. Today, Thursday, was officially day one.

峰会要点

Here's what happened:

  • China agreed that the Strait of Hormuz should remain open with no military commitment and that Iran should not have a nuclear weapon. So we're in sync on that.
  • On Taiwan, this is a little spicy: Xi warned that "if handled poorly, the two countries will collide or even clash, putting the entire U.S.-China relationship in an extremely dangerous situation."
  • Polymarket says Taiwan is safe for 2026. Only 6% chance China invades this year on 23 million in volume — a lot of volume. 17% chance, roughly triple, that something happens by the end of 2027. Some people say it will happen after Trump is out of office; some think it's going to happen while he's in office.
  • On trade: Xi committed to buy more soybeans, US oil, LNG, and 200 Boeing jets. She said the talks laid out a vision for "constructive, strategic, and stable ties."
  • Trump was effusive in his praise for his friendship, giving the disclaimer that people don't like it when he praises Xi.

Friedberg：Thucydides 陷阱与"做大蛋糕"

Let's stop here and have a bit of a discussion. Friedberg, you have been a bit obsessed with this relationship and the bipolar nature of it. What are your thoughts here and what is the goal for Trump? What is winning for Trump coming out of this? What is winning for Xi?

I mean, Xi made comments in his opening remarks that it would be ideal if the United States and China could avoid the Thucydides trap, which as you'll recall, we talked about with Graham Allison and we've talked about several times over the last few years — that as a rising power meets a kind of declining power, there's always some moment where you end up in this kind of state of conflict. And the question is, can the US and China avoid conflict and find a path to cooperation, which has happened a few times in history when we found ourselves in this moment, but doesn't often happen.

And I think the way to kind of think about the opportunity is: if you're in a resource-expansive world, where you are increasing productivity and increasing production globally at an accelerating pace, perhaps there's less of a reason to have conflict. Perhaps there's a way that both societies, both countries can increase the quality of life for their people without taking it away from the other side. And in a world where you're more resource-constrained or resource-static, that becomes less possible. You have to fight and grab land and grab territory and grab resources from the other side.

And it seems like in this moment when we are seeing these extraordinary technology shifts unlocked by AI and automation and biotech and all of these kind of moments of what could be true abundance ahead of us, it seems like the perfect moment to say, hey, maybe the world can be more multipolar, doesn't need to be unipolar, doesn't need to be bipolar — but everyone can participate in the expansion of the pie.

From an administration political perspective, it just seems like this is going to be a major coup for the president. If he can walk away with a series of trade deals with China that increase job security, increase prosperity, increase income levels, increase investment in America and American jobs, it would be a huge win. And the big strategic question, which I care most about, is avoiding conflict: is there a way we can chart an economic and cooperative path that doesn't involve eating each other and involves sharing in a bigger pie?

Chamath：经济捆绑与"划分蛋糕"

Chamath, why is Trump bringing all of these CEOs with him? And what does success look like coming out of this meeting?

I think that you find a resolution and a path forward with China through economic cooperation. It's the largest consumer economy in the world. It's still largely closed off. And so I think what this is was about bringing some amount of financial firepower with them so that they could start to penetrate that market — planes, cars, chips, very much hard equipment type stuff.

I've said this before, but I think the Chinese are very much a top-down Confucian societal philosophy, whereas Americans are much more bottoms up, kind of an individualist construction. The fact that we're so different actually gives us a lot of room to find cooperation and not find conflict. And I think at the center of that is economic cooperation.

I had a friend call me this morning — very prominent Democrat. And he said, "you know, the media gets it all wrong, because they're talking about why is he bringing these CEOs? And instead, the reality is that the simplest and surest path to a no-conflict détente with China is economic entanglement. And it has to be bi-directional, because really, for so many years, it's been one way where they're sending us the cheap goods that they wanted."

So I think strategically, it's good. I'll just take a slightly different take on what Friedberg said. I think behind closed doors, they're probably just figuring out how to divide the pie.

— Unpack that for a second. Divide which pie? The globe, the economy, the Western Hemisphere versus the Pacific?

China still needs a lot of energy. They also need a lot of critical technologies that America provides. And so I think that there is a trade there. And the quid pro quo is there are certain regions of the world where we want them to deescalate their participation in. Central / South America being probably the most important; the second is probably in the Middle East; and then maybe the third is just to find some reasonable view of the Asia-Pac region together. In that, you kind of start to carve up the world into a pretty easily identifiable bipolar construction.

Benioff：销售员、Alibaba 与 Elon 的中国特例

— They also, Mark, need customers to buy their goods. We've had a bit of mixed signals there. Tariffs, the decoupling after COVID — "Hey, we need to be independent on energy, PPE, drugs, chips from China." Now we're kind of saying, "hey, let's come to the table and build connective tissue." So sort that out for us on a business level.

Well, you heard it today from President Xi when he said he wants a wider door. And that means that he wants the door of business to open even wider between the two countries. We have our absolute best salespeople there — not just our president, who has to be one of the best salespeople I've ever met, but also Elon, Jensen selling chips, Kelly Ortberg selling planes (he's already sold 200, he wants to sell 500), financial services, Brian Sykes the CEO of Cargill — the world's largest private company, based in Minneapolis — coming away selling a lot of soybeans.

Each one is our best salesperson in this category, and they're going to come back with orders. By the way, this isn't Trump's and Xi's first meeting. These guys really like each other. They are very connected. You can see them smiling with each other. They have a sales orientation. So I expect a lot of order books to come back.

— I have a question for you. Are you allowed to sell software in China?

Well, the Chinese have a lot of data residency laws. We do it as — we don't have any offices or employees in China. We never have. The only time we end up with any residue in China is when we buy a company that has a presence over there, and then we have to divest from it. We have an exclusive partnership with Alibaba. Everything goes through them.

Key customers of Salesforce like Louis Vuitton, or Loro Piana — I know Chamath loves Loro Piana — Loro Piana sells a lot of sweaters in China. In all the other stores all over the world, they use Salesforce. But in China, it's Salesforce on Alibaba. It's still your code, but then it resides in their servers for data residency. All the data has to be kept in China, all the data has to be accessible by the CCP.

That's why it's so extraordinary, by the way, what Elon has. Elon has Tesla in China — no partnership. He's the only one. He has a phenomenal relationship with Xi. When you look at Xi and Elon together, notice how deferential and respectful they are of each other. And here is these AI cars with all these cameras, American-made, you know, Chinese factories driving around China. That is pretty incredible.

— How do you think he pulled that off? — I think Elon's the greatest salesman in the world. That's how.

The cynical take would be: they wanted him there and they wanted to study the company and the innovation. And they have now become the biggest competitor with their EVs.

— I want one of those new BYD gullwing cars. They're talking about bringing some of those cars to the US. If we had $20,000, $30,000 cars from China, it would be the end of the US automotive industry overnight.

中期选举、Andreessen Horowitz、世界划分

— How does this play in America vis-à-vis the midterms, Friedberg? Inflation over 3%, Iran war seen as a betrayal by some MAGA, China not material to their well-being. Trump is on a six-month timeline; Xi works off a 100-year playbook.

I think the second- and third-order effects of anything that comes out of China are what's going to be most consequential to the average voter — job security, income security, income growth, and cost of living impact. If there can be some deflationary effect from a trade deal that makes access to goods lower price for Americans; if Americans are exporting more (soybeans, oil, technology and software), that increases economic productivity in the US and increases job security and income.

I think that could be positive. I think that's what's ultimately going to make people make the blue-red decision when they go to the polls in November.

— Chamath, how do you handicap midterms?

I think it's a little bit above my pay grade. But the midterms are probably going to swing more based on these recent gerrymandering rulings from the Supreme Court and what happened in the Virginia Supreme Court and what's going to happen in the state legislatures of these red and blue states, more than what's going to come out of this summit.

Money is getting organized. There was an article in The New York Times this week — the largest donor in this election cycle is Andreessen Horowitz. I think it's because they're trying to establish themselves as part of the financial firmament of America, which strategically as a business makes a lot of sense. They're in the AUM business. They can't stop at 100 billion of AUM. They're marching towards a trillion. They're going to be the next Blackstone. But politics is now a permanent part of that if they're going to be investing in higher quantum, more geopolitically influenced parts of the capital cycle.

Again, I think the Chinese and Americans have a very strong incentive to kind of divide up the world. China has a stranglehold on critical resource inputs. America has a stranglehold on energy and intelligence (AI). There's a trade to be done. Does China really need to be in Chile and Venezuela and Panama? Probably not. Does America have to have an incredibly strong point of view about the Strait of Malacca? Probably not.

中国的脆弱：人口、地产、GDP

— The key issue here is China is — most people are China-phobic — but China has serious systemic issues with population, with their real estate, with their GDP. All of this is in decline.

By the way, that is the number one point of focus of President Xi. Xi's focus has been getting 500 million people from poverty into the middle class. It's not exactly how we think about the world in the United States. That has been an incredible accomplishment. But it's at risk right now because their GDP is falling. They need their factories to operate. They're being undercut by other regions.

— And then Taiwan is what they want most of all, and supremacy in their part of the world.

I'm not convinced of that, actually. What they want — to summarize — they want economic success, they want to continue to grow their middle class, they want a healthy economy, they want more trade. It was interesting that Trump is talking about not only having the board of peace, now he wants a board of trade. That was a discussion we've never heard before today. Then we have Xi saying "I want the wider door." Two people we didn't mention: the CEO of Visa and the CEO of MasterCard are on the trip. Commerce in China is dominated by Alibaba and these super apps — it's not really all the way open for Visa and MasterCard. That would be incredible for those executives.

The more economic collaboration and cooperation between the two countries, the more peace you're going to have.

三个硬问题：芯片、台湾、武器

— Three hard questions, Mark. Should we be selling the latest chips, yes or no?

I think it's irrelevant at this point. The Chinese models are as competitive as the U.S. models. They've learned to make these models without having the highest end chips. The highest end chips is kind of more of an ego gratification for us. We have Blackwell, we have this, we have that. But their models are excellent. They fast-follow us. We probably should just sell whatever we can at this point. The more economic cooperation and collaboration is better. Taiwan, it's kind of a nonsense story.

— Friedberg, your take?

I think it's inevitable that they get there. Maybe Taiwan becomes less relevant to the US and to China as both China and the US mainland fab. The TSMC facility in Arizona is running. They're printing. China's mainlanding with Huawei standing up a lot of facilities — multi-deca-billion-dollar government investment in ensuring competitiveness, not just with fabs, but also semiconductor manufacturing equipment so that they don't get cut off (ASML blocking sales into mainland).

Does Taiwan really matter? Is there really that much of a security risk? Perhaps what everyone's focused on — this pivot point around Taiwan — maybe it's not that the US is selling Taiwan down the river, but that no one gives a sh anymore**. By 2040, China wants to bring Taiwan fully into the sphere. Maybe the US shouldn't care that much at that point.

The more the global productivity index can climb, the better off all humans will be. If you let technology diffuse and proliferate, everyone benefits and conflict indices go down. You give everyone a higher standard of living, you give everyone less reason to fight.

— Should we take arm sales off the table, and ask China to not sell arms to Iran? — Yes. What Xi wants is for us to not sell arms to Taiwan. So would you make that trade? Generally, that's probably a trade that should be kind of discussed. Do the deal.

— Chamath?

We're 18 months from Taiwan not being an important moment of conversation the way it is today. We are probably 1 to 2 nanometers away from being able to do what we need Taiwan to strategically do for us. There are orthogonal technologies being developed — Neuralink was showcasing a machine that is literally operating at the almost nanometer scale to do brain operations for implantation, all automatically. When you have that dexterity and capability mechanically, the real reason for Taiwan becomes very different. Today, it's economic. Take that off the table, and we'll have a very different attitude.

Number two, sell the chips. We want Nvidia to win. We do not want to give enough oxygen for Huawei to emerge with a version of a chip that works. These models are catching up. The more important thing is: let's have a reasonable KYC for how the models get used so that somebody in a basement doesn't cook up some bioweapon. Meanwhile, sell the chips, proliferate, let Jensen win.

— Mark, last word. China sets up a blockade around Taiwan and decides they're taking it. Should the U.S. defend it?

I've said this for years. I don't agree with Neil Ferguson on that point. I think this is a nonsense issue. I think China and Taiwan will reconcile.

插曲：Neuralink、Sphere、Tony Robbins

— I do want to schedule, Chamath, for that Neuralink. Next Tuesday at five o'clock. We can drill your brain. Are you trying to put some empathy into Chamath?

I talked to Elon. He said it doesn't give empathy. You just drill right in. There's a [sound] and boom. We want a more connected Chamath. It's going to make for an even better show. "If I only had a heart." "If I only had a brain." "If I only had a Neuralink."

By the way, I took Chamath's kids to the Wizard of Oz at the Sphere. It was incredible.

— No, I'll be honest with you. I get a little freaked out in large groups of people. I get very nervous. I just have these bad thoughts of "oh my God, what if something happens? How do we get out?" Like a fire. I don't like these large spaces with lots of people. I get a little winged out.

— Tony Robbins lives very near you, Chamath. Let's get Tony Robbins in there to clear that blockage. You're a Tony Robbins guy, Mark. Why are you so into him?

I love Tony Robbins. I had him at my conference this week. He did an incredible presentation for an hour. I am the number one fan of Tony Robbins. If it wasn't for Tony Robbins, I don't think there would be a Salesforce.

— What were those big kind of blockages and how did he help you?

Just focusing on: hey, the quality of your questions is the quality of your life. Are you asking yourself the right questions? What do you want? What's important to you? How are you getting it? What is preventing you from having it? How will you know that you have it? Just no one had ever said that to me. That is what gave me motivation as soon as I got that clarity.

— Did you run across the coals? Yes, many times.

All In Summit, fifth year in a row. Allin.com/events, September 13th, 14th and 15th. We'll have you back, Mark, in year six.

议题二：AI 对软件行业的冲击 — "Sasspocalypse"

— Salesforce down a whopping 37%, $90 billion losses. ServiceNow 42%, Workday 45%. $180 billion in market cap has been lost. The assumption is that AI is going to make it unnecessary for you to use Slack or Salesforce or HubSpot — you'll just ask your AI to solve this for you. What's your response?

You're right, it's the Sasspocalypse. It's not my first Sasspocalypse, honestly. We are all now drinking Salesforce Sass-perillas. We actually have a pet Sasquatch as well. We're all a little more sassy.

Look, the market has re-rated. It's not a mystery. There are cycles. I've been doing Salesforce for 27 years, enterprise software for 40. HubSpot is trading at two times sales. I've never seen that before. They just had a great quarter. The top 10 major enterprise software companies all had great quarters and they're all trading at two times sales. Because there's like a hypnosis around AI, and we haven't seen it show up in the numbers yet. If it shows up in the numbers, maybe people will be right. Right now, all we know is there's still a lot of enterprise software being sold in the world.

— What's your strategy?

It's a lot easier to be a private company right now where you don't have to be rationalized by the public markets. What I tell my employees: you can't get drunk on the stock price. You have to find a different anchor. I'm focused on customer success, ours, our revenue. We'll do over $46 billion this year, more than $16 billion in cash flow, more than 83,000 employees. I can't control the stock price. The market's re-rated.

Chamath：低端完蛋，高端安全

I think the low end of the market is basically finished. There is no safe space. The high end of the market, where Mark operates, where the large monoliths operate, is quite safe. The tell was this week. The Deloitte deal basically shows you what OpenAI is running into. You have to put in $4 billion, you give them a 17.5% preferred guaranteed return — for what? Essentially to stand up a competitor to Ernst & Young, Andersen, Deloitte, PwC, Cognizant, etc. That doesn't actually mathematically make that much sense if you look at what those companies are trading at.

But why is OpenAI doing this? Because at the high end of the market, where all the action is, what people are finding is — hey, hold on a second, this is a lot harder than we thought. It's not like, boop, boop, boop, put in a prompt and beep, bat, boop, it all works. It's not how it works. So I think we're a little oversold. Now I think this consolidation and re-rating can happen in the opposite direction.

The opposite trade: who has constructive net dollar retention, who has negative churn that's been really predictable, who has the best relationships, meaning they're inside the CXOs and the C-suite, they have relationships with the CEOs and they're trusted, and they've been around for 20 years. Those guys are positioned to crush.

Eventually, Jason, the next trade that has to happen is when the public markets become a little bit less breathless about AI and they ask one simple question: okay guys, you've spent $3 trillion in the last four years. What is the ROI of these tokens? And what's going to happen is they're going to have to go to guys like Mark and say, "please sell my tokens." That's the next shoe to drop.

Benioff：$300M Anthropic、Informatica、Slack 与 Agentforce

You've initiated a massive stock buyback — $50 billion. One of the largest in history.

We want to buy back as much as we can. At a high level, there are awesome new capabilities. These coding agents are awesome. Anthropic is awesome. I am going to probably use $300 million of Anthropic this year at Salesforce coding. Everything's going to be cheaper to make. It's more efficient. I can do things I just could not do before. I can implement my software and sell it at the same time. I've never been able to do that before. Today, I have humans, agents, and headless platforms all interoperating, never before. Anthropic is a rocket ship that will not stop.

We just finished one of our biggest deals ever, Informatica, $8 or $9 billion. None of this stuff works if you don't have context. The AI is very probabilistic. It needs to be grounded in real data. It needs that semantic layer, locked down into the truth, into a single source of truth, or it just cannot work well.

If you call 1-800-NO-SOFTWARE right now, for the first time in 27 years, you talk to AgentForce. After it authenticates you, if it doesn't know what you need, boom, it'll auto-escalate you to a human being who says "oh, I can see everything you've been doing." That trinity of the phone, the web and the human being are all together. It's all made possible by AI.

— Was it controversial to make Slack headless?

We were the first with an XML API, a SOAP API, a REST API, now an A2A API, MCP API. Our apps are not hard-coded where we had to cut the top off them — they've always been embedded inside our platform. We can stream our apps out through a new API called ACL, and I can manifest it into any large language model or device.

When Jack Dorsey wrote that memo, J. Cal, part of what he said is he's going to try to run his company by building a world model. My initial thought was — where is the context, the semantic understanding? A lot of it for companies is actually in Slack. Slack and email. All your DMs, all your channels, we're reading through AI. Slack bot is reading stuff nobody knew was happening. I can ask it: what are my top five deals? What are my employees upset about? What are the top three things I need to focus on?

— I think you should really look at making Slack more open and cheaper and getting more of that context. I wrote literally before I got on the show a new prompt: every two hours in our Slack, tell me what decisions are being made, who's making those decisions, and what you would handicap those decisions if you were my chief of staff, CEO, or board member. I created personas to evaluate decision-making.

OpenAI, Anthropic — every AI company is standardized on Slack. And our core Sales Cloud and Service Clouds as well.

— What's your advice to a software company that was supposed to go public, didn't? Here's some Kleenex for their tears.

Guys, grow up. The public markets rationalize everything all the time. In private markets, your valuation is fantasy land until somebody is actually going to pay you. Focus on your revenue, your customers, your cash flow, your profitability, your innovation. How are you going to add value to your customers?

Friedberg：垂直 vs 横向

We're doing a big Salesforce implementation at Ohalo. One of my observations: we've kind of dropped all the vertical software, but we're doubling down our investment in horizontal tools. We're investing heavily in platform capabilities, then we build apps and workflows on top of them specific to our business — rather than buy an off-the-shelf app. There's a vertical-horizontal shift where they're trading the same right now, but you could probably break them. There's probably a good arbitrage there.

In the last 27 years, we calculated somewhere between 20 to 30 million people we didn't call back. Just this week, I called back 50,000 people just through agents — I just bought a company called Qualified. The BDR, the SDR function. I can do things I could never do before.

议题三：OpenAI vs Apple

Breaking news. OpenAI is considering suing Apple over their ChatGPT partnership. Two years ago, Apple and OpenAI announced ChatGPT would integrate into Siri, iOS, Mac OS. According to Bloomberg, the deal has gone so poorly for OpenAI that they might sue Apple for breach of contract.

OpenAI's gripes: - Apple ChatGPT integration within Siri requires users to specifically say "ChatGPT" to get results. - Apple hasn't promoted the integration at all. - Users are still overwhelmingly going to the standalone ChatGPT app. - OpenAI expected billions of subscription revenue from the deal.

Apple's side: - Concerns about OpenAI's privacy practices. - Maybe they don't trust the guy in charge. - They're annoyed that OpenAI is building hardware to compete with Apple and recruited their design guru, Jony Ive.

Benioff：LLM 的"枢轴"时代

What has happened: we have these LLMs starting to come out. Every company has chosen a slightly different path:

  • Elon (Grok) — companions, sex bots.
  • OpenAI — Sora video, ad networks.
  • Gemini — Nano Banana.
  • Anthropic — "we don't know about those sex bots and Nano Banana, but we're going to do coding agents." It turned out Anthropic was right.

Now everybody's like, "whoa, where did Anthropic — oh, whoa, they're way up there." Everybody's resetting and going coding agents — "everybody focus, focus, kill Sora, kill this, get rid of that, sex bots off, cursor on." That's where we are right now.

This is what's cool about right now: it's such a dynamic moment in our industry. People have to pivot, ask the question, "what do you want right now?" If you looked at where everybody was a year ago and where they are now, they're in a totally different place. When Anthropic 4.6 hit, boom, everyone could code in their companies. Before that, they really couldn't.

We're working on technology inside Slack to make it easier for everybody to code. Most of our engineers just speak. One of our guys has a foot-button operated thing. There is no hands-on keyboard anymore. We're going for dolphin flow at Salesforce. Dolphins on keyboards.

Friedberg & Chamath：助手之战

— What should Apple do?

At the end of the day, Google has a real opportunity to integrate a Gemini assistant into all of your personal information — Gmail, Calendar, Google Drive, Google Photos — where all of your personal information sits. It can become your point of calendaring, your point of asking questions about your personal life. In the enterprise context, that's also up for grabs with G Suite. Google has a real chance to own that assistant interface.

Apple has an embedded install piece — how many people are using Apple services for mail, calendaring, storing information? Apple is probably going to need to either build their own or white-label with a partner — it could be Anthropic, could be someone else. But Google Assistant is going to end up being kind of a real kick-ass product once they get this flywheel going.

— Apple has the clearest path to becoming a top two or three player in AI simply by buying something like Perplexity or Mistral, and then using their hardware footprint. I just got this MacBook with 48GB of RAM with an M5. It is unbelievable. The new Studios are going to have a terabyte of RAM. They have a clear path to maintaining your privacy, running local models. It's going to be very easy to index all your images without giving the data to OpenAI, which nobody trusts, or to Gemini. The future is going to be local models running on extraordinary desktop hardware.

— I'm not a huge believer in these local models. You want persistence. You have multiple devices, multiple personas, you're using a web browser in one instance, a different computer at home in another. The idea that you don't have persistence that follows you around in 2026, I think, is a breaking feature. You're not going to lug around a five-pound MacBook Pro everywhere.

— You will be able to use iCloud for this as well, so it's going to sync effortlessly.

— I just wonder, don't we have an iPhone moment that we're just all going to be surprised by, where somebody shows up and they're like, "this thing" — and you're like, oh my God, that's the thing? There's enough ingredients here where somebody's going to cook something up. Apple is the product of 30 years of meticulous process optimization. What do you do if the form factor is totally different?

多感官 AI 与 Token 消耗

Mira Murati released Thinking Machines' new real-time world. Apple at the same time has patented putting cameras into AirPods. These persistent hardware devices — the watch, the earpieces — are going to know your entire reality. This is going to lead to a use of tokens that would be a thousand times what business users are currently using.

Mira's model works by watching your desktop, listening to all the voices, watching your webcam — all at the same time. Every 200 milliseconds, it's uploading to two different models: one is deep thinking and looking backwards at maybe a 30-second clip, and the other one's in real time. That will 1000x the need for tokens if people are doing this for eight hours a day. It's not turn-based "here's my latest prompt, here's my question, I got a response." It's real time, always querying an LLM, which is going to require a hardware upgrade.

— Mark?

It's one small step for man, one giant leap towards AGI. We've been so kind of "the LLM is the be-all end-all, we're going to go to AGI." I don't really understand how large language models — only about language and words, one word, then the next word — is going to get us to where we want to go to, which is Minority Report and all the science fiction movies. But what we saw — multi-sensory models — that demo was pretty awesome. I'm a multi-sensory model in a biological computer. Eyes, ears, mouth, brain, heart. I'm not exactly a large language model.

So multi-sensory models are the next big wave for AI. But we're still not at AGI at that point. Every model company is going to pivot. You're going to have to pick your poison.

— You said you were spending $300 million on tokens with Anthropic. Imagine what this would do to an average employee if they needed 1000 times the tokens. Instead of $150K in tokens, you've got to spend $100 million per employee.

I don't think that's true. We are wasting a lot of these tokens. We're using $300 million of Anthropic this year, and we're coding, coding, coding. The vast majority of those tokens don't need to go to Anthropic. There needs to be an intermediary layer that says, "oh, that one has to go to Anthropic, but these ones can be handled by smaller models." We're in such an early moment.

The edge and the cloud are going to come together. Yes, we're going to have more distributed intelligence, on the edge. We're going to have multi-sensory models. There's going to be a hot new company that's going to come along and say: "I'm going to sit between you and Anthropic and OpenAI, and I'm going to make sure that you only need their tokens when you actually need them." It's like — "oh, I need a shower." Okay, great, all the water. No, no, no — I just need some water. I don't need all the water.

议题四：科学角 — El Niño 即将来临

This Science Corner is about the dreaded El Niño season that is coming up. This is a forecast for sea surface temperature anomalies. The ocean has heated up so much that there's now a forecast on ocean temperatures that are going to exceed anything we have seen in recent history — temperatures that might be four degrees above normal. That doesn't sound like a lot.

The top image is the sea surface temperature anomaly — how different the sea surface temperatures are. This is compared to 1877 when we had the biggest El Niño year ever.

为什么这重要：海洋是天气的电池

The oceans are the battery of weather. The oceans absorb heat energy — absorb, absorb, absorb, hotter and hotter. Then that heat energy is released into the atmosphere, and the atmosphere drives weather events all over the world. You can estimate the weather over the next year based on how the temperatures are sitting in the ocean today.

There is so much excess energy stored up in the oceans. About 11 million terawatt hours. The whole planet Earth uses 25,000 terawatt hours in a year. So 11 million extra terawatt hours — that's 500 years' worth of human energy stored in this ocean. Over the next few months, that energy is going to be released into the atmosphere. 99% confidence, that will make the upcoming year the hottest year on record.

区域影响

  • Southwest, California, Gulf Coast: major atmospheric river events, water dumped on you.
  • Northern US to Canada: very low snowfall, high heat waves. Remember all those fires a couple of years ago in Canada.
  • Interior US, Phoenix: already seeing 106°F in May. A super El Niño could extend heat domes to never-before-seen temperatures.
  • Southern Argentina, Chile, Brazil: record-shattering heat waves. Crops start to fail. Brazil is the world's largest ag exporter.
  • Australia: wheat crop fails. Goes to Indonesia and the Philippines. Hundreds of millions of people depend on it.
  • India: scariest one. If the monsoons fail — high probability event. 150 million farmers in India depend on agricultural output. 1.5 billion people depend on that food.

二阶、三阶效应

Energy prices spiking and electricity spiking, grid failing in parts of the Southwest. Commodity prices spiking globally. India, Philippines, Vietnam facing unrest if food supply isn't enough.

India is a really nasty one — significantly dependent on the monsoon, but also currently facing a shortage of nitrogen-based fertilizer because of the Iran crisis and the Strait of Hormuz. Double whammy. Over the next year in South Asia, you could see a calorie deficit and a major economic crisis.

This El Niño event — the data now seems pretty confirmed. The commodity markets are trading this El Niño event pretty actively right now.

— What about the food problem?

That's a big problem. From a US perspective, our big issues are the fire season. This does decrease Atlantic hurricanes — you're going to have fewer hurricanes this year in the South. But major storm events in California, heat waves. Brazil has a debt problem — this could end up becoming an economic problem.

— Can your super seeds help?

Longer term solution. Having crop genetics that can adapt to a more rapidly changing climate is going to be critical over the next couple of decades.

— Isn't another part of this — as certain places heat up, they become viable for crops — Canada, northern US? Farmland's expanding because of global warming.

Right. We increasingly grow soybeans in Canada. Through both plant breeding and warmer temperatures, shorter winters, we've been able to grow that crop further north. But Australia's wheat goes to Indonesia, Vietnam. They have to go scramble. On a global basis, there's always a bit of give and take. But in a year like this, when you have this much energy pumped into the atmosphere, you may not have the give and the take. That's where things start to buckle.

(Earlier Ukraine wheat warning context: Friedberg gave that warning four years ago, which contributed to a Russia-Ukraine carve-out for wheat and fertilizer exports during the war.)

议题五：Anthropic 取消 SPV

Anthropic has had enough of the shenanigans of the multi-tiered, multi-layered SPVs being sold to dentists for 10% load-in fees. They called out specific platforms and said, "we're going to negate them."

— Chamath?

Tempest in a teapot or long overdue?

Long overdue. Mark said it right. All these companies should go public and get a valuation and focus on the higher-order bid. All of these ticky-tacky mechanisms that people use to stay private longer need to get a bullet put in its head. These SPVs are the worst — the layered SPVs on SPVs on SPVs.

I guarantee you: once SpaceX goes public, once Anthropic goes public, once OpenAI goes public, you're going to see a litany of lawsuits back and forth between the purveyors of these SPVs. They should not be allowed. They should be all negated if possible. Now, you can't unbreak the egg.

I hope every company adopts this, and as a result, these companies go public sooner and they rationalize their equity structure faster. Inevitably, because it happened in Uber, somebody gets screwed in a layered SPV somewhere that they didn't know about, and they're going to be like, "what happened?" Read the fine print. You're getting double carry, you paid 10% on the load-in fee, and you paid a price 10% over what the last round was. Recipe for disaster.

结尾：慈善与 Susan Wojcicki 的纪念

— Mark, fan question: are you more Steve Jobs or more Laurene Powell right now?

The best decision I ever made — when I started Salesforce, we put 1% of our equity, 1% of our profit, and 1% of all of our employees' time into a foundation. Today, we've done more than 10 million hours of volunteerism. We've given away more than a billion dollars in grants. We run over 50,000 nonprofits for free on our platform. Every company should do this. Pledge1percent.org. Best decision I ever made 27 years ago.

— Susan Wojcicki tragically died of cancer…

Okay, you're going to get me crying. Let's not go there. You're talking about my best friend, my bestie. She was a star. The best. She was the best.

This was one of the great people on the planet — incredible woman, mother of five amazing children, two amazing sisters, great family. Everybody knows Anne, her sister, her sister Janet at UCSF. Sorry, my eyes are tearing up — she contracted a very rare form of cancer, passed away too young, a couple of years ago now. She was on our board. She was one of the great people of all time.

I'm so grateful that her family is focusing on building a new foundation to go after this rare cancer. We're backing it. Everyone should try to be a Susan. She was one of the absolute greats. I have an altar in my office. When you come to see me, you'll see the photos of her and me. I think about her every single day. My heart stays with her, her husband Dennis, all the children, the sisters, the whole Wojcicki family. One of the great institutions in Silicon Valley. We're lucky to have the whole family.

— Rest in peace, Susan Wojcicki. We'll see you all next time on the All In Podcast. Love you, boys. Aloha. Thanks for having me. Bye-bye.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的观察：Sasspocalypse 是一场"语义错位"，不是技术替代
最让我意外的不是 Salesforce 跌了 37%，而是 Benioff 在节目里轻描淡写地承认："今年我会在 Anthropic 上花 3 亿美元。" 这是一个被忽略的信号——**SaaS 巨头不是被 AI 杀死的受害者，而是 AI 算力的最大付费客户之一**。市场的恐慌定价（HubSpot 跌到 2x sales）其实是一种"语义错位"：投资者把"AI 会替代 SaaS UI"的故事和"SaaS 的现金流引擎"混为一谈。但 SaaS 公司握着的是**语义层 + 数据所有权 + CRO 关系**，这恰恰是 LLM 概率性输出必须"接地"的地方。Chamath 那句"下一只鞋掉下来时，OpenAI 会求 Mark 帮他们卖 token"——这是整集最锋利的预测，因为它把 AI 价值链翻了个底朝天：**模型公司是上游产能过剩的大宗商品供应商，SaaS 是稀缺的渠道**。
## 跨领域连接：Thucydides 陷阱 × El Niño × 算力扩散，其实是同一个"非零和"问题
仔细听这集，三个看

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Did One Mutation Make Humans Creative? – David Reich
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽略的"反向发现"

最让人意外的不是科学家发现了什么，而是他们没有发现什么。Reich 团队 2016 年的研究真正震撼之处在于：在人类行为现代性爆发的关键窗口（10万–5万年前），基因组里竟然找不到任何一个横扫全人类的"创造力开关"。这就像考古学家挖开金字塔，发现里面没有法老——空缺本身成了最重要的证据。

这意味着我们一直在用错误的隐喻理解人类智能的起源。"一个突变造就了人类创造力"是一种软件思维——以为找到那行关键代码就能解释一切。但 Reich 的数据指向一种生态系统思维：智能不是一个开关，而是上百个微小变异在群体里同方向漂移的合奏。没有主角，只有合唱团。

跨领域连接：克莱、复杂系统与"潜在维度"

Reich 说人类基因组里"包含着塑造几乎任何性状所需的黏土（clay）"——这个比喻极其精妙，几乎完美对应机器学习里的潜在空间（latent space）概念。一个训练好的大模型并不为每个新任务重新长出参数，它只是在已有的高维空间里重新组合已经存在的方向。10万年前那个一万人左右的非洲小群体，可能就是人类的"基础模型"——所有今天看到的多样性，都是这个小种子在不同环境中被微调出来的不同 fine-tune 版本。

这里有一个更深的反直觉：多样性不需要遗传上的新发明，只需要不同的"选择压力提示词"。 寒带、热带、农业、游牧——这些不是制造了新的人，而是从同一团黏土里捏出了不同的形状。

隐藏的系统性真相：文化加速 ≠ 生物加速

最深的洞见藏在节奏的错位里。考古记录显示文化在5万年前加速，但基因记录显示生物在同一时期没有加速。这条裂缝告诉我们：文化进化和生物进化可能在那个时刻彻底脱钩了。

这个脱钩点可能就是人类区别于所有其他物种的真正奇点——不是"我们变聪明了"，而是"信息的载体从基因转移到了符号"。从那以后，创新可以在一代人之内横扫整个群体，不再需要等基因频率慢慢爬升。这也解释了为什么最近1.8万年又出现了多基因选择信号：当文化把人类抛进了农业、密集人口、新疾病的全新环境里，生物进化又被反向重启了——文化先跑，基因再追。

人类不是靠一个突变变得有创造力的。人类是靠学会让突变变得不再必要，才变得有创造力的。

📝 转录文本

文化加速期的谜题

100,000 to 50,000 years ago, there's a quickening of the pace of change in culture. So, people used to see the first extensive representational art and like bead necklaces and drawings on the wall and so on and so forth. And also a rapid increasing pace of innovation in the types of tools that people use.

And so, the thought might be that there was going to be have been a some kind of genetic switch, a kind of important genetic change that was occurred in the population and in that swept to high frequency that everybody suddenly had, soon had, and that made it possible for do these things to do these things. Maybe some genes that have allowed people to have like complex language, representational language, for example.

2016 年的"反向发现"

And so, one thing that we did in 2016 in this paper by Shot Malik and colleagues, is we looked across the DNA for places that might be expected to look like this, that where all people living today, or nearly all people living today, share a common ancestor maybe 100,000 or 200,000 years ago.

And we looked really hard and right across all the DNA we could look at, we couldn't find anything more than 4 or 500 more recent than 4 or 500,000 years ago. This is like a crazy result because it looks like there's no key selective sweeps that have occurred in this period that is ancestral to everyone living today.

We talked before about no selective sweeps between Europeans and East Asians, but there don't even seem to be any selective sweeps between like share between all humans in this really important period when a lot of evidence in the material culture record appears.

多基因适应的可能性

And so, it could be that there's biological adaptation in this period, but it's polygenic. There's lots of mutations that all shift in the same direction to help the population to move to a new set point, but there's no key biological change that rises to high frequency in this time.

And this group, uh, 50,000 years ago, they're the ancestors of everybody out of Africa or also some Africans. So, this is 100 to 50,000 years ago, and this is the population that's ancestral to West Africans, to most East Africans, to all non-Africans.

非洲内部的更古老分支

And there's a couple of populations in Africa that have substantial ancestry that comes from more divergent groups. For example, Khoisan from Southern Africa or Central African rainforest hunter-gatherers have substantial fractions of their ancestry from groups that diverged maybe 200,000 years ago from the other lineages.

But all of these groups today are able to do go to college, do everything everybody else does. And so, there is like no evidence that there is any key mutation lacking in some groups that are not present in the others.

小种群中的潜在多样性

Mhm. So, um, the the differences we see between different groups of people, um, especially if this group of people 50 to 100,000 people years ago had a very small population size. I think the last time we were discussing on the order of 10,000 people.

Yeah. So basically everybody in the world or almost everybody in the world, um, or the variants we see between different humans today was latent in this group, and which is it was sort of seems. And I guess your point is that well, um, if you just stack up different uh, different different things across the genome, then stacking them up really has a big effect. But that, um, it's interesting that like we have so many different groups in the world today, and that all that diversity comes from a very small population.

"黏土"假说

A lot of us in human genetics think that the our population contains within it the clay that's needed to make almost any trait, and that depending on environmental conditions or selection conditions, the mean value of these traits will move in different directions.

There's an empirical question, a real question about how much selection there's been in different human populations over time.

近期人类的持续进化

One of the things this new work that we're involved in is doing is showing that at least in the last 18,000 years, 10,000 years, 5,000 years in this part of the world, there actually has been significant movement at least for a handful of important traits.

We looked at more than 500 traits, about 100 of them complex traits showed significant movement in systematic direction over this time period. So, it really does seem that there is a response to the environments people are living in that is occurred over this period and is potentially stronger than in previous periods.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被忽略的"反向发现"
最让人意外的不是科学家发现了什么，而是他们**没有发现什么**。Reich 团队 2016 年的研究真正震撼之处在于：在人类行为现代性爆发的关键窗口（10万–5万年前），基因组里竟然找不到任何一个横扫全人类的"创造力开关"。这就像考古学家挖开金字塔，发现里面没有法老——空缺本身成了最重要的证据。
这意味着我们一直在用错误的隐喻理解人类智能的起源。"一个突变造就了人类创造力"是一种**软件思维**——以为找到那行关键代码就能解释一切。但 Reich 的数据指向一种**生态系统思维**：智能不是一个开关，而是上百个微小变异在群体里同方向漂移的合奏。**没有主角，只有合唱团。**
## 跨领域连接：克莱、复杂系统与"潜在维度"
Reich 说人类基因组里"包含着塑造几乎任何性状所需的**黏土**（clay）"——这个比喻极其精妙，几乎完美对应机器学习里的**潜在空间（latent space）**概念。一个训练好的大模型并不为每个新任务重新长出参数，它只是在已有的高维空间里**重新组合**已经存在的方向。10万年前那个一万人左右的非洲小群体，可能就是人

item.title: [Business of Sport] What’s Going on at Real Madrid & Tennis Players Threaten Grand Slam STRIKE
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

表面是足球与网球，深层是两种"权力幻觉"的对照实验

这期节目最令人意外的地方，不在于皇马的混乱或网球大满贯的分账争议本身，而在于两件事其实是同一个故事的镜像。皇马是"民主外壳下的独裁"——10万 socio 名义上选总统，但 Perez 通过反向工程规则，让 2013、2017、2021、2025 四届无人竞选；而网球大满贯则是"商业外壳下的慈善"——温网 90% 的利润流向 LTA 做青训，球员误以为对手是吸血资本家，实则是另一群在打基础的人。两边都在和自己想象中的敌人作战。Perez 假装在为 socio 服务，球员假装大满贯在剥削他们——错位的叙事比真实的利益冲突更危险。

"Galáctico 战略"是一种被严重低估的财务陷阱，本质上和硅谷的"增长换利润"叙事同构

主持人提到的核心数据极其反直觉：皇马收入 10 亿英镑/年，比曼联高 1.5 亿，看似全球第一俱乐部——但球场债务超过 10 亿，每年利息和本金 1 亿。这意味着 Perez 不是"用最强阵容统治欧洲"，而是被自己的人设挟持：他的整个总统合法性建立在"我在任就有 Galáctico"，所以他不能像阿森纳/热刺那样诚实告诉球迷"我们要节衣缩食几年"。于是他发明了"合同到期免签"模式——把转会费转化为签字费，名义上没花钱，实际上用球员的人格筛选机制换取了财务腾挪空间。结果就是：你系统性吸引来的，恰恰是那些愿意对原俱乐部冷酷算计的人格。Mbappé 的度假照不是偶然事件，是招聘算法的必然输出。这和某些科技公司用超高股权吸引"愿意背叛上家"的高管，结果发现公司文化崩塌，是同一个机制。

跨领域连接：网球大满贯的处境，其实最像大学捐赠基金，而不是体育联盟

球员把大满贯类比成 NBA 老板（"你赚那么多凭什么不分给我们 50%"），这是一个类别错误。NBA 的钱进股东口袋，温网的钱进青训。从财务结构上讲，大满贯更像哈佛的捐赠基金——一个有几十年品牌护城河、不依赖个体明星、把超额利润再投入基础生态的非营利实体。这就解释了一个反直觉判断：球员越是顶级，谈判筹码反而越弱。因为 Sinner 和 Sabalenka 罢赛威胁的是一个不靠他们存在的品牌——温网即使没有他们也照样满座，电视合同也是长期锁定的。这和 LIV 高尔夫的处境完全相反：LIV 没有遗产，所以球员就是产品；温网有 150 年遗产，所以球员是租客。Bryson 威胁去 YouTube 反而能成立，正是因为 PGA Tour 的"遗产护城河"远不如四大满贯——这是一个被几乎所有体育评论员忽略的结构性差异。

一个隐藏的系统性真相

皇马的 Mourinho 回归和网球的罢赛威胁，都是"明星经济学"耗尽后的回光返照。当一个组织无法再靠新增长解决问题时，它会本能地呼唤"强人"——皇马呼唤 Mourinho 来用人格压制更衣室，球员呼唤 Djokovic/Sabalenka 来用名气压制赛事方。但强人模式恰恰是组织失去系统性能力的症状，而不是解药。真正健康的系统从不需要"超自然的野兽"（Perez 的自我描述）来维系。

📝 转录文本

开场：Beer o'clock 的特别录制

Charlie： Mourinho has been on a downward slide for 5, 6, 7 years now. If I was one of Real's big rivals, I'd be looking at that going, this is great. Yeah, he might stop them all fighting each other, but is he going to be the guy who's going to get Real playing like PSG? I don't think so. I don't think so.

Talking of entitled people... Is that the position you're going to come at this from? Come on, bring it on.

I'll try and line this out as simply as possible. It's been a battle between the tennis players and the tournaments, most notably the Grand Slams, between how much players are compensated in comparison to how much revenue the tournaments are making. When it comes to the ATP Tour and the WTA Tour, they have to compete for the top players. This is not a problem facing the Grand Slam.

And actually, whether Rumi Savalenka earns 20 million bucks a year or 23 million bucks a year, that's a matter for her. And if she decides, like Bryson DeChambeau, that she wants to go and play on YouTube, she should knock herself out.

I think Saudi Pro League and various other things have been the test case for the idea that it's not about the competition. It's about which other players happen to be there at one time. Where this has come from is the freak one-off case that's Lionel Messi. The greatest footballer in history.

Sorry, Charlie. No, no. No, no. Got a bit of a rant there. No, I love it. The pint's halfway down. It's starting to... He's in rant mode.

录制背景说明

Good evening, Charlie. And I say good evening because this is a slightly different setup for us today and we've brought a couple of friends with us. Well, we have. It's been a planes, trains and automobiles day for me today. My travel arrangements went slightly wrong, which meant that although I was meant to be here early in the day, I've had to... a bit of forgiveness and a little bit of patience from my fellow Charlie to allow this recording to happen at what is colloquially known as beer o'clock.

So we've decided to actually live up to... Yeah, we've gone full Farage. How? we're here with our lagers. Don't worry, this is not going to become a regular occurrence. It might do, but I think we'll just have to wait and see how it goes. But yes, it is 6pm on a Wednesday evening as we record this. And we've got a slightly troubling thing, Chas, is we've got some quite detailed subject matter to go through. It's worrying, isn't it? It is worrying. We picked a good week to do it. Oh no, it's terrible. Let's see how it goes.

一、LIV 高尔夫：资金断流与 Bryson 的反击

We've got some stuff to catch up on first though. Yeah. Should we have a look at Liv to begin with? Because there's been a lot going on in golf since we last discussed it.

Funding hadn't officially been pulled at that stage. Now PIF have pretty much confirmed that they will not be funding it. So Liv are now looking for external investment. The security of a lot of the players and the contracts that they want is massively up in the air. And there's been development in how some of the players have been approaching that since then. One of which has been Bryson turning around saying, I would rather play all my golf on YouTube than return to the PGA Tour.

Jon Rahm 的"反向雪貂"

Yeah, well, it's been sort of two quite separate approaches by Jon Ram, who's, if you think about livers having two major bankable stars that are left, right? Jon Ram and Bryson DeChambeau. Two players who are still somewhat at the top of their game as opposed to some of the more twilight players who make up the rest of the tour and who are still, you know, serious contenders at majors and who should be currently fighting it out with Rory and Scotty Scheffler to being sort of in the top five in the world.

Ram is conducting what might be called a reverse ferret coming to terms with, um, European tour as to how he might actually previously he was saying I don't want to pay my fines I'm not going to pay my fines now it seems like he actually might pay his fines total coincidence of course just an odd random fact that it happened exactly the same time that Liv started to go down in flames so that's Jon Ram who clearly wants to just get back on the mainstream tours wants to be able to carry on making his big living etc

Bryson DeChambeau：YouTube 作为新筹码

and then you were sort of expecting Bryson to do something a bit similar And he, true to form, he didn't. He came out all guns firing. He said, well, actually, maybe if Liv's not going to be a thing going forward, maybe I'll just play the majority of my golf just on YouTube. I might turn up to a few majors, might not, don't know.

So that's absolutely fascinating because I think, look, do I think fundamentally it's a bluff? Yes, I think fundamentally it's a bluff. But it does point to the fact that Bryson has become a YouTube star. And, you know, the level of business which now he is able to do through his digital content, effectively his social media content, whatever it might be, through the audiences that he's now collected, which run into the several million on all the channels, is pretty substantial now.

And if Bryson decided, actually, do you know what? I am going to be basically like the equivalent of what used to be called a trick shot golfer, and I'm just going to play for fun and do stuff on YouTube. He could probably make an equivalent amount to what a lot of the top players could make on tour. Do I think he's actually going to do that? A compester like him? No. But I thought it was an absolutely fascinating refusal to be cowed, a refusal to back down and say, no, I've still got some leverage here. I've got leverage. I don't have to come back on the USP.

It's a counter-offensive. If we're looking at what the PGA have been doing to players coming back to live, it's been, you'll be fine. You won't be allowed into the player equity scheme. You're going to be punished to come back in. And Bryson's basically in doing this saying, I don't need you. So you're going to still need to come to me. If you think I further your tour and you think I help your sponsors spend more money with you, you need me on tour. I'm there. but I'm not if I don't like the deal.

And that is, look, fair play to him again. You know, he's turning it completely on. I love the brass neck, but the reality of the situation is that actually the US PGA Tour has not just survived, it's flourished during his absence. So the idea that they're going to say, well, our biggest single problem right now is not having Bryson DeChambeau on tour is pretty minimal. But I do, I kind of like his style.

二、英格兰联赛季后赛：Rochdale 的戏剧性升级

Okay, so... We'll keep watching that. What about the playoffs?

We talked a few weeks ago about the unbelievable outcome of York City versus Rochdale. National League. National League. Getting up into the Football League. We had a really good discussion which generated lots of controversy around whether it should be two or three games. Teams provided. Rochdale lost out to York in the final day of the season to a 103rd minute goal. So 13th minute of extra time goal.

Rochdale then got into the final playoff against Boreham Wood. Boreham Wood were 2-0 up with, I think it was 12 or 13 minutes to play. Rochdale came right back into it, scored and then scored a equalising goal in the 95th minute. It was York City type drama. Yeah. at the end and they then went on to win a penalty shootout.

升级制度的反思

And I think a big sigh of relief all round right so from you know from the sporting purist point of view a a team who got more than 100 points not going up would have felt wrong in some way. Now, I laid out last time why that was very much the National League's choice. The National League could just promote two teams automatically. They choose not to. They choose to have a playoff system.

But I'm sure that everyone was praying that they would win the playoffs and therefore that wouldn't end up being a sort of really uncomfortable situation where a team got promoted with 20 or 30 points less than than Rochdale got.

But also a relief, I think, you know, there was a lot of feedback from my comments saying I'm not in favour of three up because there are still too many teams in the National League who are not yet properly league ready and you would risk teams coming up into the league who are still dare I say it, a little bit semi-pro in their nature.

And the team that Rochdale beat in the playoff final, Borehamwood, are actually full-time professional now. They were semi-pro before, but they're now full-time professional now. But they are, you know, if you go back to what I was saying, which is, do you have the infrastructure and the fan base and the size around you to be a sustainable league club? You know, you look at Boreham Wood with average attendance is about 1,300 a game, right? So that's about the same as what we get at Walton and Horsham in tier seven, not too different.

Yeah. And you're looking at that thinking, is that really... Look, absolutely respect the level of performance that Boren would have managed to achieve on very, very low crowds. And they've been outperforming their level for quite some time now and been very, very smart at being able to hoover up a certain type of London-based player who's dropped out of the system a little bit. And they've been super smart. They've actually also got quite an interesting player development system as well. But nonetheless, I think from both the National League's point of view and from the Football League's point of view, probably a bit of a relief that a club that small has not ended up coming up into the football league.

Yeah. I mean, great entertainment. That was an amazing game. It was an amazing game.

三、英超战报：阿森纳、切尔西与降级悬念

Broader football check-in though. Less exciting for me. I was wondering last week when you were gleefully talking about how many games had consistently gone against me it would take for a game to go in my favour. And I was looking at the fixtures thinking, with Chelsea playing Liverpool at lunchtime, that could get significantly worse.

But I'm pleased to say that ahead of the FA Cup final tomorrow when this comes out, Chelsea on Saturday proved they could play semi-decent football, albeit against a Liverpool team that is flattering to deceive, to say the least. Out of form. Yeah. But also that Arsenal and Spurs, you know, should we say Arsenal...

A fascinating game. I don't know what you thought about it. While I was constantly having to remind myself... This is Arsenal against West Ham. Arsenal against West Ham, sorry. You know, the... that there was so much on this game from both sides you got I kind of got wrapped up in the oh that's a goal for Arsenal which means it's better for them to win the league but then you have to look at the flip side of what that did to West Ham
