item.description:
## 一个出人意料的观察：她贩卖的"灵活福利"，本质上是在解构她自己人生的隐喻
Liya 一边在政策圈里推动"把福利从W2雇佣关系中解耦"（decouple benefits from employment），一边在自己的人生里实践着同一件事——把"身份"从单一职业角色中解耦。她是学者、是公共写作者、是政策企业家、是项目搭建者、是新手妈妈，这些角色像可携带的福利一样，挂在"她"这个人身上，而不是挂在某一个机构岗位上。
换句话说，**她的政策主张不是中立的学术输出，而是她自己人生算法的外推**。她为什么会本能地看到"工人需要可携带的福利"？因为她本人就是一个"可携带福利的人"——好奇心、resilience、startup mindset 是她的便携式福利包，跟着她从Sunni Purchase到Mercatus到国会作证再到育儿。**一个人在政策上反复推动的隐喻，往往就是她个人生存策略的放大**。这也解释了为什么访谈最后她说自己最焦虑的事是"会不会陷入确认偏误"——因为她隐隐意识到：当一个人的政策主张与她的人生哲学完美对齐时，那种"丝滑的说服力"本身就是一种危险信号。
## 跨领

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1432期】INTC其实没那么贵！CPI暴雷了，吗？对后市的看法！CRCL财报✨20260512#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：这次"AI泡沫论"其实是一场认知错位

主持人触及了一个核心点，但没有把它推到极致——我来推：这一轮AI周期最特殊的不是技术爆发，而是"金主结构"的根本性反转。

2000年的科技泡沫，资本开支来自借债扩张的电信公司和初创企业（现金流脆弱）；而这一轮的Capex来自微软、谷歌、Meta、亚马逊——它们是地球上现金流质量最好的资产。这意味着芯片/光模块/存储的下游需求方，本质上是"防御性资产"。

这就形成了一个奇特的金融学错位：进攻性最强的板块（AI芯片），其需求护城河来自最防御性的现金流。这是历史上罕见的"攻防嵌套"结构，传统估值框架根本没办法处理它——因为传统模型假设"高增长=高风险"，但现在是"高增长 by 低风险金主"。

二、跨领域连接：Circle的"建公路"逻辑 = 19世纪铁路 + Amazon AWS + 谷歌DeepMind

主持人聊到Circle自建公链Arc时给了一个货物/物流/高速公路的比喻，但更深的隐喻是：Circle正在重演"标准石油"的纵向整合策略——只是介质从原油变成了美元流动性。

更妙的跨域类比是这样的： - 货物层（USDC） ≈ 比特币时代的"数字黄金"叙事 - 调度层（CCTP/CPN） ≈ Visa/Swift的清算网络 - 公路层（Arc） ≈ 以太坊试图扮演的角色

Circle在做的，是把美元的应用场景从"金融工具"扩展成"美元帝国的基础设施"。这跟Meta用USDC给哥伦比亚创作者发钱的例子串起来看，会让人脊背一凉——稳定币的真实战略意义，不是替代银行，而是让美元在不需要美国监管的地方仍然形成"美元资产沉淀"。这是一种没有军舰的金融殖民，比布雷顿森林体系更隐蔽、更具网络效应。

三、隐藏的心理学真相：你以为的"逃顶"，其实是"被自己的成功诅咒"

主持人讲了一段非常重要但容易被忽略的话——逃顶不难，难的是逃了之后不再回去。我把它升级一下：

这背后是一个被严重低估的心理机制——"成功验证陷阱"（Success Validation Trap）。当一个人成功逃顶后，他得到的不是钱，而是一种"我能预测市场"的元认知幻觉。这个幻觉比损失更危险，因为它会驱动他用更大仓位去验证下一次预测。赚钱的快感会通过提升仓位来惩罚你——这是行为金融学里的"赢家的诅咒"在散户身上的精准复刻。

跨领域连接：这跟扑克牌玩家的"running good"陷阱一模一样，跟创业者拿到第一笔融资后的"幸存者偏差"也是同构的——所有"被运气奖励过的人"都会系统性地高估技能贡献。所以主持人提到自己减仓Intel从31%降下来，本质不是"看空Intel"，而是主动切断"集中持仓-情绪绑定-放大波动"的反馈环。这是一种"反人性的元交易"——交易自己的心理结构而不是交易标的。

四、一个被埋没的彩蛋：CPI房租通胀的"统计幽灵"

主持人提到4月房租CPI跳到0.6%是因为统计方法滚动剔除了去年10月的低基数——这不是经济现象，是数学幽灵。但更深的洞察是：美联储正在用一个6个月滞后的房租数据来决定一个未来12个月的利率路径。

这就像用后视镜开赛车。市场已经知道这个游戏，所以两年期利率反而往下走（降息概率上升）——市场正在交易"美联储的统计盲区"本身。这是个非常优雅的悖论：数据越失真，市场越聪明。

五、最锋利的一刀：英伟达老黄的"电网浮动论"暗藏一场革命

视频末尾提到老黄想解决电网利用率问题（目前低于60%），让AI工厂的电力需求"可浮动"——这条线索被埋得太深了。

它意味着什么？AI计算正在从"用电大户"演变成"电网的可调度负载"。换句话说，未来的AI数据中心不只是"消费者"，而是电网的需求侧管理（DSM）伙伴。这一下子把CEG、VST从"卖电的"重新定义成了"AI基础设施的调度层"。

跨域连接：这跟比特币矿场在德州扮演的"可中断负载"角色完全同构——计算密度越高的产业，越会和电网形成共生关系。所以主持人买CEG的真实alpha，可能不是"AI耗电多"这个共识，而是"AI将变成电网的伙伴而非掠夺者"这个尚未定价的预期。

📝 转录文本

开场与本期主题

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分选小桃，欢迎大家来到周二的更新。

今天这期内容三家公司：CG、Circle 分别是周一发布财报的，然后和大家分享一下英特尔估值的一个方法，看一下当下的英特尔到底有多贵；然后和大家聊一下周二公布的 CPI 数据，那周二市场上半场的这个下跌是不是因为 CPI 数据不好而导致的。

在聊这些东西之前，和大家分享一下我对于当下市场的一些看法。其实我在上周的直播里面已经聊得比较透彻了，但是今天我想做一个总结。好吧，那我就废话不多说了，这节开始。

在开始之前，拜托大家记得留言、点赞、加转发，让更多的人可以看到我们那么专业的频道。

一、周二市场资产价格回顾

先来回顾一下周二资产价格的走势，非常快速地带一下。美元、美债市场——美债市场的收益率这一块都出现了上涨，那主要可能和通胀数据有关。

但其实我们仔细去拆分通胀数据的话，这一次所公布的通胀数据其实没有那么的糟糕。但是市场通常会交易一个 headline，通常会交易一个新闻里面是怎么解读的，或者实际数据出来高于预期就是高于预期的一组数据，它不会做出过度的一些解读。但是如果仔细去看背后的话，因为对于我们来说更加重要的是背后的这个趋势到底有没有改变，那么通胀增速放缓的这个趋势到底还能不能维持，所以这可能更加的重要。

但是市场大多数的时候其实是交易一个故事线或者新闻。所以从 CPI 数据这个角度去触发，可以理解为什么美元指数出现上涨，美债收益率出现上涨，因为 CPI 的数据是高于市场预期的。那么 TLT 出现了下跌。

三大指数收盘其实还好：标普下跌 0.2%，纳指 0.7%，道指上涨了 0.1%。其实从板块这个角度去看，可以明显地看到周二是一个板块之间的轮动，相对科技股弱一些，相对芯片板块弱一些，而且几乎所有的板块都出现了上涨。

我在周五直播里面也有聊到，你看一下，我记得周五的直播的时候，我聊到，我们看一下医疗板块，其实我接下去换仓的一个方向，基本上就是往医疗板块去放。今天医疗板块周二是出现了 2% 的这个上涨。罗素两千出现了 1% 的下跌，比特币下跌了 1.4%。

二、对当下市场的六点看法

然后来聊一下我对于当下市场的一些看法。有几个事实和一些建议，包括我对后市一些看法。

1. FOMO 情绪已经非常明显

首先第一个事实是，我们现在不可否认整个市场里面是有 FOMO 的这个情绪面的，主要就是反映在芯片、光和存储，那这三个方向基本上都是和 AI 相关的。市场里面有非常明显的 FOMO，表现在什么？表现在 X 上面，你看到很多人晒自己的账户过去一个月、过去 3 个月回报有多少，包括很多 B 圈的人，现在 B 圈都不做了，都来做美股，觉得美股非常的简单，赚钱非常的简单。

所以毫无疑问，从各个社交媒体里面，我们都可以看到散户非常明显的 FOMO，而且 FOMO 过去一个多月以来走得比较好的一些标的。

那通常来说，我们不用怀疑，FOMO 大多数的情况下都不会有一个非常好的一个结果。确实没有人知道市场什么时候会见顶，但是这一个歌声或者我们说音乐它终究会是结束的。

所以难的不是很多人会觉得"哎，我这时候逃顶了"或怎么样，难的不是逃顶，难的是你逃了顶之后你不再回去，大多数人是做不到的。为什么？给大家拆细一下这背后的一个心态：我作为一个试图去探这个市场的人，我这一次逃顶了，比如说一周之后标普 500 下跌了 5%，他会觉得自己已经逃顶成功了。跌完 5% 之后，他一定会非常有信心——这个 5% 是随机的，可能 5% 可能 7%——他会非常有信心地在这个点位全部再 all in 进去，因为他觉得自己既然能够 time 这个顶，也一定能够 time 这个底。

而往往当你试图去 time 这个底的时候，可能还没有到底，或者说你常在河边走，哪有不湿鞋，一定会有一个时间点你最终判断是错误的。而当你每一次都非常自信地加大仓位进去判断错误的时候，那时候所给我们带来这个损失往往是非常巨大的。

所以满仓或者空仓试图去抄底或逃顶，几乎没有人可以做到。所以还是趁早放弃这样的一个想法，因为最终的话是盈亏同源的，你哪里赚来的钱，哪里就会亏回去。

2. 当下估值中性偏高，但远未到泡沫

然后第二点，这也是一个事实，当然你也可以认为是一个观察，就是现在纳指的一个估值是中性偏高的，绝对没有到泡沫的这个阶段。

然后标普的估值相对其实比纳指来说的话还是更加合理一些，标普现在可能 forward P/E 在 20 倍左右，去年的 10 月份是在 23 倍的，比现在要起码高了 15% 左右。芯片板块现在和纳指是类似的，是处在一个中性偏高的位置，绝对没有——我稍后有一张图，记得我拿了一张图和大家看一下芯片板块的这个估值——绝对没有回到去年 10 月份的这个水平。所以现在还没有回到去年的高位，好吗。

与 1999–2000 年科技泡沫的对比

其实顺着这个逻辑线下去，因为现在最近这段时间市场讨论的，拿现在当下的市场和 99 年、00 年的科技泡沫去做对比，我认为：

首先第一点，估值泡沫是绝对不满足的。但当然很多人说估值的确没有泡沫，这个泡沫在哪？这个泡沫在 earning 上面，在盈利上面。

但是现在和 99 年、00 年又不一样的是，现在走得好的这些标的——芯片、光、存储——他们未来一年的盈利其实都非常的确定。那为什么会非常的确定？也是现在和 99 年 00 年科技泡沫最不一样的是，这些受到 Capex 影响、或者获利于 Capex 投入的，谁是投入在 Capex？都是些大科技。

那大科技的现金流又非常的稳定，他们的业务本身非常的稳定，也就是说在这一轮周期里边和 00 年科技泡沫最大的差别就是——金主爸爸他自己的现金流是非常的稳定。当他自己的现金流非常的稳定，又不需要去严重依赖于债务的时候，那他的资本开支也会非常的稳定。

那么所以这一些板块或者这些标的——芯片、光、存储——他们未来的 9 个月，起码半年吧，半年之内或者接下去两个季度的财报确定性是非常非常的高。你可以说 9 个月之后、三个季度之后可能会存在一定的波动性，因为毕竟整个 AI 的一个变动还是非常的快，那么整个 AI 可能两周、一个月就会有一个翻天覆地的一个变化。

但是从盈利确定性这个角度去看，现在和 99 年 00 年去做对比的话，这确定性高非常非常的多，因为金主爸爸这背后的现金流质量是完全不一样。所以拿现在和 00 年简单地去做对比的话，非但首先估值泡沫是完全没有办法去做对比——现在估值根本就不在一个泡沫；从盈利这个角度去看，我个人认为 2 到 3 个季度之内绝对不可能有任何的泡沫，因为非常的确定；三个季度以外，确实现在不确定性非常的大。你也可以说就像英伟达一样，英伟达从 23 年一路走来，你只能确定半年之后的这个需求，三个季度之后的需求没人知道。

好吗，这是第二个事实或者第二个观察。

3. 用仓位策略避免跑输市场

然后第三个，就是我在 3 月份下跌的时候和大家说过，我说这时候的一个下跌是最好的考验我们自己投资策略的时候，对我们投资策略是一个非常好的一个考验。包括当时我说大科技非常的便宜，底线就是不要交出自己这个筹码。

现在也是一样，现在我们都可以感觉到市场非常的疯狂，那在当下市场非常疯狂的时候，仍然要坚持自己的一个投资策略。我知道有很多人自己的投资组合可能是错过了这一波的一些上涨，比如说一些重仓在 Meta、重仓在特斯拉。特斯拉可能这两天、其实这两周反弹的幅度还可以，但是 YTD 的走势也不咋地。

既然选择投资个股，投资个股是非常寂寞的，你就一定要忍受在一定的时间段里面我们可能跑输这个市场、可能跑输这个指数，这是必然会出现的这种结果。那么怎么去避免出现这种结果，或者怎么去避免跑输市场非常非常多？可能就是相对地不要那么的去侧重个股，或者不要去重仓一只个股或者几只个股，留一部分的仓位出来，留给指数，留给 QQQ 也好，留给标普也好。

这样的话就做一个卫星策略——核心的仓位靠标普 500、靠 QQQ 来掌握，50% 标普 500、30% QQQ，剩下的 20% 我们去挑一些标的，挑自己喜欢的一些标的。这时候也许整体的投资组合可以跑出一个 Alpha，而且也不会出现这种情况：all in 或者大仓位了几只个股，而在这一波里面可能这几只个股做得并不好，就导致自己的投资组合可能就走得比较的难受了。

所以是有方法去避免的，或者是自己可以通过仓位这个调整。其实也是我今年到现在为止，特别是我今年年初吧，我基本上把我所有的仓位全部公开在 App 上面，和大家分享仓位本身。

很多时候我聊一个标的，大家不知道我对于这个标的的 conviction 到底有多大。那我最简单的就是：我仓位最大的就是我 conviction 最高的这个标的。对吧，有些时候我一个标的聊多了，大家会以为我对于这个标的 conviction 非常的高，其实并不是。所以仓位本身就代表了 conviction，而通过控制仓位去控制整体投资组合的风险是非常非常的重要。那这个风险本身包括了下行的风险和上行的风险，好吗。

4. 音乐停止时人通常是麻痹的

然后第四点，当音乐真正停止的时候，通常人是麻痹的。我们不可能感受到音乐已经停止了，只有通过一个后视镜，我们才可以知道——哦，原来音乐在 3 个月之前就已经停止了。

就好比在去年 10 月份的时候，当时我和大家提出来说接下去又要慢慢地开始降 Beta 的时候，大家绝对不以为然的。然后 5 个月之后、3 月份的时候才会发现，哦，原来从去年的 10 月份开始降 Beta 可能是一个正确的决定，而实际上整个时间的跨度是 5 个月。那么当我刚刚提出这一个看法和想法的时候，很多人觉得，啊，那么早就要去降 Beta 吗。

所以还是希望大家不要被现在社交媒体上很多人就发日（指 day trade）——因为现在社交媒体很多人做日内交易的，那日内交易你去 all in、去重仓一些两倍 3 倍的一些 ETF 或者标的，短期之内投资回报就会非常的好。但是相信我，大多数这一类人是没有办法全仓逃顶的，不可能的。大多数人可能还是全仓的时候 all in 了一个三倍的标的，接下去定跌了 20%，他认为还会继续上涨，然后继续拿着拿着，再跌 20% 之后——你知道跌 20% 再跌 20% 的话，你可能就是从最高点回调了 40% 到 50% 左右了。那要回调到 50%，你要知道把这一部分的一个 ETF 要涨回来要涨多少？要涨一倍啊。

5. 当下市场参与度偏低

然后第五点是，市场的参与度其实现在是比较低的。那和我周五在直播的时候和大家分享的观点是一样，包括现在纳指和标普也一样，市场参与度其实是相对比较低的。

在指数不断破新高的时候，市场参与度非常的低，所以我期待的是整个涨幅会扩大。我说的涨幅会扩大，就是板块会扩大，就资金会出现这个轮动。

川普在社交媒体上说过，如果战争结束，美股会去到一个新高。其实这一点我是同意的，因为如果战争结束的话，现在一些相对比较弱势的板块，我认为会参与进来，或者说资金会流入这些板块。

对吧，那到那时候真的所有的标的、所有的板块都在出现上涨的时候，通常会伴随着什么结果？通常会伴随着市场参与度去到非常高的位置，去到 80，甚至去到 90%，那时候我们就需要小心了。

就有点类似当市场下跌的时候——记不记得 3 月份基本上是 3 月份的后半段、后两周吧，基本上是没有任何区别的一个全面杀跌，无论是所有的资产还是所有的标的是全面下跌的，就是我们说 correlation（相关性）趋近于 1。上涨的时候也是一样的，当上涨趋近于 1 的时候，所有的标的全在上涨，所有的板块全在上涨的时候，这时候我们就需要非常非常小心了，因为接下去市场就有可能会出现回调了。

6. 今天做了一次移动止盈

好么，最后一点就是我今天是做了一次移动止盈，减仓了 5% 英特尔的这个仓位。那我知道对于我来说是一个非常正确的一个选择，更多的是从整体投资组合控制风险这个角度去判断。

市场参与度这一块，就像我前面说的，纳指线的 20 均线只有 40%——40% 啊！纳指是接近新高，参与度只有 40%，有 60% 的公司没有参与这一波的上涨。如果投资个股里面有这 60% 的公司，可能就做得不是那么的好。罗素 2000 只有 43%，标普百只有 44%。

当然这一点我稍微要提醒一下的是，我记得去年 10 月份的时候，其实整个 10 月份开始指数下跌嘛，其实从 10 月份到 3 月份，基本上参与度就没有去到非常高的位置。我记得印象之中好像就没有去到过 80 以上。所以并不是说一定会出现所有标的都出现上涨、参与度去到非常高的位置；有可能会出现不断的轮动、轮动、轮动，到一个事件出来之后一起下跌。

三、芯片板块估值

接下来我们来看一下芯片板块的这个估值。上周五其实我出现过这张图，因为今天芯片板块稍微出现了一些下跌嘛，周二芯片板块出现了一部分的下跌。

那 Forward P/E 这一块是估值 25.5 倍，其实不算非常的高。对吧，我说和去年的 10 月份差不多在 29 倍左右去做对比的话，从现在到去年 9 月份一个非常高的一个位置，还有 14% 的一个上涨空间。所以从 Forward P/E 这个角度去看，现在芯片板块其实也没有那么的贵。

当然就像我前面说过的，很多人认为市场里面有泡沫，是 PE 没有泡沫但是 EPS、earning 是有泡沫的。那 earning 有没有泡沫，就是见仁见智了。但是从我个人的角度去看，对于芯片板块里面，几乎所有的个股、所有的公司吧，他们未来一年 earning 的这个确定性是非常的高的。如果未来一年 earning 的这个确定性非常的高，就代表这个 Forward P/E 是比较准确的去衡量起码一年之后他们这个估值到底在哪里。

那么我们可以辩、我们可以争论，对吧，未来的一年到底有没有这个确定性，未来一年的这个盈利到底有没有确定性？是因为他们客户的这个原因，他们的客户、他们的金主爸爸本身现金流非常的确定，且资本开支非常的确定，所以他们未来一年的这个 earning 我认为是非常确定的，所以没有办法直接和 00 年的科技泡沫去做对比——不存在 PE，绝对不存在这个泡沫；earning 有没有这个泡沫见仁见智。

四、英特尔（INTC）的估值方法

OK，接下来我们来看一下英特尔的这个估值方法。

业务拆分：Product 与 Foundry

首先我们来看一组——首先我们先知道英特尔两个业务，那么第一个业务是 Product，第二块是 Foundry。Foundry 这一块他们是亏钱的，然后 Product 这一块是赚钱的，而且是相对比较赚钱的。

那英特尔这个数据可能是周二下跌之前了，英特尔的市值是在 600、650 个 billion 点。那经过周二的这个下跌，可能 600 个 billion 点左右。我就拿 650 个 billion 来做一个假设，因为这是模糊正确嘛，你永远不可能得到一个精确的一个估值，或者这个精确的估值到底是贵还是便宜。我觉得这应该是周一的数据，还是上周五的数据，650 个 billion 点。

估值倍数计算

那我们把 price 或者把 market cap 除于它的一个 Operating Income。Operating Income 是多少？如果是看 25 年的话，12.7 个 billion 点，其实 26 年的这个预期 12.7 基本上是类似的。那我们把它的一个 market cap 除以它的一个 OP Income，差不多得出来是 50 倍的这个 multiple。

那接下去因为对于这类的公司，我们要看它的一个 PEG，因为接下去英特尔将会受到 CPU 短缺的这个影响，它的一个 margin 将会出现上升，所以它的一个利润也会出现上升。

那有两种方法：

第一种方法：你可以看未来，因为第一季度的财报、2026 年第一季度财报刚刚发布嘛。如果我把 2026 年底的这个 12.7 个 billion 点当做是一个起点的话，50 倍的这个 multiple，那看 27 年、28 年、29 年这三年，OP Income 的这个增速 39%、15% 和 50%，把这三个加在一起除以 3，然后拿 50 去除以这个平均数，差不多在 34 吧，然后得到的就是 1.3 倍的这个 PEG ratio。1.3 倍的 PEG ratio 绝对是属于低的。这是第一种方法。那这个方法得出来，英特尔现在的估值完全是属于相对比较低的。

第二种方法：就是把未来 4 年呢，就是把 26 年也算进去——26、27、28、29——那把就因为 26 年的 Operating Income 它的一个增速几乎是 0 嘛，那我可以把 0 也算进去，那就是把未来的 4 年全部加一起除以 4，差不多在 26% 左右。那拿 50 倍除以 26 的话，差不多是两倍的一个 PEG ratio 不到一点。两倍的 PEG 就属于一个相对偏高的一个位置，但绝对没有到非常贵的位置。

那这是英特尔 Product 这一块——就我们单拿英特尔的 Product 这一块，就它的 AI、它的 Client 这一块，你把这一块的业务拆分开来去看的话，按照现在 650 个 billion 的这个估值去看，没有那么的贵。

Foundry 业务的"白送"逻辑

然后在这个基础上，英特尔还有 Foundry 这一块。那问题就是现在 Foundry 是亏钱的，而且以前还亏得更多，那么 24 年、25 年的话亏得更多。

那未来这个亏钱会转向盈利，包括未来和很多家大科技之间的这个合作，包括和苹果——也许会拿苹果的一些唯一部分的一些订单。那 Foundry 这一块如果做得好的话，未来是有可能扭亏为盈的，这个只是这一个时间，可能时间跨度可能需要几年的时间。

那这个 Foundry 业务我们可以假设是零，对吧。但我们总不能假设它是负值，它是一个附加值吧。我们如果把它假设为零的话，那就单纯的拿 Intel Product 这一块出来做一个估值。

有点类似于以前亚马逊的——以前在 22 年的时候，市场就把 AWS 这一块拿出来，亚马逊的云到底是值多少钱，然后市场认为亚马逊的电商是直接送给你的，因为电商在 21 年 20 年的时候是亏钱的。

那有点类似于英特尔这一块也是一样的，我们把 Foundry 这块拿出来，假设 Foundry 是零，不给它任何的一个估值。单 Product 这一块看未来 3 看 27、28、29 3 年的话，PEG ratio 1.3 倍；把 26 年也算进去的话，PEG ratio 是两倍。

那无论哪一个数据，PEG 1.3 倍觉得是低的；PEG 如果两倍——未来 4 年的话——PEG 在一个相对的高位，但没有到非常贵的位置。所以从这个角度去看，现在 Intel 的这个估值其实没有非常的贵。

为何减仓？仓位与风险

那最后一点，很多人可能要问我"没有非常的贵，为什么要减仓"——我前面说过了，更多的是从一个整体仓位、投资组合的这个角度去做的这个选择。

因为我在减仓之前，英特尔在周一的时候，英特尔我占了我整体投资组合 31% 以上的这个仓位，那对于我整体投资组合的一个波动就会影响比较大的。比如说我在周二，周二的话最多是下跌 4%——因为英特尔最多下跌 10% 左右吧——所以对我整体投资组合的这个影响就会比较大了。那主要是去降低一部分的这些波动。

五、CPI 通胀数据解读

OK，然后我们快速地来聊一下通胀的宏观数据这一块。其实如果不是周二市场上半场出现下跌的话，市场可能没有那么关注通胀，对吧。

数据细节拆解

那通胀数据本身如果去看数据的话，核心通胀环比增速确实比市场预期高了 0.1%。仔细地去扒一下通胀吧：

  • 食品通胀和能源通胀：其实都比较高的。食品通胀 0.5，能源通胀 3.8%（这边都是环比增速）。能源通胀 3 月份其实上涨的幅度更大，所以和 3 月份相比的话稍微降了一些，但环比增速 3.8 还是非常高的这个位置。食品通胀 0.5 主要来自于牛肉、鱼、鸡蛋是涨价非常的明显。

但是起码现在的美联储他们更加关注的还是核心通胀，那核心通胀的话是会把食品通胀和能源通胀放在一边的。

核心通胀里面分为商品通胀和服务通胀： - 商品通胀：这一个月其实是不错的，0% 的这个增速，上两个月 2 月份和 3 月份都是 0.1 的。所以商品通胀和上两个月做对比的话，其实是有所改善的。 - 服务通胀：那这一个月为什么核心通胀会高，主要就是来自于服务通胀里面的房租。服务通胀环比增速 0.5，而房租通胀因为占比非常的大，0.6%。

房租通胀的统计幽灵

那为什么这一个月的房租通胀那么的糟糕？上个月是 0.3，这个月突然变成了 0.6。主要是因为服务通胀统计的这个方法是算的——房租通胀统计的方法是算的过去 6 个月房租的这个调查，而 10 月份房租的这个调查（去年 10 月份）数据是非常的低。

那你再看 4 月份的时候，你看的是去年的 11 月、12 月，今年的 1 月份到 4 月份，总共 6 个月，把去年的 2025 年的 10 月份过低的这组数据被剔除出去之后，然后你就会发现这个环比增速突然之间就出现了这个跳涨。

所以 4 月份的这一组环比增速，特别是房租通胀这一块的环比增速，某种程度是因为数据上出现一定的一个低估，然后低估的数据被剔除之后所出现的一个过高，那之后的话是会进行修正的。所以这某种程度是一次性的一个过高了。

剩下的一些服务通胀里面：医疗服务 0% 非常的好，交通服务 0.3% 好于 3 月份的，娱乐服务 0.1 是完全可以接受，教育服务负的 0.1%。

所以服务通胀你看，另外几块也比较大的——医疗服务 4% 吧，交通服务可能也是 4% 到 5%，娱乐服务和教育服务各占 5%。所以另外几块全部加在一起也占了 20% 的总共的通胀，而你们看到几乎是没有通胀的。

所以这一个季度其实除了房租通胀这一块出现了一次性的这一个问题以外，其实整体来说这一次的一个核心通胀是 OK 的。

文博他们这边做了一个预估，如果不是因为房租通胀的话，这一次的核心环比是 0.26%，那四舍五入之后 0.3% 就完全符合市场的这个预期了。

市场反应：Buy the rumor, sell the news?

从价格这一个走势去看，其实在 8 点 30 分公布完通胀数据之后，两年期的利率其实是往下的。如果两年期的利率往下说明什么？说明降息的概率是往上升了。也就是市场出现了类似于是 buy the rumor, sell the news 这种情况——就是明明从数据从表面去看，数据是差于预期的，但是降息的这个概率反而是上升的，因为两年期利率往下嘛。

然后从 8 点 42，那时候两年期利率往下，然后美股是上升的，然后 8 点 42 分出现了这个反转，我认为有资金进来了，可能开始做出了一些正确的一些反馈。所以从量化这个角度去看，当然我们也可以理解为这些量化的基金、这些背后机器人交易，他们并没有非常关注这一组通胀数据，也可以这么去理解。

所以这组通胀数据出来之后，虽然是高于预期，但是预期的数据出来之后，市场也没有做出非常大的这个反馈。然后 8 点 42 分出现了一个滞后的一个反应，之后再反映出来之后，其实利率也没有出现非常大幅度的这个上涨，但是接下去美股就开始出现了一个调整了，然后直到下午 1 点之后才出现了这个反弹。

所以总体来说吧，你去看 headline，你去看新闻的话，通胀的确比市场预期要差，不是一组非常好的通胀数据；但如果我们去纠结这背后的一些细节的话，这一个月的通胀数据是 OK 的。

六、Circle（CRCL）财报与 Arc 公链战略

OK，然后我们来聊一下周一发布财报的两家公司。第一家公司 Circle，Circle 会聊得简单一些，因为 Circle 发布完财报之后是出现了上涨。

但总体来说，这一份财报本身我认为没有到非常的好，但也没有非常的差：营收比市场预期要低，然后盈利比市场预期要高。

Arc 公链的战略意义

主要在他们这一次财报里边，他们发布了自己的一个 Arc——那这个公链本身我认为对于 Circle，其实在管理层的财报会议里边也有聊到，在第二季度财报的时候，他们将会公布这一条公链 Arc 对于 Circle 整体盈利上的这个影响就会有正面的这个影响。

那为什么 Arc 要去建这个公链呢？这其实非常的好理解。我给大家其实做了很多的一些比喻：你想一想 Circle 现在内部所有的一些产品，包括 USDC、包括它的一个 Stable FX、包括 CPN Network、包括它 CCTP。

如果我们把 USDC 比作为是一个货物，然后比如说 CPN 的话比作是一个物流的一个调度公司——你有了一个货物，你有了一个物流的一个调度公司，那很多时候我们需要一个高速公路。然后调度公司本身可以把这个货物从一端转移到另外一端，那这个高速公路现在是依赖于谁？现在是依赖于其他的一些区块链，比如说以太坊、Solana、Base，这些都是公链。

所以高速公路现在是依赖于其他的一些公链，但如果 Circle 想要更好地去服务他们的这些客户——因为他想要在传统金融这一块有一席之地嘛，所以现在有和很多的传统金融有之间的这个合同。还记不记得贝莱德投资了 10% Circle 的这个股份？我相信未来贝莱德在稳定币、包括稳定币的应用这一块的一些场景、包括未来所有资产上链这一块，贝莱德肯定有很多的一些想法。

所以 Circle 想要去服务像贝莱德这一类机构的话，他就必须要建自己的这个高速公路。通过和贝莱德、比如说和机构之间的这个交流，他们知道机构的这个需求，把机构的这个需求放入这条公链里面。那当 Circle 有了这条公链、有了自己的这个高速公路之后，就能够形成这个闭环了——就是自己既有货币、自己掌握了这个货物本身，也掌握了这个调度公司，也掌握了这条高速公路。

Arc 对 Circle 估值的双重影响

大家如果对于区块链不是非常了解的话，你可以理解为 Circle 内部自己建了一个公司，然后这个公司本身价值是 30 亿美金（three billion 点），然后 Circle 自己本身持股持了 25%，然后这个公司本身它未来所有的产品全部是服务于 Circle 现在内部所有的产品。

所以未来 Arc 对于 Circle 在营收上、在市值上的这个影响：

  • 营收上肯定是有正面的这个影响的。在第二季度财报里边，管理层会聊到从营收这个角度怎么给 Circle 现在的这些产品去赋能，这第一点就 other revenue 的其他收入这一块会有影响。
  • 估值上，因为 Arc 这条公链本身自己是有估值，它是 three billion；而 Circle 的估值现在也并没有那么的高。所以未来 Arc 这条公链，它的 market cap 如果上升的话，因为 Circle 掌握了类似于 25% 的这个股权，所以 Arc 这条公链如果它的一个价格上升的话，那 Circle 自己的这个估值也会随着 Arc 这条公链而上升。

有点类似于像你谷歌去投资下面投资了下公司、投资了 Waymo 啊。那好，那这么举吧——我在前段时间和大家聊过，亚马逊投资的 Anthropic，Anthropic 现在如果营收非常的好，它的估值往上升的话，没有道理——我说没有道理——亚马逊的估值应该往下跌的，对么。

当然亚马逊的体量起码现在比 Anthropic 还是大很多，那么而亚马逊也没有持有非常大或者 50%、60% Anthropic 这个股份，所以可能影响也是有限，但是一定是正面的这个影响。那但是 Circle 和 Arc 之间的这个关系，因为 Circle 现在的估值没有那么的高，而 Arc 的这个估值现在不低（相对来说不低），而且在区块链里面 3 个 billion 的这个项目其实没有那么的贵。那如果 Arc 这一条公链本身未来它这个估值往上升的话，也会把 Circle 的一个价格往上带。

所以你投资的 Circle，其实是投资了 Arc，好吧。

稳定币与"美元资产沉淀"

我认为今年下半年，随着 AI Agent 这一块的一些推出——当然 agent to agent 这一块的支付，我认为还有很长的一段路要走，我认为这条路还是挺长的，但是随着 agent 的这一块的落地，无论是苹果这一块的 Siri，还是谷歌这一块 agent 的一个落地——我认为对于 Circle 会是一个利好。

包括最重要的就是稳定币的这个落地。稳定币落地了之后，最近我不知道大家有没有关注社交媒体，最近一些大银行可能几天之前吧，社交媒体上就已经在聊了，很多大银行现在都已经开始招和 B 圈和 Crypto 相关的一些人员，那主要就是布局稳定币这一块。

那么另外一块就是怎么把资产上链。因为你想一下稳定币最终——我上次和大家举过这个例子——现在比如说 Meta 在 4 月底的时候，他们选择通过用 USDC 给哥伦比亚还有另外印度尼西亚啊（印尼吧）这两个国家的 creator 创造者去发花钱，就是去发这个激励。那发的激励的这个方式是通过 USDC。

但是如果是我来自于哥伦比亚，你发 USDC 给我，我能干吗？我啥事都干不了。但是如果美国这边它能够解决资产上链的话，那我用这个 USDC 余额就可以直接去投资了。也就是说，通过 USDC 发的这个钱发到其他国家的创造者手里，他的钱最终还是停留在美元资产上面——你停留在 USDC 就是对美债去背书，停留在美股也是对于美元资产去背书。

所以只要能够保证美元资产、你给了他一个应用场景，且这个钱最终是能够停留在美元资产上面，某种程度就是增加了全球对于美元的这个依赖性。所以很多时候我们在思考一个货币的时候、思考一个媒介的时候，我们必须要思考到整个的应用场景，因为只有应用场景越丰富，然后你这个媒介本身越有可能会被使用。

七、CEG 财报与电力 AI 投资逻辑

最后我们来聊一下 CEG。

大科技对电力的旺盛需求

那 CEG 的财报会里面没有非常多的一些重点。我主要就是要聊一下和宏观环境相关的，管理层有聊到数据中心这一块对于算力、对于电力这一块的一个需求非常的大。

整个 2026 年从 hyperscale，那么这些大科技、大客户大科技手里，对于电力这一块的一个需求同比增速是 75% 以上。所以大科技对于电力这一块的需求还是非常的高，且持续在增长。

估值分析与回购计划

看一下 CEG 的估值呢，聊到 CEG，很多人就问我"为什么买 CEG 不买 VST"。VST 也在我的观察列表里边，这两家公司我觉得我都是可以布局的。相对于 VST 来说，CEG 的估值是相对会高一些，但是即使会高一些，我认为现在去看的话也是合理的。

向后看呢，现在当下的一个 PE 吧是在 29 倍，那未来三年 EPS 这个增速 23、7 和 24，平均下来 20 嘛，差不多 1.4 倍、1.3 倍左右的一个 PEG ratio。

特别是从现金流这个角度去看：26 年 15 倍、27 年 13 倍、28 年 11 倍。在这一次财报会议里面，管理层还给出了 26 年、27 年和 28 年，包括 28 年接下去现金流这一块的一个预期，然后也公布了这个回购的一个计划。

在 6 月 30 底吧，因为他们这一次一个收购又增发了一些、又给了一些股份，那这一部分第一部分一半的这个股份在 6 月 30 解禁了。所以今年的 6 月 30 之后，可能会有一部分的一个抛压，那么但是管理层也给了这一部分的一个回购计划。所以有可能 CEG 还会有更好的这个买点，或者会有一个加仓点，所以耐心地等一下。

从估值的这一个角度去看，那现在是在 24 倍的这个估值呢，基本上我这边画了一条红线，红线是在 25 倍左右了。那从历史上、过去两三年去看的话，25 倍以下基本上都会是一个比较好的一个进场点。

三大投资理由

那我选择 CEG、包括未来有机会的话布局 VST，主要是 3 点：

第一点，他们还是受到 AI 利好的这个影响。起码是电力这一块，电力在美国仍然是短缺的，所以这是一个比较长期的这个利好。那未来 AI 这一块有任何下行的一些风险，电力这一块我认为下行的风险受到这个影响相对会小一些。就有点类似于你整个芯片行业，你越上游，你越受到周期的这个影响就会越小一些，因为上游是趋近于一些长期的布局，那电力这一块比较长期的这个布局。那这是第一块，也是受到 AI 的这个利好的一个影响。

第二块，相对来说相对于你走在 AI 前沿的这些硬件公司来说，它的波动会小一些，它的 Beta 相对来说会低一些。仍然是受到 AI 的、吃到了 AI 的这个红利，但是它波动会小一些，那么仍然是 AI 这一块的布局，波动小。

第三点是在 3 月份的时候，老黄接受采访的时候，他聊到他说接下去他要解决的一个问题就是电网的一个使用率。他希望电网的使用率可以上升——电网现在的使用率在 60% 以下。因为电网的设计本身它是要保证在所有的时刻里面，大家想用电就得用电，所以才会有高峰期和低峰期。但是如果在低峰期的时候，能够用到这些电网、能够提供出来这些电，比如说像 CEG 和 VST，它都可以在 off peak 的时候、在低峰的时候可以给数据中心去提供额外的一些电力。

所以老黄下一代他对于 AI Factory（AI 工厂）的一个理念就是：AI 工厂对于电力的这个需求不是持续地保持一个 GW 或者 500 MW，而是这个电网的一个提供、就是这个电力的一个提供，它可以是浮动的。那等到 Rubin 推出之后、下一代的软件的架构出来之后，我认为无论是 CEG 也好、VST 也好，这两家公司都会获益的。

好吧，主要是出于这三个方向的一个思考。

八、收尾与近期表现

那今天我做了可能过去两个多月以来第一次的一个减仓。减仓完之后，那么其他仓位的一些占比现在到底是多少呢，大家如果想知道的话，好奇的话，可以登录我这个 App 可以去看一下我所有仓位的这个公开。

那今年 YTD 到现在为止，因为今天下跌了，今天最后是下跌了 2% 点几吧，所以还是不错的，我还能够做到 38%，我个人还是非常的满意。

那整个 App 里面，现在有一个 AI 的机器人是无线问答的，你 24 小时都可以提问。所有我关于的持仓、包括我的一些观点，他都会基本上原文复述我以前对于任何标的的一些看法，最新加入这个延爆功能，然后有聊到了 NewCloud 他们一些估值的一些方法。

那么下载的一个方式是通过右下角的这个二维码，扫描这个二维码就可以进行下载。现在正在搞的这个优惠的一个活动是到 5 月 14 日，然后基本上还有两天不到的一个时间，大家如果感兴趣的话可以去试一下。包括里面还有免费的一些功能：Beta 计算器、市场的一个参与度，在里面都有。Beta 计算器可以用来控制整体投资组合的这个风险。好吧，建议大家有时间的话可以去试一下，如果感兴趣的话可以进行订阅。

那今天这期内容到这边，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：这次"AI泡沫论"其实是一场认知错位
主持人触及了一个核心点，但没有把它推到极致——我来推：**这一轮AI周期最特殊的不是技术爆发，而是"金主结构"的根本性反转**。
2000年的科技泡沫，资本开支来自借债扩张的电信公司和初创企业（现金流脆弱）；而这一轮的Capex来自微软、谷歌、Meta、亚马逊——**它们是地球上现金流质量最好的资产**。这意味着芯片/光模块/存储的下游需求方，本质上是"防御性资产"。
这就形成了一个奇特的金融学错位：**进攻性最强的板块（AI芯片），其需求护城河来自最防御性的现金流**。这是历史上罕见的"攻防嵌套"结构，传统估值框架根本没办法处理它——因为传统模型假设"高增长=高风险"，但现在是"高增长 by 低风险金主"。
## 二、跨领域连接：Circle的"建公路"逻辑 = 19世纪铁路 + Amazon AWS + 谷歌DeepMind
主持人聊到Circle自建公链Arc时给了一个货物/物流/高速公路的比喻，但更深的隐喻是：**Circle正在重演"标准石油"的纵向整合策略**——只是介质从原油变成了美元流动性。

item.title: [Lex Clips] How controversy on Twitter/X helps solve problems | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、表面是"网络喷子"，本质是"信号放大器"——开源世界的权力悖论

最反直觉的观察是：FFmpeg 和 VLC 这类支撑全球数字基础设施的项目，竟然只能通过"在社交平台上制造戏剧性冲突"来获得最基本的对话权。这看似是 X 平台的胜利，实则暴露出一个荒诞的真相——一个被 100 million 用户使用、跻身 Windows 前十的软件，竟然在 Microsoft 内部连一个"联系人"都没有。

这里有一条隐藏的权力公式：当正式渠道（ISV 项目、合作伙伴通道）按"商业体量"分配注意力时，开源项目必须用"舆论体量"来对冲。换句话说，spicy tweet 不是情绪化的产物，而是一种精确计算过的非对称武器——它把"被忽视的成本"从开源维护者身上，转嫁到了平台公司的公关部门。这其实是一种"以喧嚣换沉默"的反向交易。

二、跨领域连接：开源维护者 ≈ 海洋中的"鲸落"

在生态学里有一个概念叫"鲸落"——一头鲸鱼死后能维持深海生态系统几十年。FFmpeg 也是类似的奇观：一个几乎"沉没在水面之下"的项目，养活了整个上层视频生态（YouTube、Netflix、TikTok、剪映……），但它本身却连一个全职开发者的工资都凑不齐。

更深的悖论在这里：越底层、越关键的基础设施，越难变现。这与经济学中的"鲍莫尔成本病"（Baumol's cost disease）正好相反——不是成本病，而是"价值蒸发病"：你创造的价值越是被广泛吸收、越是无缝渗透到上层，你能截获的那部分就越少。Tim Sweeney、Carmack、Karpathy 在 X 上的呼喊，本质上是在对抗这种"价值蒸发"的物理定律。

三、最颠覆性的洞察：14 岁的孩子，正在重写"成人世界"的合法性

访谈里轻描淡写地提到 Felix 16 岁、Edward Wong 14 岁、Ruikai Peng 16 岁——但请把这条线索放大：全球最关键的视频基础设施，其底层汇编代码的相当一部分，是青少年写的。而与此同时，许多大公司的"安全研究员"在做什么？——给开发中（未发布）的代码挂上 CVE 8.0 高危标签来博取曝光。

这里出现了一个惊人的代际错位： - 体制内的成年专业人士 → 玩"绩效叙事"（CVE 热度、KPI、公关） - 体制外的青少年贡献者 → 玩"真实工程"（3 天修掉 bug，不发新闻稿）

跨领域连接：这与学术界的"复制危机"结构同构——专业体制激励了戏剧性发现而非沉默修复。FFmpeg 的故事其实在揭示一个更广的规律：当一个领域的"信用经济"开始奖励噪音，真正的工作就会向边缘人群（青少年、匿名贡献者、退休工程师）迁移。Andreas Rheinhardt 在飞机上重建飞机的隐喻，恰恰是工业文明里"不可见劳动"（invisible labor）问题的技术翻版——和女性家务劳动、护士照护劳动、维基百科编辑工作享有同一个结构。

啊哈时刻：FFmpeg 的"spicy tweet 策略"不是数字时代的新现象，而是一种古老的、属于"无权者"的政治技艺——它和 18 世纪伦敦的小册子写作、20 世纪初的劳工罢工传单，遵循同一条规则：当你没有正式发言权时，你必须制造无法被忽视的噪音。X 平台只是把这种技艺压缩到了 280 字符。

📝 转录文本

一、Spicy Tweets 带来的实际成果

We should say that some of the spicy tweets that you're carrying your behind, and some of the debacle, produced results — positive results.

Donations have increased substantially. They're still not enough to cover even a single full-time developer, but on both, you know, awareness level and a technical level, there's substantially more technical awareness — and sort of awareness of the importance of FFmpeg — as a result of X and what's happened.

I can say, you know, it serves its purpose. People realize the level of importance FFmpeg has.

二、VideoLAN 的同样困境：VLC on Android 与 Windows

And on VideoLAN it's the same, right? Like, for example, a very simple example —

[sighs]

— for more than a year we couldn't update VLC on Android because of a bug on the Android Play Store, right? The only way we got someone to answer was to put a very spicy, as you say, tweet saying that we were going to stop distributing VLC for Android. And we have around 100 million people using that. And then someone from Android actually came and discussed it with us, right?

We had the same issue with Microsoft — like saying that we were going to stop distributing VLC on the Windows Store. And unfortunately, we are so small that the only very strong power we have to solve those issues is blaming on social network, because it snowballs and now they listen to us.

三、为什么大公司不和开源项目对话

But so, these large companies often have difficulty talking to us. Like, for example, VLC is probably one of the top 10 software used on Windows. I am not part of Microsoft ISV programs —

[snorts]

— right? I don't have a point of contact at Microsoft, right? Well, I'm sure any other software — Adobe, Spotify — has a point of contact. I don't have that.

So, raising awareness works. It's sometimes very spicy, a lot of drama. X and Twitter are okay for that, but it's efficient.

四、呼吁支持与致谢

So, everybody listening to this should go follow FFmpeg on Twitter / on X. Follow VideoLAN on Twitter / on X. Go donate. Donate to FFmpeg.

And thank you, Lex. Over the years, several years, you've been a supporter of, you know, FFmpeg and VideoLAN on X. You're giving us shout-outs, appreciating, you know, what we do. FFmpeg for life.

And for example, like Tim Sweeney, Carmack, and a few others — like very high-level people — have raised also the awareness on our X accounts, and that helped a lot also. Karpathy as well, yeah.

五、开源项目作为文化与工程的典范

Yeah, I mean also, you know, outside of the fact that so many people use it, it's so impactful on the world — it's also a great representation of a great open source project. Like, the value of assembly and C, and making sure that you take programming seriously for real-world systems.

It's not just that. We'll talk about assembly later, I'm sure, 'cause that's a whole topic in itself — but it's also celebrating people like Andreas Rheinhardt who do maintenance. It is, I believe, unpaid. I believe, as a volunteer, he's doing massive refactorings.

Andreas Rheinhardt and Anton Khirnov — of rewriting FFmpeg.c with threading. Celebrating those guys. Celebrating the untold labor that's gone into this that actually doesn't change anything from the user standpoint. The files are exactly the same, but — wow — the airplane has been rebuilt while it's in the air.

六、关于"青少年贡献者"的回应

Christian Garcia said "there's a teenager running this account", referring to the FFmpeg account, and you responded:

"Teenagers have written more assembly in FFmpeg than Google engineers."

But also just pointing out that there's a lot of incredible contributors who are teenagers. Like JB said, we don't care who you are, where you're from, what you do. Teenagers have written thousands of lines of assembly over the years.

Give a shout-out back in the day to Daniel Kang. So, also highlighting the work of people like Ruikai Peng — this is a 16-year-old. Some of his first contributions to FFmpeg — actually doing, and putting some of these quote-unquote "security researchers" to shame by actually finding issues and fixing them, and being 16.

七、没有门槛的世界级技术贡献

There's no barriers. There's no barriers — you don't have to study at college under this person and understand these. You can learn C — and let's be honest, it's from the K&R book. Learn C. You can learn assembly. We'll talk about that maybe a bit later. You can contribute to world-class technologies.

In VLC, one of the oldest contributors is called Felix. He's the one doing everything on Mac and iOS. He started working on VLC when he was 16.

We had a guy called Edward Wang who used to be a Google Summer of Code student, who stayed for three years around VideoLAN. He was 14, right? Part of Google Summer of Code and Google Code-in — programs where basically we have students or high schoolers. We wrote a ton of assembly for x264, and for VLC, and for FFmpeg.

So, everyone can contribute.

八、反对"安全戏剧化"：扎实修复 vs CVE 炒作

And he also did a good job, because he didn't play the alarmist — "CVE heist", create a CVE, which is like a public exposure of security — and put these big scary red 7.5 high-priority labels. He just fixed an issue in Git after three days and just fixed it. He didn't need to go and play a big security drama about it.

And I think I posted, you know, "the kids are all right", whereas —

[laughter]

— there's, you know, I'm not saying all security people do this, but there is a portion of the security community, as Alex said, that likes to hype themselves up by creating drama. They would have happily raised — this is a high priority CVE 8.0 or whatever — on an issue that actually was in Git. It wasn't even in a release; it was in development, and three days later it was fixed.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、表面是"网络喷子"，本质是"信号放大器"——开源世界的权力悖论
最反直觉的观察是：**FFmpeg 和 VLC 这类支撑全球数字基础设施的项目，竟然只能通过"在社交平台上制造戏剧性冲突"来获得最基本的对话权**。这看似是 X 平台的胜利，实则暴露出一个荒诞的真相——一个被 100 million 用户使用、跻身 Windows 前十的软件，竟然在 Microsoft 内部连一个"联系人"都没有。
这里有一条隐藏的权力公式：**当正式渠道（ISV 项目、合作伙伴通道）按"商业体量"分配注意力时，开源项目必须用"舆论体量"来对冲**。换句话说，spicy tweet 不是情绪化的产物，而是一种**精确计算过的非对称武器**——它把"被忽视的成本"从开源维护者身上，转嫁到了平台公司的公关部门。这其实是一种"以喧嚣换沉默"的反向交易。
## 二、跨领域连接：开源维护者 ≈ 海洋中的"鲸落"
在生态学里有一个概念叫"鲸落"——一头鲸鱼死后能维持深海生态系统几十年。FFmpeg 也是类似的奇观：**一个几乎"沉没在水面之下"的项目，养活了整个上层视频生态（YouTube、Netf

item.title: [Lex Clips] Regret is a tax on your mind - I regret nothing | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"后悔是大脑的税" —— 一个被误读的认知经济学公式

表面上，这是一句励志金句："别后悔，向前看。"但真正惊人的是说话者使用的隐喻——"tax"（税）。税的本质不是损失，而是强制性的、持续性的、无法协商的抽成。这意味着说话者把后悔识别为一种自动从认知带宽中扣除的资源税，而不是情绪。

这里藏着一个反直觉的观察：大多数人以为"不后悔"是一种道德选择或心理韧性，但说话者把它降维成了资源管理问题。后悔之所以该避免，不是因为它"不好"，而是因为它没有 ROI——除非你有时间机器。这是一个工程师式的、近乎冷酷的功利主义框架，恰恰来自 FFmpeg/VLC 的开发者——一群一辈子都在与"计算资源约束"打交道的人。他们处理情绪的方式，和处理 CPU 周期的方式是同构的。

二、隐藏的跨领域连接：FFmpeg 哲学 × 斯多葛主义 × 法语香颂

让我建立一个出人意料的三角连接：

  • Édith Piaf 的 "Je ne regrette rien"（1960）——战后法国的情感解药
  • 斯多葛主义的 "dichotomy of control"（爱比克泰德）——只关心你能控制的
  • FFmpeg 的设计哲学——只处理输入流，不回溯已写入的字节

三者结构完全一致：过去 = 不可变（immutable）；现在 = 唯一可写区域。

更妙的是，VLC/FFmpeg 处理视频流的核心抽象就是"流不能倒带，只能 seek"——而 seek 在底层往往是重新解码，代价昂贵。说话者无意识中用了他构建二十年的软件架构来描述他的人生哲学。他没有在比喻，他在描述一个他亲手实现过的数据结构。这就是为什么 Johnny Depp 那句"hate is expensive"会让他立即共鸣——同一种思维：情绪 = 操作 = 有成本。

三、最让我意外的观察：感激是反向的税收

整段对话以"regret is a tax"开始，却以连续四次"grateful / eternally grateful / thank you"结束。如果你接受"后悔是认知税"这个公式，那么感激（gratitude）在数学上就是它的反函数——一种持续给大脑分红的认知投资。

说话者没有明说，但他的语言行为暴露了一个完整的情绪资产负债表： - 负债侧：regret、hate（昂贵情绪 → 删除） - 资产侧：gratitude、joy、特殊感（"I feel like a wizard"）→ 复利

这里有一个更深的悖论：那些声称"不后悔"的人，往往比常人更频繁地表达感激。为什么？因为如果不后悔是一种"删除操作"，那么大脑必须填充被释放的认知空间——感激就是最低能耗的填充物。这解释了一个普遍现象：斯多葛主义者、长期开源贡献者、极简主义者，几乎都共享一种"过度表达感激"的语言习惯。这不是巧合，这是认知系统的能量守恒。

那顶让他"feel like a wizard"的帽子，本质上是同一笔交易的物理凭证——用一个微小的当下符号，换走整段对话里所有可能残留的认知负债。

📝 转录文本

关于后悔的提问

Lex： Uh, any any tweets uh carrying you regret? Any tweets I regret?

嘉宾： Or is it like that — well, how's the French song go? Regret nothing. Je ne regrette rien. Yes, that's very important for me, right? Don't regret anything.

"后悔是大脑的税"

No, it's because regrets are attacks on your mind, right? So, learn from your mistakes, but don't regret. Because you've done it. So, except if you have a time machine to go back in time. Don't regret, right? It's going to just tax your brain. Learn from your mistake, sure. Don't regret.

[snorts]

与 Johnny Depp 的呼应

It's like — it reminds me, it's beautiful, "to tax in your brain." Reminds me of the Johnny Depp quote I saw where he was saying hate, you know, I don't hate. That's — hate is a very expensive emotion.

[laughter]

Are you comparing me to Johnny Depp? Because that would be your first one.

结尾的感谢

Well, gentlemen, like I said, I'm internally grateful for the the software that the two of you and the bigger community have been part of building — with FFmpeg and VLC and everything else. I'm eternally grateful for the spicy tweets. Never stop.

And I'm grateful that you would talk with me today and give me this sexy hat. I feel like a wizard. I feel special. Um, and I feel special to get a chance to talk and celebrate the piece of software that brought me so much joy over the years.

So, thank you for everything, and thank you for talking to me today.

嘉宾： Thank you for having me. Thank you so much.

观看原视频 →

表面上，这是一句励志金句："别后悔，向前看。"但真正惊人的是说话者使用的隐喻——**"tax"（税）**。税的本质不是损失，而是**强制性的、持续性的、无法协商的抽成**。这意味着说话者把后悔识别为一种**自动从认知带宽中扣除的资源税**，而不是情绪。

- **Édith Piaf 的 "Je ne regrette rien"**（1960）——战后法国的情感解药 - **斯多葛主义的 "dichotom

item.title: [All-In Podcast] How We Grew Koch Industries to $150 Billion Without Going Public: Charles & Chase Koch
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：科赫的"私有性"本身就是一种产品

表面上，科赫工业是"没有上市的最大私营公司"。但真正的洞见是——他们的私有结构不是结果，而是核心产品。

注意 Charles 说的那句话："如果我们上市，没有人会相信这个故事。"这暴露了一个深层悖论：他们最有价值的资产是"不被市场理解的权利"。

公众市场本质上是一台叙事压缩机——它要求每个企业把自己压缩成一句话（"我们是石油公司"、"我们是消费品公司"），以便分析师能定价。一旦你被压缩，你就必须生活在那个标签里。Georgia Pacific 在做木业时市盈率只有 6 倍，因为市场只能用"商品"这一个标签理解它。

科赫拒绝压缩。"能力边界 vs. 行业边界" 这个区分听起来像管理学口号，但它真正的含义是：他们买下了"模糊性"的版权。在一个所有人都被迫清晰自我定义的世界里，"无法被简单描述" 本身成了护城河。

二、跨领域连接：科赫的组织模型 ≈ 进化生物学中的"亲缘选择"

Chase 反复强调他们不是 Berkshire Hathaway（独立运营、互不相干），而是 "a republic of science——能力的整合集合"。

这让我想起威廉·汉密尔顿的亲缘选择理论（kin selection）：基因不是通过个体存活传播的，而是通过与自己相似的拷贝传播的。科赫的"能力"就是它们的基因——纺织塔设计能力、商品交易能力、品牌运营能力——这些"基因" 不归属于任何一家子公司，而是漂浮在整个企业生态系统中，遇到合适的"宿主行业"就嫁接过去。

所以为什么 Joe Lamont 的学校项目、Scott Strode 的健身房戒瘾、KDT 的硅谷投资、Molex 的连接器制造，看起来毫无关联却都"属于科赫"？因为它们都是同一组底层算法在不同基质中的表达。这就是为什么 Chase 说"全公司 13 万人若都深度理解'比较优势'这一条原则，会发生什么？"——他在描述的不是管理，而是让模因（meme）替代命令在组织中传播。

更惊人的连接：Charles 引用 Polanyi 的《Personal Knowledge》谈"重新连接大脑神经"，并说"你的大脑是身体的一部分，要像练马拉松一样改造它"。他实际上在描述组织级别的神经可塑性——不是改造个人，而是改造一个 13 万人的分布式神经网络。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：科赫的"失败崇拜"实际上是反熵机制

Charles 自嘲式地讲了无数失败故事——气体到面包战略、猪饲料合同、激进交易、活性炭项目……几乎所有听众都把这理解为"谦逊的智者讲教训"。

但真正的机制是：他们用失败来对抗组织熵增。

任何组织在规模化后都会自动滑向"保守均衡"——Chase 一针见血地指出：中层管理者的理性选择就是"做最不可能失败的事，保住工作、领奖金、回家"。这是组织热力学第二定律：没有外部扰动的系统必然走向最低能态。

科赫的"实验性发现"原则，配合"按未来贡献而非短期 KPI 奖励"的薪酬结构，本质上是人为注入熵的反向流。他们花钱购买失败，就像活细胞花 ATP 维持低熵状态。

这也解释了一个奇怪现象：为什么他们坚持留在 Wichita。David 把这归为"避免硅谷的单一文化"，但更深层的原因是——地理隔离本身就是反熵装置。在曼哈顿或硅谷，社会模仿（Charles 用了 social mimicry 这个词）的拉力太强，组织会被迫向均值回归。Wichita 是他们的法拉第笼。

四、最值得读者警惕的一个未明说的张力

Charles 用 Viktor Frankl 解释社会问题：当人失去意义，就转向权力或快感。但他自己的人生轨迹——50 年回避主流政治，最近 10 年深度卷入——恰好是这个框架本身的一个尴尬数据点：当"通过商业改造世界"不够时，他也走向了"权力"路径，尽管包装成"原则驱动的政策"。

他自己承认了这一点（"通过单一政党去做是错误"），但没说透：Stand Together 的本质是把企业内部的"自下而上赋权"原则反向投射到社会层面。这是一个未经验证的跨尺度迁移——在 13 万员工的封闭系统里有效的原则，能否在 3 亿人的开放社会里复制？这是他在 90 岁仍未解决、也不会承认未解决的根本问题。

📝 转录文本

开场

What an honor to be here. Thank you for hosting us Forbes and welcome. This will be put out as the all-in interview, so I'm really excited to share this conversation with everyone on the world, on the internet, and to get some time with Charles Koch, Chase Koch.

Chase and I have known each other since 2013 when we overlapped in the agriculture industry, got to know each other. We've been business partners. And Charles and I have gotten to know each other a few times over the years. But I'm really excited for this conversation tonight. So Charles, thank you for being here.

Thanks for having us. It's an honor.

♪ I'm going all in ♪

赞助商插播：Axon.ai

Every few years, a new ad channel opens before the market catches on. That's Axon.ai right now. The AI ad platform behind one of the biggest runs in tech with access to over a billion daily active users. Full screen video ads in mobile games watched for a median of 35 seconds. Businesses are profitably spending hundreds of thousands of dollars a day on it. And most advertisers don't even know it exists yet. The window is open at Axon.ai slash all in.

All in.

一、被低估的商业故事：科赫工业的全貌

In Silicon Valley, entrepreneurs and even mature company CEOs always like to learn about the story of other businesses and the success of those businesses. And I've always felt like Koch Industries was that untold story — probably the most profitable private family-owned business in the world. Maybe I'm off on a couple points, but certainly up there. And one of the most impressive business stories because of the evolution of the business, which I'm hopeful we can hear a little bit about how that evolution came to be tonight.

And just for some statistics, if Coke were publicly traded, the revenue would put it easily in the top 25 of the Fortune 500. It's a family-owned business based out of Wichita, founded in 1940 by Fred Koch, with businesses ranging from energy, agriculture, chemicals, building products, consumer products, even cloud computing. And a very active minority investment portfolio with 120,000 plus employees. That statistic might be off across 60 countries.

Very unique operating model, which we'll get into today, including principles around disruptive innovation of the business, reinvesting 90% of profits in new businesses and growth, meritocratic values. And I'm hopeful that tonight we can take an opportunity to hear about the evolution of the business and talk about some of those principles.

And maybe we can get started, Charles, if you could give us a sense of the scale of the business. What are the business lines that you operate today? And maybe, you know, provide a little more color to those high level statistics I shared today.

I can go back through some of the history and the failures and successes, but I'll go through what we've grown since the early 1960s. And then we had 300 employees. Now we have more than 130,000 in 60 countries. And we have increased in value 9,000 times over that period.

二、Charles 的早年：从 MIT 到接手家族企业

When did you join the business?

  1. Full-time, I'd been working… Well, my father, we lived on a farm, and he told me at age six he didn't want me to be a country club bum. So he made me work in all my spare time, which I hated, and so I was always in trouble. And so he was kind of tough on me, rightfully so. And thank God he did. Years later, I asked him, "Pop, why were you… so much tougher on me than you were on my younger brothers?" And he said, "Son, you plumb wore me out."

When you came into the business, what was the scope of the business? What was the business operating?

We had two main businesses. One was to design and make fractionating trays — that is, they separate liquids by differences in boiling points. And then our largest business was a crude oil gathering system in Oklahoma.

And so my father — and I was, I finished just a few years earlier, finished MIT, and I was working for Arthur D. Little, then a leading consulting firm. And I was, and you'll think this is a joke, at age 25 I was doing management consulting. I have to laugh at the absurdity of that. But they were paying me for it, believe it or not.

So my father called me and he said, "Son, I want you to come back and join the business." And as tough as he had been on me, and as I say rightly so, I declined. So he called me a few weeks later and he said, "Son, either you come back to run the company or I'm going to have to sell it because my health is bad and the companies aren't doing well and I don't have long to live."

So I agreed because — well, for a number of reasons. One, the first one is I got three degrees at MIT in engineering and I sucked as an engineer. I mean get that — and what? So how'd you get through MIT? Because I was real good at the math and the science and the theory and I was no good at making or operating things. So I figured out pretty quickly that I wasn't going to make it as an engineer so I need to be an entrepreneur — and because I was good at principles. And that's why we led. So I was always looking for principles that would help me contribute and succeed. And that's what transformed our company.

三、第一次大改造：纺织塔业务

So you come into the business, a couple hundred employees, you said?

300 employees.

And how did you think — was the mandate to grow the business? Was it just to keep it stable?

No, no, it was. Could I take a few minutes and go through those first two businesses?

Your show, I'm in. That sounds great.

Okay, the first one was making fractionating trays, designing those. We had a president then who was one of our principles you don't want to be. The negative is top-down and obsessed with controlling everybody. So he would send out memos every week demanding on what they spent, how did you spend it on, what did you do, did you do this right? So they were frustrated. As a matter of fact, they started ignoring him.

And then the whole culture was protectionist. That is, when they sold the internals for a fractionating tower, they wouldn't tell them the design. "Well, we need to know the design so we can correct it." No, they wouldn't give it to them.

And then what's even worse, to satisfy the European market, they didn't even build a plant there. They had multiple subcontractors do parts of a tray and then bring them all together and assemble with another contractor. Now you can imagine how that was for speed and cost. So we were losing our ass, if you can use the expression.

And so I changed the management and changed the philosophy. Okay, the first thing we're going to focus on is creating value for our customers. And then the second thing, we're going to empower our employees so they want to do this. And the third thing, we're going to do a plant — we're going to build a plant in Italy to satisfy the European market. We're going to do it all ourselves. And so we became profitable. And then we started adding related products.

四、25 岁的判断力从何而来？

Can I ask a question? You come in at 25 plus or minus a little bit and you see the problems at the business. It's not profitable, it's not being well managed. And you overturn the management team. How did you have the confidence at this age coming with the experience you had to take that level of action that quickly?

Well, it was life or death. And my father said, "You can run this business any way you want. The only thing you need my approval on is to sell." That's the way he talked me into coming back after I said I didn't want to or I wasn't going to.

And then in 1970, what really helped is my brother, younger brother David, joined the business and then he continued that growth.

五、能力边界 vs. 行业边界：科赫的核心范式

And then you're now running a profitable operation. You've got a European business. And at that point, did you start to think about expanding into other products and other…

Well, that's it. And this is — so I was learning all these different principles. And what I saw we were doing, not just here but in other things, is we were building capabilities. That is, I looked at it, we need to be capability-bounded, not industry-bounded.

Like, okay, you could say to a certain extent, because we were in crude oil gathering, we're in the oil industry. Oh, that means everybody would say you need to be an integrated oil company. You need to be in everything. And I was applying division of labor by comparative advantage. No, you need to be in the part of the industry, in the part of the value chain where you can create more value than others, otherwise you're going to fail. And that's what we're seeing happening now. There's more specialization by comparative advantage.

And so I started this, created this principle called Creating Virtuous Cycles of Mutual Benefit. And what that led us to do is to start this never-ending cycle of growth, innovation, success, and failures — failures that, when we did it right, that we learned from and made us better and taught us better how to apply principles to create value. And we're still going through that. We have a lot of failures and that's when you apply creative destruction in your new things. If you're not failing at everything, you're not doing anything new.

六、从失败中学习

Where did you learn that lesson? So what was the first major failure that — they always say you've got to plan until you get punched in the face. What was the first punch in the face?

Well, I had a bunch of them with that company. It was called Koch Engineering then. And like I said, okay, when we got into refining, we created petroleum coke. So I said, well, let's come up with a way to use that as a base to make activated carbon. And that was — we spent a fair amount of money on that. And I just, we had a whole bunch of those. And we've had many more.

How did you make the decision to shut it down or walk away? At some point, a lot of entrepreneurs have this problem — they build something, they're too in love with it, and they don't know when to say enough is enough.

Yeah, well, when enough is enough — when we lose our ass enough. No, it's when we decide we don't have the capability to create superior value for our customers and that we're going to be rewarded for.

And sometimes it can be the structure of a business — like a company that Chase founded, Koch Disruptive Technology, Insight Tech. It does tremendous things, but it is a structure that makes it hard to make it profitable. And so that's another — these are principles that we've learned. Okay, we didn't apply that. What were the principles that we didn't apply that caused us to fail? That's what I mean — we learned from failure.

七、今天的业务版图

So the businesses where — you asked the businesses we're in now, and Jason, there's Coke people here, you all can catch me up if I miss any, but we have:

  • Engineering projects, engineering construction. We build solar plants.
  • Commodity trading and distribution.
  • Fertilizers.
  • Refined products.
  • Chemicals and polymers.
  • Glass.
  • Forest and consumer products.
  • Four different investment firms with different comparative advantages.
  • Electrical products.
  • Software systems for management.

Dave, let me just hit one point. Did I miss anything? No, you got it. You did a great job.

八、Chase 的总结：从原油到帝国的逻辑

Basically, like eight wholly owned business unit platforms that he described and then four investment different businesses. But I just wanted to kind of really drill a point home — because when I came out and when I started really hanging out with you and the whole tech community and trying to build that network, a lot of people had the same question that you did about who is Coke, what are you guys all about? I know it's a large private business, but being in Wichita, we don't know that much about it.

I think this point that is so different about Coke versus almost any other company out there is what my father said on being capability-bounded, not industry-bounded. And how do you get from a small crude oil gathering company in southern Oklahoma to all of those businesses that he described? There's the principles obviously throughout, which we'll be talking about in this discussion, but one of the absolute core differences is that whole approach to capabilities.

And I would encourage anyone that's in a business and trying to scale, think about it from that lens. What capabilities have I demonstrated that I can add value to customers? And then point it at new industries where I can experiment. This is one of our principles as well — experimental discovery. Not trying to do everything at once and trying to conquer the world, but experiment and test: does the customer value my product or not?

And then along the way — you know, those core capabilities for us started off as operations, logistics, trading. In the very early days of Coke, that's what we demonstrated we were good at. We were getting great customer feedback. But then when we have the capability approach to say, "Okay, we started in energy, we started in crude oil gathering at pipelines and refineries. Can we point those same capabilities into natural gas? Can we point those into chemicals? Let's experiment there. Can we point those into fertilizers?"

Because then we learned about natural gas. And then the Georgia Pacific opportunity comes along. And it's like, hey, these are wood products. It doesn't seem similar to these other businesses, but it's the same core capabilities. We buy Georgia Pacific and along the way it was somewhat of a happy accident that we started learning about consumer products and branding. So branding became a new capability for Coke through acquisition, but it started with: where do we think we can add value and do a good job on that and collect new capabilities along the way.

So I think that's like a really simple way to think about Coke and how we're different over the course of time.

九、不是 Berkshire Hathaway：a Republic of Science

One other thing I'll mention too — because I've been asked many times — it's like, "Well, so is it sort of like a Berkshire Hathaway where you have all these different businesses, a conglomerate?" And I would say no. I mean, obviously Warren Buffett and his team have done an unbelievable job operating the business the way they have, but we think about our business very differently. Instead of operating them all as independent businesses and almost like in silos, think about it as a republic of science. We're not a conglomerate. We're an integrated set of capabilities.

Was it fair to say that you wouldn't consider an acquisition or a new business line if there wasn't some relatedness to an existing competency at the company?

It depends. I mean, as you see when you read the book, we went through one chapter on creative destruction and what Schumpeter called all the different ways to do that. And one is to create a new management approach. Okay, that's our biggest one.

So the question is when we bought Molex — it makes electrical connectors — which has done fantastic. And at first it wasn't doing great so we said, we think if we can get them to apply these principles it will turn them around. And the problem when we do that, the tendency is to learn the lingo and so you can call everything by these names and you still do what you always did. And that's what was going on there. And so finally we changed the management and once we did that and they started applying these principles, they took off and now they're knocking it out of the park.

十、最糟糕的失败：价值观与领导力

But let me go back to failures, because we're understating our great strength in failures. And that is, I'll give you our worst failures and what caused it. And it was caused by violating the principle of hiring people first on values and second on talent.

And for years I've told people, look, if you want to hire somebody with bad values because you like them or something, hire them slow and stupid. And so we can catch them real quick and get them the hell out. Maybe get them to go to work for our competitors or something. Maybe help them get a job.

But anyway, that was huge. And then we made that even worse by taking people who had terrible values and made them leaders. And so what we call that is — rather than we want everybody in the company to be contribution motivated, that "I want to succeed by contributing, I want to be rewarded for my contributions, not for anything else — the value I create for our customers or for the future." Some of these people were destructively motivated. What they wanted was power or control, and they would hide their failures and make up their successes.

So I'll give you two examples. One goes back to 1973. You remember the war in the Middle East and everything. And they had gotten us into all kinds of wild, reckless trades. So that could have bankrupted the company.

And then later — because, I mean, much later, so shows you that repetition penetrates even the dullest of minds. So I needed this to happen a bunch of times. So finally, okay, I got it. I got it. God, don't punish me anymore, please, for my stupid mistakes.

So this was — we did it about the same time, our ag group. We put leaders in who were destructively motivated. And in refining, we got a leader, and they were destroying those businesses. And it almost wiped out all of Koch's earnings in the late 1990s.

十一、Chase 详解："Gas-to-Bread Spread" 灾难

You'll appreciate this, being an ag guy. To go a little deeper on what happened in the late 90s in our ag business, we called it a strategy — the gas-to-bread spread strategy. So we wanted to basically be in every element of the value chain, all the way from pulling the natural gas out of the ground, converting it into fertilizer, making the nitrogen products to grow the crops that would ultimately then end up on the grocery store shelves and be in bread. We got in pizza crusts, all this crazy stuff. When you look back on it, you're like, what the hell are you doing? But it was what he was saying — leadership thinking about, "We can do anything, and if we could basically kind of control the entire value chain, we could make that successful."

Completely violates probably all 41 principles in the book, right? Experimental discovery, knowing where your capabilities are, right people, right roles. And so, yeah, we called it the gas-to-bread spread. Some people called it the ass-to-bread spread too.

And there's another one in there — integrity. Because when they knew there were losses in some of these, and then they wouldn't tell us, they wanted to go ahead anyway. We had a deal within that, you know, like Purina dog food. So one of the things that was acquired was the large animal Purina feed — mainly hog feed — and did no diligence. And this is one of our principles: to apply the scientific method, so disprove your hypothesis as much as you try to prove it.

And so we closed that acquisition and within days we found out that we had hundreds of millions of out-of-the-money hog contracts. We didn't even look at the contracts.

So, I mean, that's when — I think this is really important for founders that want to grow, right? You have this growth-at-all-cost mindset and you start not asking "why not?" This is the kind of trouble that you get yourself in.

十二、如何让原则真正落地

So let's go back to the management piece. How do you take these principles, which you've applied successfully, to — I would use the term iterate, because for me, failure is all about iteration to success and finding paths that work, finding businesses that work and ultimately finding people that work. But how do you drive that culture that represents the principles? Because you could create a book and give it to all your employees and say, "Guys, here's 41 principles. We've sat down, we've thought about it, we've written them, they're going to work." But to actually live them, to realize them, to hold people not just responsible but accountable to them — how did you do that as you developed these over the decades?

Because at first we tried to get them to do it through sheep dipping. That is, you take everybody in, you give them a big seminar, and I'll go do this.

And from Polanyi — if you want to read a book that's hard to read… I mean, if you want a really hard one, you can read Human Action. This is even harder, called Personal Knowledge by Michael Polanyi, who was a chemist and then became a philosopher. And he goes through what it takes to develop personal knowledge. You have to rewire your brain to have it work differently, right? You have a habit, so you don't need to think about it. And then if you want to change — like, "Do I brush my teeth first or do I comb my hair first? No, I want to start combing my hair first." And all of a sudden, you're back brushing your teeth first because you're not thinking about it.

Because your brain — let's say you're a weightlifter and you want to be a marathoner. Okay, it's going to take work with intensity over time to change your body. Well, your brain's part of your body, so you've got to do the same thing.

So we said, okay, we've got to start with — let's find a group that's really interested in this. They're struggling, they're having problems, and here are the principles, and we'll coach them, we'll help them start doing it. And if they work with intensity on it, and then they succeed, then we don't need sheep dipping. Because then the other businesses and capabilities say, "Gosh, I'd like to do that." So you don't need to call them in. Then we have more demand for people who can help them. And the hardest thing is to have our people in strategy or our principle-based management group who are really good at helping them. Boy, they're in more demand than anybody. So the best thing is, success will drive social mimicry.

十三、Chase：自下而上赋权

Here's another take on what he's saying that I think really connects about culture to your question. And that is, the essence of principle-based management and all the principles in this book as well is: what if you could have a business and a culture, small, medium, or large, where everyone knew what to do without being told?

And so that's hard to get your head around, right? Because I think most businesses come at it from top down. There's the iconic leader that's the smartest guy in the room, building the strategy and then telling everyone what to do.

And so I think one of the most important principles in this — that we tell a lot of stories around — is to flip that on its head. It's about bottom-up empowerment with principles and empowering your talent, your team, your leaders with these principles so that then you use the collective knowledge of everyone, not a couple smart guys at the top of the company.

Most people in most enterprises that aren't owner-operators don't want to fail. They want to keep their job. They want to move up the ladder by being repeatedly successful. And if you want to create a culture of creative destruction, if you want to create a culture of failing and learning from failure, it's very hard to get individuals who live on an income, on a salary, to do that. Because if they fail and then they fail again and they fail again — "I'm worried about losing my job."

So what you typically see in most scaled organizations is middle management and even senior management, when founders or owners don't operate it anymore, saying, "I'm going to take the less risky path. I'm going to do the less creatively destructive thing. I'm going to do the thing that's least likely to fail because I don't want to lose my job. I want to keep my job, get my bonus, move on to year two, and go home to my kids and my wife and take care of the family."

十四、激励的重新对齐与 KDT 的实验

And that approach creates perverse incentives. And so we try to align our incentives — we want to reward people according to their overall contribution to Koch's future. So for example, if they have an experiment — and that doesn't mean doing this thing in ag where you buy all these hogs and you lose hundreds of millions of dollars. That's not an experiment. A good experiment is where the value of what you learn from this failure is higher than the cost of the experiment.

And when we're evaluating a person, you are building capability for the future — that's why we put so much emphasis on, "Are you building capability?" And capability is part of the culture. And what Chase did with Koch Labs when he started Koch Disruptive — he said, "Koch Labs, I want every business to be a laboratory for what we find both to help us source these tech opportunities. And then, if they're just trying something, we'll try it out in that business." Being in all these different businesses that touch almost every part of the economy gives us a big advantage in that.

But that affected the whole culture. I mean, in your business, don't you want to be part of Koch Labs? We're an experimental discovery group. We're just not a bunch of grunts here grinding stuff out.

Yeah, I think the KDT example is a really good one. You asked about motivating and what if you fail and what if you get fired and all that. We tried to basically take a little bit of the Silicon Valley approach and bring experimental discovery — you learn more and you can pivot and learn, have a failure. But then now I know what I don't want to do. I'm going to pivot my strategy. As long as you don't go sink the company with some massive bet, right?

And I think KDT was a great experience. When we made those first investments, you know this in venture — the losers fall out first and the winners take a hell of a lot longer to materialize. So if we would have just judged it based on, "Okay guys, you got three or four years to figure this out," we would have shut down KDT. But it was that experimental discovery principle and mindset that we applied to it. And oh, by the way, we were learning so much as Coke from seeing the technologies that were coming around the corner that might disrupt our core business.

If you just look at it on the bottom line basis in the first couple of years, you say, "Just shut this down." But then over time, like all these different things — and then the returns are starting to come because we thought long term about it. But it all came from that experimental discovery principle, and then creative destruction. Like, if we're not in the game on technology, we don't see what's coming. Something's going to happen, especially with how fast technology's moving today. Some of our businesses are going to become dinosaurs.

十五、收购 Georgia Pacific 的赌注

How much of that risk were you willing to take and did you take on acquisitions? Doing homegrown experiments on new business ideas and strategies and products can be lower cost, but if you're going to do an acquisition, do you have less room for failure?

Well, how about this? We were a much smaller company and we bought Georgia Pacific for 20 billion.

Can you just tell us what Georgia Pacific is for those who don't know?

It's a wood products company and it's got two big pieces to it: building products and consumer products. Well, it's got a third one, but yeah… Okay, I'm generalizing. But go ahead. Sorry, no, no, no. I'll shut up. Shut up, you old guy. No, no, no.

So when did you buy it and how big of a "betting the company" move was that?

  1. We were much smaller in 2005. I can't remember how much smaller, but it was a lot smaller. It was a massive bet.

How did it come up?

Okay, we were applying this Virtuous Cycles of Mutual Benefit. We were saying, okay, what's one of these cycles? Chemical process industries. And wood — creating the pulp and stuff. Matter of fact, we found in my father's thesis, he did a study in Maine on this very thing, on pulping. I found it later, but that didn't… His MIT thesis. Yeah. Wow.

So we said, okay, let's look at this. They were saying they need to spin off some of those parts — the pulping part. And we said, okay, why? Let's buy that. And we bought that as an experiment and we did really well with it. And so we said, wow.

They — that was a commodity business and they were trying to get their price-earnings ratio up. It was like six. And if they became more of a consumer products, they could get it up to nine. So we proposed — we met with them and proposed that we buy the commodity part and we'll pay them a high enough price that then they can be all consumer products and get their price up, and we showed them all the economics. And they said, "That's fine, but we'll be sued for constructive fraud because we have all these lawsuits against us and so we can't do it."

And so we went home and said, "Well, okay, what if we just offered the whole thing?" And a couple of them were getting ready to retire, the senior officers, so they were really liking it — and they were kicked out of all the board meetings from then on. But anyway, we closed them, and that was a time when money was tight and stuff, so nobody came in and topped us.

十六、把 Georgia Pacific 文化扭转过来

I'll give you just one funny story. We sent one of our people in to be the CEO, Joe Moeller, who had been president of the company. And they had — it was totally top-down, bureaucratic. They were in Atlanta. They had this 51-story building. Is it 51? You can correct me. That's right.

And they had a private elevator to get up there. And you didn't have to wear a coat and tie. But if you came up to visit, all the management was on this 51st floor. And you had to put on a coat and tie and get permission to come up there.

And so Joe immediately kicked them all out — we fired a bunch of them — and then sat the remaining ones down to work with their groups on the regular floor. And then turned it all into meeting rooms open to anybody. I mean, you asked about how you get culture change — a lot of it is signals like that, particularly when a bunch of them get fired for being so bureaucratic and hierarchical.

Would you say that that business unit operates like the rest of Koch Industries today?

Oh, absolutely.

But how long will it take?

I'll just say that is such a rare and difficult thing to pull off. I mean, there's just countless stories of acquisitions where the acquirer thinks that they have culture, thinks that they know how to transfer culture, and literally no one seems to be able to do it. This was one of the insights from Warren Buffett — you find great managers, you let them continue to operate as owners of that business and they get some profit share or whatnot. And they've got a durable moat so he can make a long-term investment and he just leaves them.

Yeah, but that wouldn't work for us.

十七、明尼苏达炼油厂：更艰难的文化重塑

Let me give another one, if I could, that was even more difficult. And that is — sadly, my father died not too long after I came with the company in 1967, and we had owned an interest in a small refinery in Minnesota. But two years later, we were able to buy it, and it was not being operated very well. Because the management had let the union control how it was run. And so it was run very inefficiently.

And so the first thing we tried to do is change the work rules. So they went out on strike. And by the way, that was at the start of my honeymoon. Thanks a lot, guys. And it was violent. I mean they ran a switch engine and tried to knock down one of our units. They shot high-power rifles in there and they blocked the gates. I mean it was impossible to get in there. We had to take helicopter. But we were successful in operating without the union workers for nine months bringing people in from our other plants. And it operated better than they did.

So finally we got the work rules changed and then we said, "Okay, we're going to empower the employees. We're going to change the culture." Now you think the Georgia-Pacific was tough — this was much tougher than that. And so we worked and worked on it to try to make their jobs better, get their opinions, get them to work as teams, get them to come up with innovations. And when they did, we rewarded them and we got the unions to agree with that.

Like one — a group of them said, "God, we're buying all these spare parts we need. We can — if you build a machine shop — we can do it cheaper and faster." And they did. And other things, they saved a ton of money for us, made things more efficient. And now the culture there is fantastic. And we're not talking about Wichita, we're talking about Minnesota. So, I mean, we're so proud of what they've done. And it still blows me away how much they've taken these principles to heart and using them to make that place — we've increased the capacity tenfold. And it's one of the best refineries in the country.

十八、Molex 与公开市场的对比

Pop, wouldn't you say that, I mean, the common theme on all of these — because we have the same story on Molex, similar but different than Georgia Pacific. This was a technology company, a connector and cabling company, one of the largest in the world that makes products in your iPhone and MedTech products, your automobile products.

And when we bought that in 2013, it was a paradigm that needed to be changed. You know, you described Georgia Pacific top-down versus bottom-up — there was a lot of that too. But it was top-line thinking versus bottom-line thinking. It was all about revenue growth, right? This is a technology company. And it was also a public company for 30-plus years as well. Because that's what the market rewarded them for.

And so like the whole stock price, the public versus private discussion is interesting here. And it's just — what we've learned is it takes a hell of a lot longer than you think to change the culture. And almost in every case — Pop, tell me if you agree with this — it requires changing leadership that has the paradigm of bottom-up empowerment and that learns and applies the principles.

Almost every time when we fail, it comes down to ignoring the principles. And that's what the book is about. You can kind of reverse engineer these stories like, "Well, we missed that principle. We missed this principle." But it comes down to talent and the right people with the right mindset.

十九、Values First, Skills Second, Credentials Last

And I want to share just one story with you that really shows how we apply this at Coke. He talked about our talent vision being you start with basically culture first, you know, skills second, values first, skills second. I always add a third dimension to that as well. Values first, skills second, credentials last.

And that is a very different mindset than most companies. Because most companies, I think, look at it and say, "I want the guys that have the 4.0 from the Ivy League school and all that." And there's some incredibly smart, obviously, folks from that. But by our experience, and also one of the reasons why we stayed in Wichita, Kansas, is that we can basically hire the farm team. Right? Kids that have grown up on the farm, that have that contribution-motivated mindset, that work their tails off and want to come in to make a contribution, as opposed to coming in expecting all this stuff and coming in with more of an entitlement mindset.

So we — case in point, our CIO today, his name's Jared Benson, his first interaction with Coke was painting striping lines in our parking lot. So no college degree whatsoever, but he found his way into Koch because he demonstrated that, hey, he knows a little bit about data science and he could help us. This was about 20 years ago. And then ultimately he came in, he proved himself. He was running circles around a lot of the team and like, just, you know, contribution-motivated mindset, adding value. He saw the cybersecurity risk and that wave coming, built a whole capability to protect us from cyber attacks. And then now he's CIO of the company, guy with no college degree.

So, I mean, that kind of mindset in terms of our talent vision — that values first and someone that just wants to come in and they just love the job and they want to make a difference.

二十、原则的制度化与 Wichita 的位置

Do you actually codify these principles and everyone at the company has a handbook that lists them out and then they're part of the assessment process for quarterly or annual reviews with people?

Yeah, I mean, obviously there's the book — and this is his fifth book, this is my first. But so, Good Profit, Science of Success, we have our values. My best book, because of Chuck. Hey, Brian — the one we did is pretty good too. Hux will like that, right? But yeah, no, there is a discipline, right? And really it comes down to the leaders taking it seriously, and the leader's first responsibility is to help their people.

Okay, I'm going to put my analyst hat on for a second. And I'm going to say my observation would — I would create a theory, we could test the theory right now — that being in Wichita, being founder- or owner-operated, and having this ability to be isolated from a monoculture… I just feel like Silicon Valley, a lot of companies replicate each other. There's this term that's being used a lot now: over-socialization. You have to operate like everyone around you or you're not part of it. You have to fundraise in the right way and do these deals, you have to hire people this way, you have to do this sort of vesting schedule, this sort of equity. Everyone's the same. And if you don't, you're kind of a weirdo. But by being in Wichita, you don't really have that problem. You can think your own way. You can challenge yourselves. You can debate. You can come up with your own principles without feeling like everyone else is conforming to the groups around you.

But they can — because there are a bunch in Silicon Valley that are. They're challenged. So they're not all that way.

Yeah. But has it always been a competitive advantage? I mean, did you ever think to move the headquarters to New York City?

No. We've never thought of that. I mean, there are advantages of being in New York City. I mean, you have a great mayor now, so they're good to go. But anyway, that's a competitive advantage of us. That'll be a clue.

二十一、为什么坚持不上市

But this is — no, but the main threat we've had is, I wanted to go public. And I said, "It'd be over my dead body." And some of them thought that would be a good idea. Just like they're thinking that about Trump, what a lot of people still think about. I get a lot of nice notices of my imminent death. And they say, "Okay, your brother died. We hope it was — I know this is painful, but I hope it was slow and painful, and I hope Charles is even worse." That's the kind of crap we get.

But anyway, the biggest push has been for us to go public. "God, we'll be worth so much and stuff." And I think our view is, we never could have accomplished what we've had. First of all, we never would have built a principle-based framework. And then we never would have been able to pull off this capability-bounded versus industry-bounded. Because people don't understand it. Now, if you're Buffett and you've sold that long, then people, okay — but he wasn't trying to integrate them the way we do. No one would believe it. And that's because you've got to have a story that the analysts can understand. Otherwise, we would, like Georgia Pacific, we would have a low price-earnings ratio.

So being private, being in Wichita — competitive advantages.

二十二、所有者运营 vs. 职业经理人

What about being owner-operated or founder-operated? There's this argument that the best Silicon Valley companies are those who are founder-led for as long as possible because the founders are willing to destroy the business creatively. They're willing to think about what's over the hill, make the tough decisions, reinvent the company, hire and fire as needed, be willing to take the short-term financial loss for the long-term. Have you been able to get that to distill down into the organization? Because that's the thing that most public companies that are not owner-operated deal with and struggle with — managers that are short-term incentivized and aren't and can't take the big risks and the risks of failure that you're able to embrace.

No, but I think it depends on the values of the owners. One of our principles is that any good partnership of any kind, whether it's marriage, friend, employee, partner — requires three things. It requires shared vision, shared values, and having complementary capabilities that you use to make each other better. And if you miss any one of those, you're not going to have a lasting good partnership.

And so I remember I was at the YPO group in Wichita. I was presenting — this is like 20 years ago — presenting what we were doing and why. And one of them said, "Well, how do you get that to work in a private company?" And I answered, "Well, no, it's easier than in a public company, just like I did." And then I thought who it was. And he was talking about his father. His father was a total dictator. And there's no way he could apply any of these principles in that. So it all matters who the owners are and what their values are.

Can a public CEO that doesn't have a big ownership stake in the company adopt these principles and transform the culture of that company?

I mean, he — Buffett — wasn't doing these principles, but he had a different principle. And that is: "I'm going to buy a company. I'm not going to take a top price, but what's meaningful to them is they run it. So I'm going to buy it and let them run it." And so that was his principle, his value, that made him tremendous. Well, the other one was buying insurance companies, so you'd have a lot of liquidity to go do all these things. And those two things are what made him successful.

二十三、Chase 的童年与转折

Chase, I want to just go back to your getting involved in the business. We didn't get into that, but how did you get started at Coke? And were you always a believer in the principles from a young age? Were you around the organization?

Are you kidding? Yeah. I'm going to chip off the old block — it took me a while to come around. No, this is the most remarkable transformation I've ever been talking about. This is the primo. Here we go. Unbelievable. Do I get to tell my story? To the absolute butt — to the absolute top. No, he's blown beyond me. He's doing things I wouldn't even dream of or have the capability to do. Unbelievable. He'll correct me ten times as I tell this story. Well, because you're too damn humble. No.

So I didn't start when I was six. I started when I was 15. So he cut me some slack, but it's because I was a pretty competitive tennis player. He was nationally ranked. Say the humility. He was nationally ranked. That's the first time. Yeah.

So, at 15, I got burned out — you know, tennis, like typical story — where I wanted to hang out with my friends. I wanted to have a good time. I was tired of playing six hours a day. So I started throwing tennis matches intentionally to get out of these tournaments, so I'd go home and party with my friends. And he said, "Look, your attitude is terrible. You can either give 100% on the tennis court and apply yourself, or I'm going to get you a job."

I said, "I'm sick of tennis. I'm done with it." And so I had — the job was figured out the next morning for me. Yeah, but I'm going to interrupt again because this is important. He thought he would get a nice cushy job in Wichita so he'd go out and party with his friends at night. Yeah. Well, you know, I may be old and slow, but I'm not that slow. Yeah, he was kicked out of a number of schools. I was born at night, but not last night.

So, basically all my shit was packed for me. And it was thrown in the back of a truck. And six hours later I showed up at a feed yard. And I lived in a single-wide trailer the whole summer with my boss. I slept on the floor. Worked seven days a week. And just shoveled cow shit and dug post holes. No, puppy boils. I forgot about that. Let's keep it manageable here.

So anyway, but the interesting part of that story — even though I went from literally being like, you know, kind of country club rich kid to doing that within 24 hours — it was an absolute transformation for me. The fact that he made me do that. And I chose, but I didn't know what I was exactly choosing. But after I was a month or two into this job, I started actually feeling better about myself. You know, it was like I hadn't really made a contribution up till that point in life. And then I'm actually working with a team, I'm getting paid minimum wage, I'm working my tail off, I'm adding value, even though it may be like just menial work.

And this — I go back to a letter that his father wrote to him and his other brothers. No, no, my older brother. Your older brother, sorry. Because I was three months old when he wrote it. This letter about, basically, "When I pass on, you're going to get what seems to be a large sum of money. I hope you don't squander it. I hope you don't use it for — but I hope you actually apply yourself because I want you to feel the glorious feeling of accomplishment." And it's a really amazing letter. He has it hung in his office.

But, you know, that was the first time I felt that, even though it was working at a feed yard. I was like, "This feels good, actually." You know, I could have gone down the path of just kind of staying on the tennis circuit. I have no idea where I'd end up in life had I not gone down that path. And so I basically worked every summer for Coke from that summer on. I worked in the gas liquids plant, I worked in our refineries, all the way through junior year in college. Always had a Coke kind of summer job.

二十四、Charles 对 Chase 的赞许

Let me give you one more credit here. See, he has great humor, which is great, but — Chase has — I have this gift for abstractions. He's like his mother. He has a gift for people. He understands people and can relate to them. And he can go around like she can, or Sterling Varner, who was our president in the early days, who, by the way, was born — his father ran mules in an oil field camp and he was born in a tent. Damn near died, never went to college, and whoever he met with wanted to do business with us. And that's the way Chase is. He goes around, meets these people, and all of a sudden they're friends and they want to do business. And that's true for Stand Together too — go to people you think, "Oh, they don't want these capitalists or these free enterprise people to do business with them," and he gets them on our side and shows them the value of these principles.

Let me make — is that fair?

Whatever you said. No, I mean, that's my job. That's what I do now — origination of partnerships. And about the time I met you, that's when I started getting into technology and trying to build a new community so that we could get access to the most disruptive founders.

二十五、比较优势：Chase 给自己"开除"

But one quick story I want to tell — because I think it'll be helpful for your audience as well, that we haven't touched on yet — is the principle of comparative advantage.

Because another meaningful, total shift in my job at Coke and my role at Coke — but also in my personal life — was when I was running the ag business. This was later on. We talked about the late 90s, the gas-to-bread spread and all that. This was a separate business with Coke Fertilizer.

I spent 10 years in that business really understanding the operations of a business. So I worked in sales and marketing and the accounting, the finance, like every piece of it, the trading. And I ran a lot of the smaller business units. But at one point, my boss at the time wanted to add a whole natural gas trading business to it. So he's like, "Hey, I want to put a parent company called Ag and Energy Solutions, and I'm going to throw you the keys to the fertilizer business. You're ready to run it."

And so I was promoted to president of Koch Fertilizer at that time. And about nine months in, I realized that I was not the guy for the job, and I walked in my boss's office and fired myself. And it was humiliating, right? Especially being the boss's son and thinking about, "Oh my God, I'm a failure. I couldn't make this work." The business was still doing fine, but I wasn't doing a good job as a leader. And I knew there was someone else that had the comparative advantage to be a great operator, CEO, president type role.

And so I learned through all that — call it a failure — in that job that I wasn't an operator and I wasn't a good optimization-type leader, and I was a builder. Like all I wanted to do was go work on the innovation stuff. That was about the time I met you and I learned about Climate Corp and all that. I just wanted to go focus on that and go build this stuff — this whole idea of creative destruction that would disrupt the core business that I was running.

And so that whole thing around understanding your comparative advantage — what you're good at and what you're not relative to others that could be doing that job — was a huge deal. And my hope was that that was a little bit of an example for other Koch leaders as well. It's like, if you're not in the right job, you don't have to fire yourself, but figure out what your power alley really is and where you can contribute and add the most value.

So that experience for me — what was amazing, if you look at what happened after that — we got a great president to continue to transform the fertilizer business. It's one of our most exciting businesses today. We keep growing with it. So that did better than it would have done had I stayed in the role.
