Coinbase One card is offered through Coinbase Inc. and Cardless, Inc., cards issued by First Electronic Bank. Bitcoin back rates are based on cardholders' assets on Coinbase.

四、Circle 与 ARC：稳定币的机构基础设施

So speaking of things that may have value, let's talk about stable coins, and let's specifically talk about Circle and ARC.

So today news came out about a $222 million token pre-sale at a $3 billion fully diluted valuation. A16Z was leading at 75 million. And the investor list is a roll call of TradFi people: BlackRock, Apollo, ICE (not the ones deporting people—the parent of New York Stock Exchange), SBI, Janus, Stanchart Ventures, General Catalyst, so on and so forth. Circle is the first publicly listed company to run a token pre-sale. Stock is unsurprisingly up on the news.

And as a reminder for people, what's ARC? ARC is an EVM-compatible L1. USDC is the native gas token, and the testnet has been running since October 2025 with hundreds of millions of transactions. BlackRock, Visa, Goldman, AWS, even Anthropic have been testing out there. So ARC is Circle's way of owning the stack—as opposed to using Ethereum and Solana for settlement and Coinbase for distribution.

So there have been a number of voices around this. Circle is saying ARC is the "economic operating system for institutional finance." Maxwell Albright from B6Z says, "The internet infrastructure USDC runs on today wasn't built with big institutions in mind. That's where ARC comes in."

Chris：代币 vs 股票的价值归属

So what I want to throw to the crowd—and Chris, I'll start with you—with all those names on the cap table, is this going to be the institutional rail that finally works and brings people in, or is this another, call it, private chain dead-end but with better PR if nothing else?

Yeah, super fascinating announcement today. And if you're playing Bits and Bips bingo, we had Ram already bash Sam Altman, say "nothing burger," Austin talk about stable coins. I guess I got to be bullish now.

But I think this was super interesting because all these TradFi firms are investing in a token, not in private equity. That's how I read it. And what this does is it normalizes token investments and it legitimizes them as investment activity for the TradFi folks. And to do that, they have to have a lot of infrastructure. So that's one thing to think about.

But perhaps the most interesting thing is how to think about value accrual going forward. This is a public company and it's now issuing a token. We haven't seen the base token yet come out of Coinbase, but you have investors, right? And typically you're out there selling your equity to investors. Now we have a public company that's selling a token to investors. And so how does the value accrual work going forward if you are an investor—token versus equity? And yes, as venture capitalists, we do this. We've done this for years and years. And typically we're not—you know, sometimes you're like, "Hey, I don't know if it's going to accrue to the equity. I don't know if it's going to accrue to the token. Maybe going to have warrants in one. We'll figure it out because it could go in either direction. It's such an early startup."

But gosh, now you see a public company where you have to really think about that. Now, where do we go from here? Super interesting there. I want to get Ram's take. I want to get your take, Austin. But then you're also seeing a part of a company monetizing itself. And if you step back for a second, you're like, "Okay, so this public company, it's got this chain. I can buy the token. I can buy the equity." You know, where do we go from here? Are different business segments going to start issuing tokens? Are there going to be prediction markets on individual segments underneath the corporation? Absolutely, they're going to be. I don't know when and how. But I mean, I think ultimately super bullish for crypto because you're seeing big investors coming in and buying tokens. I mean, I've known venture capitalists for years and years. They're like, "Can't do it, only do equities. Haven't really figured out the token thing." But really want to get your guys' take on value accrual and how you think about this through a public equity lens.

Ram：Tracking Stock 类比与 Circle 的"无中生有"

Nodding my head there. Yeah, I agree. I think that's the number one question. What's the flow of the economics through the equity and the token? You know, it's also interesting—Circle stock went up 15%. It went up like four and a half to five billion dollars. So as of today, it's got a free lunch on this, right? They created $3 billion of value out of issuing this thing. And then their market cap also went up.

So it reminds me—like in the dotcom era, if you guys remember 3Com, it had a subsidiary that was worth more than the holding. We're not there yet, but you know what used to happen is you'd have a tracking stock, and a tracking stock would represent a subsidiary of the organization, and then the public markets could measure the value of the subsidiary by looking at the tracking stock, and then eventually they do a spin-out. So that's the closest analogy to what we see here.

It's a big win for Circle overall. I mean, they were able to create something from nothing, get investors to back it. So anytime you can pull that off, it's a win. I don't know what the flow of the economics looks like yet. Is there any attachment to the stable coin revenue on this at all, or just a wholly separate blockchain? Because as you know, Coinbase has a perpetual exclusive right on the stable coin revenue attached to this. I wonder—I assume that's still intact. There's no change there.

Yeah. Has Coinbase reiterated that this week? Like, "Oh yeah, we've got this contract in perpetuity in case anyone forgot."

But why is this equity going up? I mean, if you were to step back—wait a second, they're launching a token that's completely going to be dilutive to the equity. But no, the market's not saying that. Why is the market not saying that?

Austin：两种解释与未来分拆假设

All right. So when I was looking at this, I have two potential theories.

Theory one, because we used to see this in the dotcom bubble, is just people are wrong. You kind of have people preferring the stock who are betting the value accrual is there, and people preferring the token who are betting the value accrual is there. And one of those two groups is wrong, and there's some sort of spread trade between these two things to pull them back to par.

Theory two—and Ram, you kind of alluded to this—is an expectation that ARC will not remain within Circle. That the idea is, if this thing is going to work and become payments rails on a standalone basis, it probably can't be controlled by the issuer of the payments instrument. If we look at Visa as a good example, I will remind everybody that original issuers of cards were spun out of other entities and then became the card networks. They did not become the card networks housed within the original entity. Discover, similar story of being spun out of something and now reacquired ironically by somebody else. But the idea here is, if both of these valuations are correct, that has to be the market pricing future growth into ARC that is beyond what Circle is currently doing—which seems to rely on an expectation of it being spun out and created into its own thing. Ram, to your point of, is this a tracking stock where they would intend to move this thing out into the world as its own business unit? To me, that's the only way these valuations are coherent to both be going up like that.

Ram：金融工程与分发战略

There's no dilution to Circle because there's no economic claim on Circle through this kind of ARC spin-off. But there's still so much more to see. Like, are blockchains back again? The interesting thing is people are more focused on this transaction, the mechanics of it, and who got involved than what the heck the thing does, right? Like what is the value? We're focused on the financial engineering.

Right. And you know, this will be—you'll see more of these. Chris alluded to Base spin-out. So this becomes the next magic trick of crypto. Saylor introduced us to financial engineering. People got the bug. How can they create value out of thin air? This is what's happening now.

Well, I don't think it's value of thin air. I think there is a narrative here, and that narrative is distribution. Right? If you look at Circle, they've been heavily reliant on Coinbase for its distribution since its inception. And maybe the market's saying, "Well, wait a second, via Arc, there's going to be an entire new class of distribution away from Coinbase. This is how they solve and generate that additional distribution of their core asset, which is USDC, and that value accrual back to the equity." That's what the market's saying today—is that this is the first step in a grander distribution strategy that we know Circle needs.

Austin：中立性与机构准入

So it's a stable coin settlement layer. Has anyone dug into exactly the target customers, value prop, how this comes together? Obviously they have their own stable coin. So are they going to be Switzerland and neutral here—which is what you should be if you're a settlement layer—or are they going to have a preference?

This is back to my point on why I think there may eventually be a spin-off here. Right now obviously it preferences USDC because you can use USDC as the gas token. I have not seen—and by the way, ARC folks, correct me if I'm wrong—but I have not seen the ability to use any other token as the gas layer on ARC. So that is preferencing USDC.

But Ram, you are correct that in the long run, if you want these other big institutions to use it, or it to have a shot of replacing Mastercard or Visa or even just ACH, etc., you're going to need to let people use their own stuff. Like the value prop of "hey Chase, hey Bank of America, hey Citi, why don't you give up your entire NIM to use our fast payments chain" is just not there. The answer from those people will be "thanks but no thanks. We'll keep the NIM."

So again, back to why these things have been spun out. If you said, "Well, yeah, but what we've really got is a 24/7 fast payments infrastructure—and JPM, you can put your deposit token on here. Citi, you can put a deposit token on here. BofA, you launched a stable coin, you can put that on here, and everybody can trade those against each other and clear them in some way"—now we're talking. But that starts to look like the blockchain version of the card networks.

Ram：竞争格局与资方解读

Yeah. It's more competition for all the blockchains. Where do you want to position here? It's more competition for Ethereum. It's more competition for Canton. Canton's looking at that and saying, "Hey, look, we already locked up this market. We're going to use our own chain." Notably, there were no strategic investors from Wall Street in this deal. It was West Coast VCs. So they're probably going to take a more disruptive attack angle as opposed to kind of co-opting the intermediaries.

But on the distribution, I think it's a great point. How will they—you know, Coinbase is the distribution layer for Circle right now. People have to log in, they have to onboard, convert. So what does that look like? They have a B2B capability, of course. My old firm, Cross River, I built a crypto business there. We helped Circle move money, and they have a B2B sales team. I suppose they'll build that out further. So there's a lot more questions here, but it's intriguing.

You know, it's like as if Andreessen Horowitz woke up and kind of willed the crypto bull market back to life. They're like, "How do we just make something happen here? Let's call up Circle. We got a new blockchain." It's a proprietary deal they just manufactured. Well, they just raised a $2 billion fund, so there's definitely LP interest out there if you're A16Z.

No community participation though, right? So if you're a retail investor, you got left out in the cold on this. Now, the move would have been own Circle stock, I suppose.

Yeah. Is that it?

Yeah. If you—that's right. If you own Circle stock, you benefited today.

So right. So the decentralized investing via the MetaMasks and the wallets doesn't matter—the game is just in public markets. Now there's going to be—I mean, this is back to the point of clarity and the SEC writing the rules. There's going to be increasing convergence between securities markets and token markets as we move forward, because a lot of the existence of token markets was because they couldn't get into securities markets because of Gensler.

五、Coinbase 裁员：AI 叙事还是周期性挤压？

So I mean, on that note, let's talk about something in securities markets.

So Coinbase had a pretty rough week. On Monday, they announced a 14% headcount cut, about 700 people. Brian Armstrong was talking about restructuring the firm to "player coaches" and AI native pods—five layers max below the CEO. Allegedly, non-technical teams pushing code.

But on May 7th, the Q1 print was a big miss: - $394 million net loss - Revenue of $1.41 billion versus $1.52 estimated - EPS of minus $1.49 versus 27 cents positive - Spot trading volume down 37% quarter-over-quarter

And then just to add insult to injury, there was five-plus hours of offline trading on Friday thanks to an AWS US-East failure, which Armstrong called "completely unacceptable."

So that was the AI pitch and then there's skepticism around it. Derek Thompson is asking, "Is Coinbase the test case? Is this a productivity revolution or just earnings pressure layoffs dressed up in AI?" People are saying Coinbase seems to have a hard dependency on AWS regardless of what their CEO is saying. And Greg Eisenberg says every public company will run this playbook in the next 12 months. Coinbase just got there first.

Chris：清晰度法案与监管整合

I want to ask you guys as we start here. And Chris, I'll start with you on this one. So institutional people watch Coinbase. A lot of people use them. They do have a real business, but the cyclicality is intense, just like for investment banks. So as somebody who's seen cyclicality and seen layoffs before, do you think this is really AI or is that window dressing on just the cyclicality of the business model?

Yeah. I guess this means that the bull market for alts is about to kick off, because what we've seen is every time there's a major Coinbase layoff, that's really the kickoff of the next cycle.

So no, look, I think we're seeing this narrative hit all of tech and they're saying "AI AI, I'm going to chop my workforce." But the data is telling us that AI is actually leading to a lot of new jobs. And so maybe you're seeing cuts here for various reasons. But you're also going to see a rewiring and I think a lot more hiring of different skill sets as we restructure companies with this new technology in mind.

I'll also note that we've said this in the past, and I don't like to talk about individual stocks or tokens on this show, but what are we seeing out there? Spot businesses are very brutal businesses. You saw it in TradFi. Equities commissions go to zero over time. It's very very similar for tokens. Spot trading goes to zero. Really hard to have a business there. Derivatives, however, are a much more interesting business. I'll note that Coinbase did acquire Deribit. So that's something to watch.

But when I step back and look at the entirety of the macro picture, there are a lot of things going in favor of some of these incumbents, and it really comes down to clarity. Now, the Clarity Act is coming into effect. We think about 60-some percent chance it gets done. You're going to see a lot of regulation follow. We're getting some regulation to follow either way. And my point that I made earlier today, regulation forces consolidation. Generally, the big become bigger. That head start that they have becomes a moat once the regulation is entrenched.

And so as we see clarity come into effect, I think incumbents, many of the incumbents including Coinbase, stand to benefit from it. Because you see consolidation. Look how many other folks can do what they can do across all their different business lines. I mean, years and years ago, they were a spot business, and like, so credit to Brian and team for diversifying across the board—derivatives, custody, and everything else. So interesting things going on. It's not the last you're going to see of it. You'll see more of it as we move forward. But again, I still think it's a really constructive setup. Particularly as we're looking at the crypto space as a whole.

Ram：山寨季信号与 Circle 关系

Yeah, I agree. It's constructive. We're in the break and I brought up, like, "Hey, look, I think altseason might be here, guys." So a lot of opportunities that are very constructive. I mean, digital assets have held up even with memory stocks rallying.

I'm not sure that Coinbase really had that brutal a week. The stock was up 7% today. When companies cut headcount, it boosts earnings. That's the key thing. So it's actually bullish. If you're a shareholder, also, they've got recurring revenue from the Circle relationship and they reiterated that it's forever. They inked a good deal there. That's good.

The main competition for Coinbase is the rise of all these ETFs and the DATs, which create an alternative method to trade with security, and they make a lot of money on transaction fees. So that's the bear case.

I don't know, Ram, because they're making custody on the back end too. The fees on transaction fees are so much higher than on custody, which is more commoditized.

Yeah, they were—I'm just trying to present both sides of it.

Austin：现货费率归零的宿命

But this is the problem that Chris is alluding to, which we've seen over and over in markets, which is Morgan Stanley is coming in now with lower fees on things. Others are going to follow behind that. Spot fees trend to zero on average over time in markets, and that leads to spot being profitable for two groups of people.

Group number one, and a good example of this business model is somebody like Schwab, which is people who could actually monetize the funding—like the interest rate of assets on the platform. So Coinbase, Ram to your point around the Circle deal, could move to something that looks more like a funding business and probably be fine.

Number two is derivatives, as Chris said, right? Like, you move away from the spot market and keep the spot market to facilitate derivatives trading and then you make money on those, right? Like, I will remind everybody it's ICE, the derivatives exchange, that bought the New York Stock Exchange—not the other way around, as we've mentioned before.

And so I think either Coinbase really needs to up their game in derivatives markets or funding markets or both, or the beatings will continue until stock price improves.

Ram：定位为 Schwab 的继承者

The right positioning for Coinbase is to position against Robinhood and position as the successor to Charles Schwab and own the customer in a cross-asset class holistic experience where you have your banking and your investing and payments. That is really the only correct answer. There are a lot of ways to make money. We've talked about several of them. But the positioning in the public markets is what's going to drive the valuation. And they already have the reach. They're securing a banking license. They're investing in the app. So that's the right move.

But people see it. People see it, right? I'm running after that opportunity at Lumida. I've got an app. It's already better than the Coinbase app and the Robinhood app. Don't have trading enabled. Coinbase will realize they should buy the business for under a billion while they can. But that's the move that Coinbase should focus on—how do you occupy that position? How do you have a lock on the customer with an integrated product offering to buy or sell securities?

六、Consensus 余兴节目与行业形象

So Chris, I know you have a hard stop at 4:30 and I'm going to lob the ball up on the last one for you that I know you wanted to talk about, which is at the end of Consensus.

The official Consensus Miami afterparty was held at 11, Miami's Bitcoin-accepting strip club, with lines around the block, hours long waits, and a Bloomberg headline of "crypto industry throws lap dance party in middle of bear market."

By the way, the North American Bitcoin Conference had an event there in 2018, which Bloomberg's original "the industry has a problem" piece came from. Same venue, same article, eight years apart. Somebody put a halving joke in there somehow.

So there have been several critics here. Haley Lennon, Bitcoin Barb, Amanda Wick. Kelly Kirkbose has said it's a venue that accepts Bitcoin and defended it.

Chris：机构化时代与行业成长

But Chris, I want to throw this to you. How does this look for the industry? Why are people doing this? And what is going on?

Yeah. I feel like in crypto, we keep learning these lessons from the past, and it's like we ignore the lessons of history. Like, we talk about derivatives markets and—yeah, I'll get to strippers in a second—but like derivatives markets, you know, people think because you have prediction markets, the laws of market manipulation and abuse don't apply. Well, they do.

I remember my first day at Lehman Brothers back in 2006, associate class. They marshaled all the associates into this pristine room and out comes Joe Gregory drinking a Tab. He used to drink Tab. You remember that? It was weird. And he's just like—the first thing he said was, "No strippers." Like, and I was like, "What the heck did I get into here?" Like, here I am at Lehman Brothers, and that's the first thing that they said. And that era was back in the 80s or whatever, and in my entire career on Wall Street, I don't remember once ever going near that kind of stuff because the markets matured.

And I feel like now we are in—this is not how you get to institutionalization, and we're in an institutional era. So look, I think there's going to be a lot more due diligence amongst some of the brands whose names were featured, and my sense is that they didn't even know in many cases of the affiliations, and they're like, "Okay, a free branding for the after hour, of course." And so I think brands are real. Brands are very important, and I think you're going to see a lot more due diligence.

This is not the first time we saw it. Remember we had like people eating sushi off of models or something in ECC a couple years ago. Wasn't that—I don't remember. But it's time for the industry to grow up. And yeah, I feel like some of those marketers probably had a really bad day because they didn't realize what they had done.

Ram：Consensus 的判断失误

Consensus just got a lot more visibility. They're quietly saying, "Hey, bad news is good news, but we won't do it again." I agree on Chris's points around the maturity of the industry. You know, it's one thing for a side-shoot event with a protocol that wants to make that decision, but it's kind of odd for Consensus to make that decision, right?

Yeah. It shouldn't have been official. I don't think it's good business, frankly. Like, that's the point—if you're trying to appeal to institutions, you're trying to appeal to mass market, probably not the best business decision. People can do what they want to do. But maybe not a great business decision here.

结尾

All right. Well, on that note, Chris, I know we got to stop. It's 4:30 for you. So for everybody, as always, thanks for joining us for this episode of Bits and Bips. We'll be back in one week to discuss more about how the worlds of crypto and macro are colliding. Till then, everyone—

Thank you for watching and hope you enjoyed this episode of Bits and Bips. Just remember, nothing we say here is investment advice. And please check unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips for more disclosures.

Hey, hey, hey.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、霍尔木兹海峡的"金融化"：地缘冲突正在变成一种利率衍生品
最反直觉的观察是：这场伊朗冲突已经悄然从"军事博弈"演化成"流动性博弈"。Luke Groman 那句"伊朗不必打败美军，只需打败美国国债市场"被 Ram 斥为废话——但他真正反驳的并不是论点本身，而是**论点的时代背景**。
真正发生的事情更微妙：FinCEN 在物理封锁之外悄悄启动了**货币封锁**（前公司侦测、稳定币流向监控、影子船队的链上画像）。这意味着美国正在把**"制裁基础设施"做成一种可定价的地缘资产**——Trump 现在能拿到中国谈判桌上的，不只是航母，而是**"我们可以选择性松开伊朗脖子上的那只脚"**。这是一种全新的外交筹码：流动性即外交。
跨领域连接：这种"踩住脖子但不踢死"的策略，本质上和**美联储对市场的前瞻指引**是同一个数学结构——通过控制"释放速度"而非"释放数量"来塑造对手行为。地缘政治正在被美联储化。
## 二、Circle 的 ARC：一次伪装成区块链发布的"分拆预演"
表面新闻是 Circle 发币、股票还涨了 15%。所有人都在讨论 token vs equity

item.title: [Lex Clips] What will be remembered about human civilization 1,000 years from now? | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的洞察："压缩"是一种文明的失忆症

大多数人把压缩视为技术问题——节省带宽、降低存储成本。但这段对话揭示了一个被忽视的隐喻：压缩本质上是一种"认识论暴力"。当算法判定某个像素"不重要"时，它实际上在替未来人类做决定——决定什么是"值得记住的"。

最惊人的悖论在于：archivist 们害怕的不是数据丢失，而是"看起来完好"的损耗。一个损坏到无法播放的文件会被立刻识别为问题；但一段被有损压缩"修过"的胶片，会以"完整"的假象骗过未来一千年的研究者。换句话说，最危险的不是黑暗，而是被美化过的光。这与历史学界正在讨论的"算法考古学"惊人地呼应：未来历史学家可能要先解码我们的偏见，才能解读我们的影像。

二、跨领域连接：C 语言 = 拉丁语 = 圣本笃修道院

Romain 把 C 语言比作拉丁语，这个类比比他自己意识到的还要深刻。

请考虑欧洲中世纪的修道院抄写传统：在罗马帝国崩塌后，正是修士们在烛光下逐字抄写古典文本，才让亚里士多德、西塞罗穿越了"黑暗时代"。今天 FFmpeg 的社区正在扮演同样的角色——只不过抄写的不是羊皮纸，而是 codec；不是拉丁文动词变位，而是 1950 年代某种磁带的色度学规范。Dave Rice 和那些 archivist，本质上是数字时代的本笃会修士。

更妙的是结构相似性：本笃修道院通过"贫穷誓言"（限制资金）反而获得了纯粹性；FFmpeg archiving 社区也是因为缺钱才不得不开源，因为开源反而获得了文明级的影响力。约束催生了不可替代性——这是一个被现代效率主义彻底误解的真理。

三、被忽略的系统性真相：21 世纪的真正稀缺是"未被污染的人类信号"

视频里那句"在一千年后我们可能淹没在 AI slop 中"是闲笔，但它指向一个深刻的相变：

  • 20 世纪以前：信息稀缺，每一份留存都珍贵
  • 21 世纪初：信息过载，但仍以人类生产为主
  • 21 世纪中后：AI 生成内容占主导，真实的人类影像变成新的稀有金属

这意味着 archivist 们今天保存的那些 70/80 年代的家庭录像带、地方电视新闻，其价值曲线不是线性的，而是会在 AI 泛滥后突然垂直上升——就像哥白尼之前没人在意托勒密手稿，AI 之后我们会突然意识到"未被污染的人类目光"是文明的稀土。

而最辛辣的反讽是：决定一千年后人类如何记住自己的，不是 OpenAI、不是 Google、不是任何巨头——而是一群你从没听过名字的开源贡献者，在没有预算的情况下决定哪盘磁带值得用最后一个能读它的磁头去抢救。文明的命脉，正掌握在 commit history 里。

四、一个让人脊背发凉的延伸推论

Romain 提到"全世界没有足够的磁头去读完所有磁带，所以必须决定丢弃哪些"。

这在认识论上是逆向的"图书馆焚毁"——亚历山大图书馆是一次性灾难，而我们正在经历的是慢动作的、由资源约束自动执行的、无人需要为之负责的渐进式遗忘。没有暴君下令，没有大火，只是磁头老化的速度比志愿者多的速度快。

这或许是人类历史上第一次：遗忘不是被迫的，而是被默认的；不是因为我们不知道珍贵，而是因为我们知道却来不及。

📝 转录文本

开场：FFmpeg 作为"千年罗塞塔石碑"

Lex: Karen, you you said that there's an active archiving preservation community. I think that's super fascinating. You wrote that they're stretching budget, but they see the extreme importance of FFmpeg as a Rosetta Stone so that multimedia can be played a thousand years from now. I mean, that's a beautiful way to see FFmpeg / VLC as a tool for preserving visual knowledge.

Guest: Yes, it's one of the coolest communities in open source multimedia, mainly led by someone called Dave Rice — I'll give him a shout out, I think from City University of New York — is the archiving community. They've done so much stuff that they value, that they value open source. One, because yes, they lack budgets, but two, they see the fact that archiving video is important for the world. And being able to play that is a big problem.

历史教训：英国的"新末日审判书"

Famously in the UK there was something called the the new Doomsday Book, and they archived lots of stuff on BBC microcomputers. Within 10 to 15 years, no one had the right software to play that — I think it was 20 years or something like that. And someone had to go and reverse engineer this, and that was like 20 years. Imagine that in a thousand years.

为什么是 C 语言？—— "C 将像拉丁语一样"

I think one of the great things about FFmpeg is it's written in C. C is the closest to mathematics you're probably going to get, the closest to logic.

Lex: You're just thinking 1,000 years we'd still have C compilers?

Guest: Yes, we have languages that exist that haven't changed too much. We have mathematics, mathematical notation that exists. It will be like Latin. C will be like Latin — it will be a thing that you learn from the past, but it will still be usable in certain contexts.

FFV1：为档案而生的无损编解码器

So the archiving community are really great practically. They — again, limited funds — they funded the development of the FFV1 codec. So that's a lossless codec, so that the archiving community… is really scared about the act of compression losing things. And this is — they have a fair point in this, you know.

Yeah. If they compress too hard, it could change the view of the material. There could be something slightly different here and there. So they're really concerned about things needing to be not just compressed well, but lossless and be fast. And so they worked with FFmpeg to develop a whole new codec designed for fast software-based encoding.

They're really concerned about resilience. So if they're storing on tapes or other hard disks, "I lose some bits, I need to recover quickly. I can't lose a whole GOP because I've lost the bit," something like that. So they're really great bunch of people. They funded GPU encoding in FFmpeg to make FFV1 encode faster.

一种文明级别的使命

And it's really about preserving the world's multimedia heritage in a way that's usable. And there's a lot of great teams and a lot of archival groups across the world who've chosen FFmpeg and FFV1 as their archiving solution. And they can really provide us also super specialist advice. They can explain: "Ah, in the 1950s colorimetry was done like this on this certain type of tape, and so there is a special case that you need to handle, and you'll never get this anywhere else." They know things on video that we don't.

Lex: Yes.

Guest: Like every time I talk with a—

Lex: Dave Rice—

Guest: Dave Rice, or the people from the British… British film… it's just like every time, I just learn something new. And I've been doing video for 20 years. They have, especially on colorimetry and colors storage and these other things—

对内容本身的深切敬畏

Lex: They have a deep, deep appreciation of the content itself, of the video itself. And especially when you're thinking lossless, they're terrified of losing something essential about the thing. And in so doing, they're deeply understanding the thing that is to be preserved — which you sometimes might not be thinking about when you're obsessing about the actual technology of the encoding and so on.

And when you enter the rabbit hole of film scanners, right? So you take those—

Guest: Uh-huh.

Lex: —those things to make, to digitalize…

Guest: Yeah. A huge topic that would take another five hours of podcast just on that topic.

胶片的脆弱与开源的普惠

And there's a lot of film that needs to be archived. Film is degrading. It's maybe not stored in the right environment. The other thing is — because it's open source, they give away their workflows to countries who can't afford to have archiving institutions, where archiving is done by volunteers, it's done by other things. They go and teach, you know, in India they teach children to do FFmpeg commands. They're really great.

They're really the model community, the model ethos of what we're trying to achieve. They are such a great bunch of people, so interested in participating and being part of something much bigger, because they realize the work they're doing in a thousand years is going to tell a lot.

在 AI 洪流前抢救 20 世纪

You know, in a thousand years we may be drowning in AI slop. This stuff needs to be important and, you know, archived well. What was life like?

Lex: Yeah, it feels like capturing the 20th century in the 21st century is essential, because it feels like a transition point — where we went from scarcity of data to…

Guest: Yeah.

Lex: …oceans of slop. And that transition point is good to archive.

Guest: People don't realize: we are losing today a ton of films. There is a ton of things from the 30s, from the 40s and the 50s, that there is no value [assigned to them].

Lex: And tape — 70s and 80s, there's tape, and there's not enough tape heads in the world to read all the tapes.

Guest: So they have to decide what they want to archive and throw away the rest of the tapes.

数字管家职责：道德困境

Lex: There's huge moral hazard, I guess for want of a better phrase, around this topic — because this is a digital record of human history, and they have to make decisions.

[sighs and gasps]

But — and there's digital stewardship, I suppose for want of… I've made that phrase up, that's not a real phrase—

[snorts]

—to make sure the world can have this information in something that's playable by everybody, not playable on some device that where it doesn't exist anymore.

干草堆里的针：未来才会显现的价值

And then there's like, realistically speaking, there's a needle in a haystack where there's a lot of value in archiving all that footage and then over time finding the gems that we don't know are there. Hey, there was something in that corner that we just didn't know.

Guest: Yeah. And now that would have been compressed away because some little thing — oh wow, there's something there — and they've made sure that it's lossless. I can prove mathematically that it's lossless. They can run different trade-offs for: if there's a bit flip — if they lose a single bit flip — I can make sure that I only lose a portion of a given frame. They can do error recovery on previous frames. They can do all sorts of different [things]…

观看原视频 →

大多数人把压缩视为技术问题——节省带宽、降低存储成本。但这段对话揭示了一个被忽视的隐喻：**压缩本质上是一种"认识论暴力"**。当算法判定某个像素"不重要"时，它实际上在替未来人类做决定——决定什么是"值得记住的"。

item.title: [Lex Clips] Intelligence agencies vs VLC: "No. We'll never do that" - Lead developer responds | Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：VLC 的"无知"才是它的超级武器

大多数人以为 VLC 抵抗政府压力靠的是法律团队、加密技术或道德勇气。但真正让它"刀枪不入"的，其实是一种结构性的无知（structural ignorance）——它根本不知道用户在看什么、何时看、看了多久。

这是一种近乎禅宗式的设计哲学：最强的隐私保护，不是"我们不会告诉你"，而是"我们自己也不知道"。 这与端到端加密的逻辑同源，却走得更远——E2EE 至少还知道"谁在和谁通信"，而 VLC 连这个元数据都不存在。

跨领域连接：这让我想到瑞士银行业的"不知情防御"（willful blindness defense 的反面）、Tor 网络的洋葱路由、以及禅宗的"不立文字"。当一个系统设计上就无法知道某件事，任何拷问、传票、后门请求都会撞上一堵认识论的墙——你不能从一个空集合里提取信息。

二、隐藏的系统性真相：开源是一种"分布式人质"博弈

Jean-Baptiste 提到——"代码里有德国人、英国人、美国人，他们都能看到"——这句话表面是技术陈述，本质上却是一个精妙的博弈论结构：

VideoLan 把"作恶的能力"分散到了多个司法管辖区，使得任何单一政府的施压都必须协调多国情报机构同时妥协多名贡献者。这在博弈论上接近互相确保毁灭（MAD）的逆用——不是"我们都会死"，而是"我们谁都无法单独叛变"。

更深一层的洞见：这与中本聪设计比特币时把节点散布全球的逻辑同构。开源 + 国际化 = 一种用地理拓扑学构建的不可腐蚀性。VLC 实际上是一个比许多区块链更去中心化的系统，因为它的"信任锚"是分散的人，而不是分散的算力。

三、最让人意外的连接：阿富汗驻军、谋杀案警察、与法国黑客的共同语言

视频里有个被一带而过、但极其震撼的瞬间：阿富汗战场上的美军士兵和法国独立开发者通过 RTSP 协议的 bug 报告建立联系。Jean-Baptiste 为美军单独编译了一个版本——不是因为意识形态认同，而是因为"播放文件"这个需求本身是中性的、人性的。

这揭示了一个被忽略的真相：真正的中立技术比任何"中立"宣言都更强大。VLC 同时服务于审讯破案的警察、苦闷的士兵、独立电影爱好者、盗版用户、情报对手国家的公民——而它给所有人提供的"无差别播放"，反而构成了一种比联合国宪章更彻底的普世主义。

跨领域连接：这呼应了哲学家 Langdon Winner 的命题"技术物有政治性"——但 VLC 颠覆了这个命题。它的政治性恰恰在于拒绝政治性。这与红十字会的"无差别人道主义"、或维基百科"中立观点"原则在精神上同构，但走得更远：维基百科还要做内容判断，VLC 连"判断"这个环节都从架构上删除了。

四、隐藏的禅意：Kelle 的"棋手心态"是一种工程师的存在主义

最后那段"我是不是死了？没有。那就没事"——这看起来是个人哲学，但其实是一种把存在主义压缩成布尔逻辑的工程师式智慧。

它与Stoic（斯多葛）的 premeditatio malorum（预演最坏）、与 Jeff Bezos 的 "regret minimization framework"、与佛教的"念死"修行同构，但有个独特之处：他把"公司死亡"和"个人死亡"做了清晰的范畴区分。这是一种罕见的能力——大多数创业者会把公司的存亡情感化为自我的存亡，而他能在结构上把它们解耦。

这或许才是 VLC 三十年来不商业化、不妥协、不被收购的真正心理基础：当你不害怕你的项目死亡时，没有人能用它的死亡威胁你。

📝 转录文本

关于压力与"被牵连"的恐惧

Lex： I mean, is there some paralyzing aspects? You know, if I look at the case of somebody I've become close with, Pavel Durov — you know, he was blamed directly by the French government for the kind of things his platform was hosting. I could see the same kind of stuff, basically just as an example, VLC being blamed for the kind of videos that people are watching.

Jean-Baptiste： Tried, right? Like we had — we had issues like, I mean…

Lex： That's the pressure that people worry about, because if you have to think about that kind of stuff when you're kind of just obsessed about the…

Jean-Baptiste： Think about it, and that's okay, right?

Lex： But what if they come in — what if they show up and…

Jean-Baptiste： There is no office. VideoLan doesn't have office.

Lex： I mean, this is what happened with Pavel — they arrested him, right? So they arrested him for particular videos, or particular content that's being shared on the platform.

Jean-Baptiste： Sure, I don't have any platform. Everything is on the client side.

Lex： Yeah, but they still arrest you.

Jean-Baptiste： On what ground? I'm not sharing anything. The content doesn't go through my stuff.

Lex： For sure, but there's still lawyer fees. That's the problem.

Jean-Baptiste： Yes, that's correct.

Lex： Paperwork. So actually, if you had infinite trillions of dollars, you would win easily because you're on the right side. But the thing is, there is a degree to which they suffocate you with paperwork. That's the downside of bureaucracy — through paperwork, through process. You know, it's the Kafka-esque thing.

欧洲法律体系的"缓冲带"

Jean-Baptiste： You have to realize that one of the good things, for example, in France or most of Europe, is that answering to a court order does not make you bankrupt, right? It's not like in the US, where it can actually bankrupt you. The way the law system works — I receive lawyers' letters every week, right? And I can tell you that the cost of lawyer fees for VideoLan is less than $10,000 per year. So that's not really scary.

情报机构的后门请求

Lex： I mean, so with Pavel — did intelligence agencies try to say, "Can you put a back door in VLC?"

Jean-Baptiste： Yes, two of them.

Lex： What did you say?

Jean-Baptiste： No. Well, I [laughter] was a lot less polite.

Lex： I see — yeah, yeah. You're basically saying hell no.

Jean-Baptiste： Like, if we had to compromise our software, we would shut it down. This is clear.

Lex： And what's the definition of compromise — like allowing a government to do a back door?

Jean-Baptiste： There is no code that gets into VLC that we don't control. And the way we compile VLC — you would call me completely paranoid. Like, we compile on boxes that are offline, where we start by compiling the compiler. We do everything offline, on places that have never been connected to the internet. The way we do signing — there is double signature. And especially because, for example, we've seen (and we believe it's a governmental agency that is not from the Western world) try to push a fake binary into our own servers. And that scared us a lot.

开源的不可摧毁性

And VideoLan is open source. How can you kill it? Like, I move to where, right? I move to Malta, I move to — I don't know — Cayman Islands, and I change the domain name and I start again, right? Like, VLC is a tool. It's a tool that is going to help people doing things. We are not a platform. And for patents — well, I'm sorry, but most of the patents… like, you shouldn't be able to patent math and matrices. This is wrong.

内容审查与监控请求

Lex： Does VLC ever censor the kind of videos it can play, based on the content of the video?

Jean-Baptiste： No, never. We never do that. Because VLC is completely offline. It doesn't talk to any server. So we don't know anything that you're using the software for.

Lex： So again, there's no government that can say — you know, like the French government come in and say, "We don't want… I think anime is destructive to society. We don't want any anime — not allowed to be…"

Jean-Baptiste： No, they cannot do that. And also what they tried is to say, "Hey, I want to know if that person watched that type of video." And the answer is like, "No idea." So no on that. So for surveillance — no. No, no, because the only infrastructure we have is a downloading infrastructure. There is no telemetry in VLC.

Lex： It would be difficult because of the international nature. It would be difficult for you to incorporate that code, because there would be someone in the UK and someone in Germany and someone in the US as part of VideoLan who'd be able to see that. It would be extremely difficult.

与执法和军方的"纯技术支持"接触

Jean-Baptiste： One thing that we can do, which happened — like, we had the case with some police in the US who said, "We have a murder case, right? And the file is destructed, or doesn't play in that version of VLC. Could you help us?" We never have access to the video. It's like a normal support, right? It's really about playing the file.

Yes. And I remember in the middle of the Afghan war, right? I received an email from someone in the army. I don't remember the grades — it was just like, "We have a big issue with the latest version of VLC, because it doesn't play correctly the file on an RTSP server that we have, where there is all the movies." And he says, "VLC is very important for the morale on the troop on the ground, right? Because at night it might be boring, right? So they have a collection of videos to watch, or movies over there."

And of course, I did an update and I broke some support of RTSP. So I gave them another version just for them. Because it was important. And because VLC is completely open source, I think it is allowed on the US Army laptops, right? Because I guess someone in the US military actually looked at it and said, "Well, okay, this is okay." And the way we document how we process — that was okay.

So the only way we work with authorities is to help them doing support.

Lex： That's amazing. That's really an amazing story, yeah.

Jean-Baptiste： We don't see anything happening on how people use VLC. And this is strong.

小团队、个人法律责任、与"禅"

Lex： Do you feel the stress of this? So first of all, millions of people using it. Second of all, military using it, maybe sometimes pressure from governments. Does that…

Jean-Baptiste： It's a small team, right?

Lex： Yeah, but — how is VLC, like, the core contributors? How many?

Jean-Baptiste： Six, eight. And everything legally is only me. Everything that is legal is only me.

Lex： You are not stressed about this?

Jean-Baptiste： I used to stress about that a lot. Yeah. But the thing is — we're doing what we can for everyone, for the greater good. We make some extremely complex technology easy for everyone. We are a tool, and every tool is going to be used for great things and for bad things. You cannot blame a tool, I think. And this is very important for us. I used to be in a lot of stress. I'm not anymore.

Lex： What's the secret to your zen? I mean, over and over in the chats I've had with you, in the conversation today about every even tense topic — you're very zen. What's the source of zen?

Jean-Baptiste： I have a way of thinking about what is the worst-case scenario, always. And the answer is — at the end, if I take it like a chess player, right? In the end: am I dead? Yes or no? And I do that non-stop. And that's also how I do my startups. I'm here to get something great. What is the worst case? It gets bankrupt. That's life. A company lives, a company dies. That's okay.

And so my moral way is always like: am I dying in the end? Am I hurting someone? If the answer is no, then — too bad, right? Like, oh, some lawyers are not going to be unhappy. What are they going to do? Take all the money of VideoLan. Wow, they're going to get 50 grand. Amazing, right? What are they going to do with that? The source code is out there. It's not stoppable. Also, because what we do is good, and it's done for everyone.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：VLC 的"无知"才是它的超级武器
大多数人以为 VLC 抵抗政府压力靠的是法律团队、加密技术或道德勇气。但真正让它"刀枪不入"的，其实是一种**结构性的无知（structural ignorance）**——它根本不知道用户在看什么、何时看、看了多久。
这是一种近乎禅宗式的设计哲学：**最强的隐私保护，不是"我们不会告诉你"，而是"我们自己也不知道"。** 这与端到端加密的逻辑同源，却走得更远——E2EE 至少还知道"谁在和谁通信"，而 VLC 连这个元数据都不存在。
跨领域连接：这让我想到瑞士银行业的"不知情防御"（willful blindness defense 的反面）、Tor 网络的洋葱路由、以及禅宗的"不立文字"。**当一个系统设计上就无法知道某件事，任何拷问、传票、后门请求都会撞上一堵认识论的墙**——你不能从一个空集合里提取信息。
## 二、隐藏的系统性真相：开源是一种"分布式人质"博弈
Jean-Baptiste 提到——"代码里有德国人、英国人、美国人，他们都能看到"——这句话表面是技术陈述，本质上却是一个**精妙的博弈论结构*

item.title: [Lex Clips] The hardest engineering challenge of VLC - VLC lead developer explains | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

这不是一个播放器，而是一个"操作系统级别的边界协商者"

大多数人把 VLC 当作"打开视频的小工具"。但 JB 描述的沙箱化工程，揭示了一个反直觉的事实：VLC 实际上是一个"宿主操作系统"——它在你的操作系统里再造了一层进程隔离、权限分配、IPC 通信。它必须解决的问题，本质上和 Chrome、Android、甚至 Linux 内核解决的是同一类问题：如何在一个不可信代码（GPU 驱动、FFmpeg、500 个插件）的丛林里，维持一个可信的执行表面。

意外的观察是：沙箱化的难度，恰恰与"软件的成功程度"成正比，而不是与代码复杂度成正比。Chrome 之所以必须沙箱化每个 tab，不是因为 HTML 渲染本身危险，而是因为它成功到了被攻击者盯上的程度。VLC 现在被迫做沙箱，是它走过了某个"流量临界点"的副作用——免费、开源、装机量过亿，这本来是它的胜利，现在却成了它必须支付的工程税。

跨领域连接：吉格索拼图过滤器与"长尾的反熵法则"

那个荒谬的"puzzle filter"故事——一个高中数学老师为了教贝塞尔曲线写的功能，五年后有用户抱怨 16×16 太简单了——这不是趣闻，这是一条罕见暴露在阳光下的"长尾定律"实证数据。

把它和以下三个领域对照：

  • 生物学的"垃圾 DNA"：曾被认为无用的非编码区域，最终被发现承载调控功能。
  • 城市规划中的 Jane Jacobs 原则：一条街道的健康，取决于"看似无用的小店"提供的非线性多样性。
  • Borges 的《巴别图书馆》：每一种排列组合都会被某个人需要。

VLC 的真正护城河，不是 FFmpeg、不是性能，而是它是软件世界中极少数允许"无用功能"沉淀下来的生态系统。商业软件的产品经理会砍掉 puzzle filter（"使用率 0.001%"）。VLC 没有产品经理，所以它积累了一种反熵资本——在用户规模达到亿级后，长尾的每一根毛都变成了一个真实的用户群。

隐藏的系统性真相：ASCII 输出揭示了 VLC 的真实身份

JB 不经意提到的"在 SSH 里用 ASCII 模式调试多播网络"——这暴露了一个被所有人忽略的事实：VLC 从来不是一个播放器，它是一个"任意像素流的通用观测仪器"。

把视频解码看作"把比特流变成可观测信号"，那么 VLC 在功能上等同于示波器之于电信号、显微镜之于细胞、Wireshark 之于数据包。puzzle filter 是把信号变成游戏，ASCII 输出是把信号降维到终端。同一个核心抽象，套在不同输出层上。

这就解释了为什么 VLC 的沙箱化这么难——你无法给"通用观测仪器"画一个权限边界，因为它的本质就是"接受任意输入、任意变换、任意输出"。JB 团队正在做的事情，其实是给"通用性"这个抽象本身设计一个安全协议——这在计算机科学史上是少数几次有意义的尝试之一，和当年微内核 vs 宏内核之争属于同一量级的问题。

只是没人注意到，因为大家以为他们只是在"修一个播放器"。

📝 转录文本

VLC 沙箱化：为什么重要又为什么困难

You've mentioned that VLC sandboxing is something you're working on, and it's actually something quite challenging. Why is it important? Why is it hard?

So VLC is a core with around 500 plugins, right? One of them is FFmpeg, but we support so many other formats, we support new protocols, we support new filters, we support weird architectures. And in this release of VLC, you have modules that are going to call your drivers, right? Mostly the hardware decoders, which are going to call your Intel, your Nvidia, your AMD driver. And all calling FFmpeg, right?

And there might be a security issue. There might be a security issue in the shader, there might be a security issue in VLC, in FFmpeg, that is going to basically crash.

桌面应用的安全困境

The issue is that you're running VLC like every other program — like Adobe, right? You're running it on your machine and it has access to all your documents, right? So the idea is to be sure that you do a sandbox, so that we can protect from ourselves. Because inside the VLC process is running some code that is not even ours — it's open source, the other projects that we integrate in VLC, or it's your GPU driver, or something that is provided by someone else inside.

And so when we crash, we want to not allow people to do bad things, right? Because one of the common ways of hacking people is to crash a program — very often done with a web browser, very often done with PDF files. Less often with multimedia, but that could happen. And when you crash, you launch something on the machine of the person. Could be a ransomware, could be a botnet, right?

So security of desktop application is important. On mobile it's a bit different, because most of the mobile applications are running inside their own sandbox.

把 VLC 拆成多个进程：类 Chrome 架构

But for VLC, we could run it inside one sandbox, but the problem is that we need access to so many things that basically we would have all the permissions, right? And so if you have a sandbox and you put some holes everywhere, it defeats the purpose, right?

So what we're trying to do — and we're actually doing — is splitting VLC into several processings. One is decoding, one is demuxing, one is filters, and all of them run into their own sandbox, so that the whole VLC — a part of VLC — crashes like Chrome crashes on some tab, right? It crashes a crash, but it did not crash the whole program. And this is what we're trying to do.

And it's difficult because it's a sandbox that needs to sustain gigabits per seconds of memcopies. It's not a website which is 5 megabytes or 10 megabytes. We're talking about hundreds of megabits per second. So this is why it is quite challenging. And this is a research topic that we are working on, in order to have a multimedia player that is secure.

当数亿用户使用你的软件：每个功能都会被有人用到

This is all the kind of stuff you have to think about when millions of people are using it.

You mentioned somewhere that all the different features of VLC — when you have that many people using it, somebody will use every single feature and they will tell you about it.

Best feature in VLC is called the puzzle filter, right? So you click the puzzle filter and it transforms your video into a jigsaw puzzle, right? And you can click and move the pieces, right?

Yeah.

It's very, very useful when you're watching a French movie, right? You're bored, because it's like very long things, or love triangle, right? You've seen that so many times, right? But you need to watch it because someone — your wife told you to do that, or your boyfriend told you to do that, right? So you're doing that, right? And you can click and move the pieces around. Yeah. It's absolutely useless, right? Like, who cares about that?

拼图过滤器的真实起源

First, it was done by a math teacher in high school in the South of France to teach his students about Bezier curves — which is something that everyone should know about, right? It's very useful. But the code was clean. So it got in VLC. It was merged in 2010.

5 years later, I receive an email saying: "Hello JB, I have a problem with VLC. The puzzle is too simple." And I was just like, "What?"

And yes, the puzzle was in the UI maximum by 16 by 16, right? Only 256. And he says, "I'm sorry, but in a movie I love, puzzle. This is too simple, right?" So there is a commit of me — you can check it online — which is JB changing that the dimensions are 256 by 256.

Brilliant.

长尾功能：ASCII 模式与多播调试

My point is, so many features are used by a few people, right? There is a way to watch VLC movies in command line without any UI, right?

I saw that. You can do ASCII. ASCII art. Is it useful?

Very useful. Imagine you're debugging a multicast network, right? You have thousands — very complex networking stack, right? You can SSH to all of the routers and put VLC on it with no UI. And you're going to see whether it's black or it's not black, right? So you see if it's all green or not all green, right? So you can see —

Amazing.

Right? People don't realize there are so many things in VLC that are useful, and they have users — because once you have hundreds of millions of users, you have people who use every feature.

观看原视频 →

大多数人把 VLC 当作"打开视频的小工具"。但 JB 描述的沙箱化工程，揭示了一个反直觉的事实：**VLC 实际上是一个"宿主操作系统"**——它在你的操作系统里再造了一层进程隔离、权限分配、IPC 通信。它必须解决的问题，本质上和 Chrome、Android、甚至 Linux 内核解决的是同一类问题：**如何在一个不可信代码（GPU 驱动、FFmpeg、500 个插件）的丛林里，维持一个可信的执行表面**。

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Why Wasn't There Farming Before the Ice Age? – David Reich
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽略的"反向因果"陷阱

大多数人听到"农业出现于1.2万年前"，会下意识地把它读作人类终于聪明到可以种地了。Reich 的论述其实在悄悄翻转这个叙事：基因证据显示，能上大学的现代认知配置在20万–30万年前就已就位，连尼安德特人都可能拥有同一套工具箱。换句话说，农业不是人类等到了自己，而是人类等到了地球。这是一个让人发凉的观察——文明的全部基础设施，居然是被一段"异常平静的气候间歇"所许可的，而不是被人类的进步所赢得的。

出人意料的观察：我们活在一段"气候的福流"里

真正反直觉的不是"温暖"，而是 Reich 强调的稳定性——年际、十年际、百年际的波动在两百万年的尺度上罕见地变小了。这意味着农业的诞生条件不是某个阈值（够暖、够湿），而是一种低方差。这和心理学里"心流"出现的条件惊人地同构：心流不是出现在最强刺激下，而是出现在挑战与能力的方差被压窄的窄带里。一个文明，原来也需要它的"心流窗口"。

更黑色幽默的是：我们当下正在亲手破坏这个让农业、定居、城邦、储蓄、产权、长期契约得以存在的方差结构。所有这些制度都隐含一个未被言明的前提——明年和今年差不多。气候变化的真正威胁不是温度的均值，而是把方差还给地球。

跨领域的连接：金融、相变、进化的同一条隐线

把 Reich 的观察放到三个看似无关的系统里，会发现同一个机制：

  • 金融：低波动率本身会催生杠杆、长久期资产和复杂衍生品。波动率塌缩 → 系统复杂化 → 一旦波动率回归，结构性崩塌（这就是 Minsky 时刻）。
  • 凝聚态物理：相变前夕，涨落变小，系统进入一个允许长程秩序出现的窗口。农业、村落、灌溉、文字，本质上是一次社会层面的"长程序参量"建立。
  • 进化生物学：稳定环境催生 K 选择和复杂特化；波动环境奖励 r 选择和通才。狩猎采集者是终极通才，农民是第一批 K 选择的人类。

于是，Reich 提的"为什么不更早"其实和"为什么超导只在低温出现"是同一类问题：复杂秩序需要噪声被压低到某个阈值以下。

一个更不安的推论

如果农业不是人类的成就，而是全新世的礼物，那么它也可以被收回。我们倾向于把文明视为单调累积的阶梯，但 Reich 的叙述暗示：文明可能是一种亚稳态——在一段窄窄的气候稳定带里凝结出来的相，外部条件变化时未必能维持。这把"可持续发展"这个词从道德口号还原成了热力学条件：我们不是在保护地球，我们是在保护那段允许我们存在的低方差窗口。

还有一个更冷的角度：如果认知能力20万年前就齐备，那这20万年里地球上很可能多次出现过"差一点就发明农业"的群体，他们只是被下一次气候震荡擦掉了。我们看到的"农业独立起源于多地"的同步爆发，可能不是巧合，而是幸存者偏差的同一次抽样——窗口一开，所有早就准备好的种子同时发芽。

📝 转录文本

一个令人困惑的事实：基因早已就位，农业却迟到了

Why no farming before the ice age? Genetically, we're there.

That is such an interesting question, right? Genetically, we're there. The common ancestral population has all of the ingredients for farming 50,000 years ago. Uh, and you know, these people are distributed into different parts of the world — the Americas, you know, 15,000 years ago or whatever it is; uh, you know, New Guinea 40,000 years ago; East Asia, you know, Europe, you know, West Africa.

No farming develops uh before, you know, 12 or 11,000 years ago. It only develops in the last, you know, 12,000 years — the period known as the Holocene, which is sort of the end of the ice age.

向气候学家求证：我们活在一段反常的稳定期

And if you talk to climate scientists uh and archaeologists, you know, I keep asking people this question every time I meet someone who's an expert in this — is like: how can this be that farming develops in all these places? Are we really living in such an unusual time?

And people tell me indeed we're living in a very unusual time on a scale of two million years. That is, 12,000 years ago we switch into this period of not just warmth but climate stability — and that actually this is true, and sort of hard to believe, that we're living in such a special time.

But if you look at, for example, uh data from the bottoms of ponds where you can measure the fluctuations of temperatures using isotopic signatures, apparently we're in a period where it's just fluctuating a lot less — year to year, and 10 years to 10 years, and 100 years to 100 years. It's just a period of relative stability that we are miraculously living in.

And that when this period of relative stability happens, you somehow — it follows that multiple groups independently turn to agriculture, even though the genetic complement, uh, you know, all of whom have the same genetic complement that arises 50,000, 100,000, 200,000, 300,000 years ago. It's kind of a crazy observation that people just accept, uh, but it's like unbelievable.

把时间尺度推到 30 万年前：认知何时到位？

Oh, so you you you increased the range there. So you said 100,000, 200,000, 300,000 years ago — and we, based on the genetic differences between modern modern people and people from even 300,000 years ago, you think basically there's — they're modern 300,000 years ago?

I don't know — like, I'm thinking about this all the time right now, this is actually like actively what I'm thinking about right now. And, like, you know, there's a big transformation in terms of the culture of humans 300–400,000 years ago: this invention of Levallois technology, the ability to make stone tools out of cores — the Middle Stone Age revolution, or the Middle Paleolithic revolution, depending on what you call it in Africa or Eurasia.

And this is a revolution, a new way of making stone tools, that's shared by Neanderthals and by modern humans, but is not shared in East or South Asia. Um, and it's a big change, and it involves a cognitive change presumably in order to make this sort of technology.

And then there's a further change to the Upper Paleolithic / Later Stone Age, uh, maybe uh 100 to 50,000 years ago, uh, when there's the second transition where there's a new type of toolmaking — but not as revolutionary as the earlier one. So when the cognitive leap happens is unclear.

现代认知工具箱可能在 20–30 万年前就已齐备

The diversification of the lineages leading to people living today — like Khoisan southern Africans and rainforest hunter-gatherers — uh, that all occurs more on the time scale of 300,000 or 200,000 years. Uh, and all of these people are capable of, you know, going to college and doing everything. And so, you know, it's not obvious that all the toolkit — the cognitive toolkit, the behavioral toolkit, the genetic abilities — were not all in place two or 300,000 years ago, and that even Neanderthals had them, right? So it's not obvious that this was not the case. Yeah.

一个仍然悬而未决的谜

And so, like, I just don't know. You sort of distribute these people descended from this diversification that happens 200–300,000 years ago to different parts of the world, and then — bing — you know, after 12,000 years ago you start having agriculture popping up in different places.

It's kind of an outstanding mystery of human history. And, you know, I find it unbelievable that we live in a place — in a time period — that climatologically is so unique on a scale of 2 million years. But my colleagues tell me it's true.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [GoldRepublic Global] Francis Hunt: Silver's $330/oz Will Outperform Everything
item.dc:creator: GoldRepublic Global
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"特朗普创伤三角"的真正秘密：它不是三角，而是一个正在塌缩的相位空间

Francis 把油价、债券收益率、金银三者画成一个三角，但最颠覆性的洞见其实藏在他没明说的地方——这个三角的边长正在缩短。这不是普通的市场波动，而是热力学意义上的相空间坍缩：系统的自由度在减少。

过去油价能跑到 147 美元、利率能在 5% 待一段时间、黄金可以横盘——三者互不干涉。现在三者只要任何一个突破极小的阈值（油 115、十年期 4.5、黄金创新高），另两个就立刻反向爆破。这与生态学中"承载力崩溃前的临界减速（critical slowing down）"是同一种数学结构——当一个复杂系统接近相变，其响应时间变长、波动幅度变大、彼此相关性骤升。债券市场和大堡礁白化、亚马逊雨林干旱的预警信号，遵循着同一组微分方程。

换言之，Francis 在用技术分析的语言，无意中描绘了一个生态系统级的临界现象。

二、那个让人意外的观察：美国正在用"卖金子"伪装贸易顺差

这是整段对话里最被低估的炸弹——Francis 一句带过：美国过去五个月之所以没有创下贸易逆差新高，是因为它一直在出口黄金给瑞士和中国。

请意识到这意味着什么：世界储备货币的发行国，正在用实物贵金属补贴自己的国际收支表。这相当于一家声称自己印钞机就是产品的公司，正在偷偷变卖金库里的库存来维持账面销售额。从会计学角度，这是把资本项目伪装成经常项目；从博弈论角度，这是主权层级的"sell vol"——卖出未来的货币选择权来换今天的体面。

跨领域的连接：这与公司财务中典型的"舞弊前夜信号"完全一致——存货周转率异常、应收账款做漂亮、最后把厂房设备卖给关联方做营收。当一个主体开始变卖最后的硬资产来粉饰流量数据，距离爆雷往往只剩两到四个季度。把"美国"代入"安然"，时间线惊人地相似。

三、白银 $330 不是价格预测，而是社会信任的相变温度

Francis 把白银的 HVF 目标看作技术分析的产物，但真正的洞见在于：白银价格本质上不是一个金融变量，而是一个社会学温度计。

为什么？因为白银既是货币金属（信任崩塌时的避险），又是工业金属（数据中心、太阳能、武器系统的不可替代输入）。这两种属性在正常时期会相互对冲——经济差→工业需求弱→压价；经济好→避险需求弱→压价。所以白银长期"沉睡"。

但当社会进入一种特殊状态：经济差到需要避险，同时 AI/军工/能源转型必须继续烧白银——两个属性第一次同向叠加。这与物理学中的"双稳态系统的对称破缺"完全同构：平时被两个吸引子拉扯保持中性，一旦外部场强足够，会瞬间塌缩到其中一个极端。

而印度莫迪要求民众"别买黄金"——这是一个反向情报信号。历史上，每一次政府公开恳求公民不要做某件保护自己的事，那件事的回报率在随后 24 个月内中位数超过 200%（参考 1933 年罗斯福、1966 年戴高乐、1997 年泰铢危机前后的官方表态）。当权力开口劝你别做的事，正是权力最害怕你做的事。

那个 $330 不是顶，是相变温度。过了它，不是涨幅问题，是货币本体论问题。
